notes.title: CLAUDE
notes.content:
# Malaysia Property Wiki — Schema & Rules (CLAUDE.md)

> This file is the rulebook for this wiki. Read it before every session. It tells you how the wiki is structured, what conventions to follow, and how to run each workflow. You (the LLM) own the wiki. Wai Win Han owns the sources and the questions.

---

## 1. Purpose

This wiki tracks and synthesises knowledge about **Malaysian real estate** across all asset classes (residential, commercial, industrial, land) and all major regions (Klang Valley, Penang, Johor/JB, and others). It serves two purposes simultaneously:

1. **Investment Research** — understanding market trends, evaluating projects, identifying opportunities
2. **Portfolio Management** — tracking owned/acquired properties, monitoring yield, valuations, tenants, loans

The LLM writes and maintains the wiki. The human curates sources and asks questions.

---

## 2. Directory Structure

```
malaysia-property/
├── CLAUDE.md                  ← This file. The schema. Read it first.
├── index.md                   ← Master catalog of all wiki pages
├── log.md                     ← Append-only audit trail of all actions
├── overview.md                ← High-level Malaysia property market synthesis
│
├── raw-sources/               ← DROP ARTICLES/REPORTS/DATA HERE (immutable, LLM reads not writes)
│
├── sources/                   ← One summary page per ingested raw source
│
├── entities/
│   ├── developers/            ← One page per developer (Sime Darby, SP Setia, IJM, etc.)
│   ├── areas/                 ← One page per region/submarket (KL, PJ, JB, Georgetown, etc.)
│   └── projects/              ← One page per named property development project
│
├── concepts/                  ← Key terms, policies, mechanisms (HOC, strata, bumiputera lot, etc.)
│
├── market-analysis/           ← Synthesis pages: price trends, yield analysis, comparisons
│
└── construction-costs/        ← Construction cost tracking (BOQ, unit rates, project totals)
    ├── projects/              ← One page per property project (BOQ summary + cost breakdown)
    └── rates/                 ← One page per cost category (unit rate benchmarks across projects)

Note: raw BOQ files, rate schedules, quotations, and cost summaries go in:
    raw-sources/construction cost/   ← DROP CONSTRUCTION COST FILES HERE
```

---

## 3. Page Types & Templates

### 3.1 Source Summary Page (`sources/`)
Created when a raw source is ingested. Filename: `sources/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md`

```yaml
---
type: source
title: ""
date_ingested: YYYY-MM-DD
source_type: article | report | data | listing | news
url: ""
publisher: ""
region: []         # e.g. [klang-valley, johor]
asset_class: []    # residential | commercial | industrial | land
tags: []
key_entities: []   # developers, areas, or concepts mentioned
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Summary` — 3–5 sentence synthesis
- `## Key Data Points` — specific numbers, prices, yields, dates worth preserving
- `## Notable Quotes` — verbatim quotes that matter
- `## Updates To` — list of wiki pages updated as a result of this ingest
- `## Open Questions` — things this source raised but didn't answer

---

### 3.2 Area Entity Page (`entities/areas/`)
One page per geographic submarket. Filename: `entities/areas/kebab-case.md`

```yaml
---
type: area
name: ""
state: ""          # Selangor | Kuala Lumpur | Penang | Johor | etc.
region: ""         # klang-valley | penang | johor | east-malaysia
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
source_count: 0
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Overview` — character, infrastructure, demographics
- `## Residential Market` — price range (PSF / per unit), popular project types, demand drivers
- `## Commercial Market` — office/retail/shop lot rates if applicable
- `## Industrial Market` — factory/warehouse rates if applicable
- `## Key Developments` — notable current or upcoming projects (link to developer pages)
- `## Yield Snapshot` — gross rental yield range for each asset class
- `## Risks & Watch Points` — oversupply, infrastructure gaps, policy risks
- `## Sources` — wikilinks to source pages that informed this page

---

### 3.3 Developer Entity Page (`entities/developers/`)
One page per developer. Filename: `entities/developers/kebab-case.md`

```yaml
---
type: developer
name: ""
listed: true | false
bursa_code: ""     # if listed
tier: tier-1 | tier-2 | tier-3 | niche
regions: []
asset_classes: []
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Overview` — company background, scale, reputation
- `## Active Projects` — current launches with area, type, price range, completion date
- `## Completed Track Record` — past projects, on-time delivery, defect history
- `## Financial Health` — if listed: revenue, net profit, gearing, landbank
- `## Reputation Notes` — build quality, after-sales, complaints, awards
- `## Sources`

---

### 3.4 Concept Page (`concepts/`)
One page per term/mechanism. Filename: `concepts/kebab-case.md`

```yaml
---
type: concept
name: ""
category: policy | legal | financial | market | tax
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Definition` — plain-language explanation
- `## How It Works` — mechanics
- `## Malaysia-Specific Context` — nuances, governing law, authority
- `## Investor Implications` — what it means for buying, selling, holding
- `## Related Concepts` — wikilinks

---

### 3.5 Market Analysis Page (`market-analysis/`)
Synthesis pages generated in response to queries. Filename: `market-analysis/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md`

```yaml
---
type: analysis
title: ""
date: YYYY-MM-DD
regions: []
asset_classes: []
question: ""       # The question that prompted this analysis
tags: []
---
```

**Body:** Free-form, but always end with:
- `## Confidence Notes` — what data is solid vs. estimated/extrapolated
- `## Sources Used` — wikilinks

---

### 3.6 Project Cost Page (`construction-costs/projects/`)
One page per property project. Created when BOQ or cost data is uploaded. Filename: `construction-costs/projects/kebab-case-project-name.md`

```yaml
---
type: construction-cost-project
project_name: ""
developer: ""          # wikilink to entities/developers/ page
area: ""               # wikilink to entities/areas/ page
asset_class: []        # residential | commercial | industrial | mixed
contract_type: ""      # lump-sum | remeasurement | design-and-build | cost-plus
gfa_sqft: 0            # Gross Floor Area in sq ft
gfa_sqm: 0             # Gross Floor Area in sq m
contract_sum_myr: 0    # Total contract value in MYR
cost_psf_gfa_myr: 0    # Construction cost per sq ft (GFA basis)
cost_psm_gfa_myr: 0    # Construction cost per sq m (GFA basis)
contract_date: YYYY-MM-DD
expected_completion: YYYY-MM-DD
main_contractor: ""
data_source: ""        # BOQ | quotation | tender | final-account | estimate
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Project Overview` — brief description, location, total units/floors, contract type, key dates
- `## Cost Summary` — table breaking down total cost by trade/element (see standard categories in §6)
- `## Cost Benchmarks` — cost PSF vs. comparable projects; notes on why cost is above/below market
- `## BOQ Highlights` — notable quantities (e.g. total concrete m³, rebar tonnes, curtain wall m²)
- `## Key Unit Rates` — the most important rates extracted from this project's BOQ/schedule; link to `[[construction-costs/rates/...]]` pages
- `## Contractor & Procurement Notes` — tendering method, nominated sub-contractors, notable specs
- `## Sources` — wikilinks to raw source files or source summary pages

---

### 3.7 Unit Rate Reference Page (`construction-costs/rates/`)
One page per cost category. Aggregates benchmark rates across multiple projects. Filename: `construction-costs/rates/kebab-case-category.md`

```yaml
---
type: construction-rate-reference
category: ""           # e.g. piling | structural | mep | finishes | external-works
subcategories: []      # e.g. [bored-pile, precast-pile, micropile]
unit: ""               # primary unit for this category (m³ | m² | m | tonne | item | lot)
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
source_count: 0        # number of projects contributing data
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Category Overview` — what this category covers; where it sits in a typical Malaysian QS bill
- `## Rate Table` — the core benchmark table (see format below)
- `## Notes & Assumptions` — spec assumptions, regional adjustments, escalation notes
- `## Sources` — wikilinks to project pages that contributed rates

**Rate Table format:**

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Low (MYR) | Mid (MYR) | High (MYR) | Date | Project |
|------|-------------|------|-----------|-----------|------------|------|---------|
| Bored pile Ø600 | C35 concrete, 20m depth | m | 350 | 420 | 500 | 2025-Q3 | [[project-name]] |

- Low/Mid/High reflect the range across ingested projects — not a single quote
- Always record the year/quarter so rates can be escalated forward
- Flag outliers (very high spec, abnormal site conditions, distressed contract) with a ⚠️ note

---

### 3.8 Project Entity Page (`entities/projects/`)
One page per named property development project. Captures launch data, unit mix, sales status, and key features for competitor analysis and investment research. Filename: `entities/projects/kebab-case-project-name.md`

```yaml
---
type: project
project_name: ""
developer: ""               # wikilink to entities/developers/ page
area: ""                    # wikilink to entities/areas/ page
region: ""                  # klang-valley | penang | johor | east-malaysia
asset_class: []             # residential | commercial | industrial | land | mixed
tenure: ""                  # freehold | leasehold
title_type: ""              # residential | commercial (strata title type)
total_units: 0
storeys: ""                 # e.g. "49 (2 blk)" or "10"
unit_sizes_sf: ""           # range e.g. "1,324–2,863"
launch_psf_myr: ""          # range or single value
launch_price_range_myr: ""  # e.g. "MYR 1.25M–3.5M"
take_up_pct: ""             # e.g. "98%" or "95% (as at 2025-Q3)"
status: ""                  # pre-launch | launched | under-construction | completed | cancelled
launch_date: ""             # YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-QN or YYYY
expected_completion: ""     # YYYY or YYYY-MM
maintenance_fee_psf_myr: "" # e.g. "0.39"
tags: []
last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
source_count: 0
---
```

**Body sections:**
- `## Overview` — project description, positioning, concept, land size/plot ratio if known
- `## Unit Mix` — table of unit types, sizes, counts, prices, bedrooms/baths/parking
- `## Sales Status` — take-up rate, SPA signed, launch timeline, absorption notes
- `## Key Features` — design spec, finishes, amenities, sustainability, brand/concept
- `## Competitive Positioning` — how it compares to peers by PSF, size, take-up
- `## Risks & Watch Points` — construction, sales, planning, developer, market risks
- `## Sources` — wikilinks to source pages that informed this page

---

## 4. Workflows

### 4.1 INGEST (adding a new source)
When the human drops a file into `raw-sources/` or pastes a URL/article:

1. Read the source fully
2. Discuss key takeaways with the human if needed
3. Create a source summary page in `sources/`
4. Update relevant `entities/areas/` pages (prices, projects, risks)
5. Update relevant `entities/developers/` pages
6. Update relevant `concepts/` pages if the source clarifies/changes any
7. Update `overview.md` if it affects the macro narrative
8. Append an entry to `log.md` (format below)
9. Update `index.md` with the new source page (and any new entity/concept pages)

One source may touch 5–15 pages. That is normal and expected.

### 4.2 QUERY (answering a question)
When the human asks a question:

1. Read `index.md` first to find relevant pages
2. Read the relevant pages
3. Synthesise an answer with citations (wikilinks to source pages)
4. If the answer is valuable and reusable, save it as a `market-analysis/` page and update `index.md`
5. Append a query entry to `log.md`

### 4.4 INGEST CONSTRUCTION DATA (adding BOQ / cost data)
Raw files land in `raw-sources/construction cost/`. When the human uploads a BOQ, rate schedule, quotation, or cost summary:

1. Read the uploaded file(s) from `raw-sources/construction cost/` fully
2. Create or update the project cost page in `construction-costs/projects/`
3. Extract unit rates from the data and update the relevant rate reference pages in `construction-costs/rates/` — create a new rate page if the category does not yet exist
4. Flag any rates that are notably above or below existing benchmarks (>20% variance) — note spec differences that may explain the gap
5. Cross-link: add a wikilink from the project page to each rate page updated, and back
6. Update relevant `entities/developers/` page if the project data reveals construction cost info
7. Append an entry to `log.md`
8. Update `index.md` with the new project page (and any new rate pages)

One BOQ upload may touch 1 project page + 8–12 rate pages. That is normal.

### 4.3 LINT (health check)
Periodically, run a wiki health check:

- Flag contradictions between pages (e.g. two different PSF ranges for the same area)
- Flag stale pages where newer sources have superseded claims (check dates)
- Find orphan pages (no inbound wikilinks)
- Find concepts or areas mentioned in other pages but lacking their own page
- Find missing cross-references
- Suggest data gaps that a web search could fill
- Report findings in the chat and add a lint entry to `log.md`

---

## 5. Logging Convention

Every action appends to `log.md`. Use this format so entries are grep-parseable:

```
## [YYYY-MM-DD] ACTION | Title/Description

- Key detail 1
- Key detail 2
- Pages updated: [[page1]], [[page2]]
```

Where ACTION is one of: `ingest` | `query` | `lint` | `manual-edit`

---

## 6. Malaysia Property Conventions

### Pricing
- Always record residential prices as **MYR per sq ft (PSF)** for built-up area
- Record land prices as **MYR per sq ft** for land area
- Record total price in MYR (not RM shorthand in data fields, spell out MYR)
- For commercial: record NLA (net lettable area) PSF and GFA PSF separately where known

### Yield Calculations
- **Gross yield** = (Annual rental income / Purchase price) × 100
- **Net yield** = ((Annual rental − maintenance fees − assessment − quit rent − insurance) / Purchase price) × 100
- Always note whether a yield figure is gross or net

### Regions Taxonomy
Use these consistent tags:
- `klang-valley` → KL, Selangor, Putrajaya, Cyberjaya
- `penang` → Penang Island + Seberang Perai
- `johor` → JB, Iskandar Malaysia, Kota Tinggi, etc.
- `east-malaysia` → Sabah, Sarawak
- `other-states` → everything else

### Asset Class Tags
- `residential` → condo, serviced apartment, SOHO, terraced, semi-D, bungalow
- `commercial` → retail lot, shop office, office suite, SOHO classified commercial
- `industrial` → factory, warehouse, semi-D factory, detached factory
- `land` → development land, agricultural land, industrial land

### Construction Costs

**Units & Measurement**
- Record all quantities in SI units: m³ (concrete, earthworks), m² (finishes, formwork, waterproofing), m (piling, piping, cable), tonne (rebar, structural steel), item/lot (for lump-sum elements)
- Record unit rates in **MYR per unit**
- Record total contract sum and element totals in **MYR**
- Always state the GFA (Gross Floor Area) basis when expressing cost PSF or PSM — never use NFA/NLA for construction cost benchmarks

**Cost PSF / PSM Benchmarks**
- Express construction cost as both **MYR PSF (GFA)** and **MYR PSM (GFA)** for cross-comparability
- Typical Malaysia residential construction cost ranges (2024–2025 indicative):
  - Low-cost / affordable: MYR 80–120 PSF
  - Mass market landed: MYR 120–200 PSF
  - Mass market high-rise: MYR 150–220 PSF
  - Mid-range high-rise: MYR 220–350 PSF
  - Luxury high-rise: MYR 350–600 PSF
  - Ultra-luxury / super-prime: MYR 600–900+ PSF
- These are construction cost only — exclude land, professional fees, infrastructure, sales & marketing, financing

**Data Source Classification**
Always tag cost data with one of:
- `tender` — pre-award tender price
- `contract-award` — awarded contract sum
- `final-account` — post-completion settled amount (most accurate)
- `quotation` — contractor or supplier quote (not necessarily awarded)
- `estimate` — QS or developer internal estimate

**Standard Cost Categories (Malaysia QS Practice)**
Use these consistent category names for rate pages and cost breakdowns:

| Category slug         | Covers                                                                     |
| --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `piling-foundation`   | Piling (bored, precast, micropile), pile caps, ground beams, raft slab     |
| `structural`          | RC columns, beams, slabs, PT slabs, shear walls, structural steel          |
| `brickwork-blockwork` | Brickwork, blockwork, lightweight blocks                                   |
| `roofing`             | Roof structure, deck, membrane, cladding, gutters                          |
| `external-finishes`   | Façade, curtain wall, cladding, external plaster & paint                   |
| `internal-finishes`   | Internal plaster, paint, ceiling, partition walls                          |
| `floor-finishes`      | Tiles, timber, carpet, epoxy, screeding                                    |
| `doors-windows`       | Doors, windows, glazing, ironmongery                                       |
| `waterproofing`       | Wet areas, rooftop, basement, external                                     |
| `plumbing-sanitary`   | Water supply, sanitary, drainage, sewerage                                 |
| `acmv`                | Air conditioning, mechanical ventilation, pressurisation                   |
| `electrical`          | HV/LV switchgear, wiring, lighting, ELV systems (CCTV, access control, PA) |
| `fire-protection`     | Sprinkler, hose reel, fire alarm, suppression                              |
| `lifts-escalators`    | Passenger lifts, service lifts, escalators, travellators                   |
| `bas-bms`             | Building Automation System, BMS, IBMS                                      |
| `external-works`      | Roads, drains, landscape, hardscape, car park, perimeter wall              |
| `preliminaries`       | Site setup, supervision, hoarding, tower crane, temporary works, insurance |
| `contingencies`       | Design contingency, site contingency                                       |

### Key Policy Concepts to Always Track
- Bumiputera quota / release status
- Foreign ownership threshold (> MYR 1M for residential in most states)
- RPGT (Real Property Gains Tax) — holding period matters
- HOC (Home Ownership Campaign) — active or not
- State-level specific restrictions (e.g. Johor foreigners, Penang heritage zones)

---

## 7. Obsidian Usage Tips

- Use `[[wikilinks]]` for all cross-references between pages — this builds the graph
- Add `#tags` consistently so Obsidian tag search works
- Use YAML frontmatter — Dataview can query it for dynamic tables
- The `raw-sources/` folder is the inbox; everything else is maintained by the LLM
- Run Graph View to spot orphan nodes and hub pages visually

---

## 8. Starter Commands

Tell the LLM any of these to trigger the right workflow:

- `"Ingest: [paste article or drop filename]"` → triggers INGEST workflow
- `"Query: [your question]"` → triggers QUERY workflow
- `"Lint the wiki"` → triggers LINT workflow
- `"Overview update"` → LLM re-reads all area pages and refreshes overview.md
- `"Ingest cost: [drop BOQ / rate schedule / quotation filename]"` → triggers INGEST CONSTRUCTION DATA workflow (files should be in `raw-sources/construction cost/`)

---

*This schema was initialised on 2026-04-28. Co-evolve it with the LLM as the wiki grows.*

notes.title: Bumiputera Quota
notes.content:
# Bumiputera Quota

## Definition

A government-mandated policy requiring property developers to set aside a percentage of units in each development for purchase by Bumiputera (indigenous Malay and East Malaysian) buyers at a discounted price (typically 5–15% below market price).

## How It Works

- Developer allocates a fixed % of units as "Bumi lots" before public launch
- Bumi lots are marketed only to Bumiputera buyers for a set period (usually 6–12 months)
- If unsold after the holding period, developer may apply to the relevant State Authority (SUK/EXCO) for a **Bumi lot release** — allowing non-Bumi buyers to purchase
- Discount percentage and quota % vary by state and project type

## Malaysia-Specific Context

- **Selangor:** typically 30% Bumi quota; State EXCO approval needed for release
- **KL/Federal Territory:** 30% requirement but enforcement has been more flexible
- **Johor:** varies; Iskandar Malaysia zones may have different rules
- **Penang:** state-specific; PDC guidelines apply
- Policy is state-regulated, not federal — each state sets its own rules
- Low-cost (PPR) and affordable housing have separate, often higher quotas

## Investor Implications

- Released Bumi lots can sometimes be bought by non-Bumi at a discount vs. non-Bumi units (since the lot was priced lower)
- Non-Bumi investors should check whether unsold Bumi lots have been released before assuming they're paying market price
- For Bumi investors: the discount is a structural advantage; buying Bumi lots in high-demand projects is valuable
- Quota restrictions can affect project velocity — if Bumi buyers don't take up lots, developer's cashflow is affected

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/foreign-ownership-rules]] | [[concepts/hoc]] | [[concepts/spa-process]]

notes.title: Data Centre Real Estate
notes.content:
# Data Centre Real Estate

## Definition

A **data centre** is a facility housing computing infrastructure (servers, networking, storage) and the supporting systems (power, cooling, security) that keep that infrastructure running continuously. In real estate terms, data centres are classified as **specialised industrial assets** — they require industrial-zoned land but have fundamentally different valuation drivers from conventional factories or warehouses.

The key unit of measurement in data centre real estate is **megawatts (MW)** of IT load capacity (the power the computing equipment consumes), not square footage.

---

## How It Works

### The Asset Stack

Data centres sit on a spectrum from raw land to fully operational facilities:

| Layer | What's Sold/Leased | Who Buys |
|-------|-------------------|----------|
| **Land** | Industrial-zoned land near power substations | Developers, hyperscalers |
| **Shell & core** | Building structure + power + cooling infrastructure | Colocation operators |
| **Colocation (colo)** | Rack space / cage / suite within a DC | Enterprises, cloud operators |
| **Cloud** | Virtual compute (AWS, Azure, Google) | End users |

Malaysian property investors most commonly encounter this market at the **land** and **shell & core** levels.

### Revenue Model

Data centres generate revenue primarily through **power contracts** — tenants pay for power capacity (measured in MW or kW) rather than floor space. Lease structures:

- **Wholesale DC**: Large blocks (1–10+ MW) leased to a single tenant (hyperscaler or large enterprise). Long leases (10–15 years), lower $/kW but stable income.
- **Retail colo**: Small allocations (single racks to cage rooms). Shorter leases, higher $/kW, more tenants.
- **Build-to-suit**: Developer builds to hyperscaler specification; hyperscaler signs long pre-commitment lease before completion.

### Key Technical Metrics

| Metric | Definition | What It Tells You |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **IT Load (MW)** | Power capacity for computing equipment | Size of the data centre |
| **PUE** (Power Usage Effectiveness) | Total facility power ÷ IT load | Efficiency (1.0 = perfect; modern DCs target 1.2–1.4) |
| **Tier Rating** (Uptime Institute) | Tier I–IV availability certification | Tier III (99.982%) or IV (99.995%) are investment-grade |
| **Uptime / availability** | % of time facility is operational | Mission-critical workloads demand 99.99%+ |
| **Power density (kW/rack)** | Power per server rack | AI/GPU workloads demand 40–100+ kW/rack (vs 5–10 kW traditional) |

---

## Malaysia-Specific Context

### Why Malaysia Emerged as a Data Centre Hub

1. **Land cost advantage** over Singapore: Singapore land for DC is severely constrained and expensive; Johor and Cyberjaya offer large, cheaper industrial parcels within latency tolerance (~20ms round-trip JB–Singapore)
2. **Stable political environment and English-language legal system**: Confidence for long-term infrastructure commitments
3. **Power tariff**: TNB (Tenaga Nasional) commercial rates historically competitive vs. Singapore
4. **Government incentives**: MSC status, data centre incentives under MIDA, and digital economy grants under 12th Malaysia Plan
5. **Fibre connectivity**: Good submarine cable access and growing cross-border connectivity (Singapore–JB fibre corridors)

### Two-Hub Geography

Malaysia's data centre market is geographically concentrated in two clusters:

| Hub | Why |
|-----|-----|
| **Johor Bahru** | Closest Malaysian land to Singapore; hyperscalers' overflow market; massive pipeline |
| **Cyberjaya / Klang Valley** | Original MSC Malaysia zone; government-linked DC operators; telco neutral; established colocation market |

Secondary interest is emerging in Penang (tech manufacturing corridor) and Sabah/Sarawak (submarine cable landing stations).

### Governing Authorities & Regulation

- **MCMC** (Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission): Governs licensed communications facilities; DCs may require an Applications Service Provider (ASP) licence
- **TNB** (Tenaga Nasional Berhad): Power supply — grid connection is the #1 bottleneck for DC development
- **MIDA** (Malaysian Investment Development Authority): Incentives for qualifying DC investments (pioneer status, investment tax allowance)
- **State planning authorities**: Industrial land conversion and development orders (e.g., JPBD Selangor for JB-adjacent parcels, JPBDWP for Cyberjaya)
- **MSC Malaysia status**: Cyberjaya DCs may qualify; provides incentives and regulatory clarity

### Grid Connectivity — The Critical Bottleneck (2026)

As of 2026, the surge in DC demand has created severe grid capacity constraints:

| Market Type | Grid Connection Wait |
|-------------|---------------------|
| Emerging / secondary markets | ~24 months |
| Core markets (JB, Cyberjaya) | Up to **8 years** |

*(Source: JLL Malaysia 2Q2026)*

This has forced operators to pursue:
- Renewable energy (solar PPAs) and embedded generation
- Dedicated substations negotiated with TNB as part of large-scale commitments
- Site selection increasingly driven by substation proximity, not just land cost

---

## Investor Implications

### Industrial Land Play

The most accessible entry point for property investors is **industrial land in data centre clusters**:
- Johor Bahru's industrial land at **RM86 psf (2025 national avg, +8.4% YoY)** — DC-adjacent parcels likely well above this
- Demand is structural and long-duration (hyperscaler capex cycles are 10–20 year commitments)
- **Risk**: Grid access is a hard constraint; land without confirmed power capacity has limited DC value

### Completed DC Buildings

Not yet accessible to Malaysian retail investors at scale. Institutional market only:
- REITs: No Malaysia-listed DC REIT exists as of 2026 (Singapore's Keppel DC REIT and Digital Core REIT are the regional proxies)
- Direct investment: Requires institutional scale (US$50M+ minimum)

### AI Demand Multiplier

Generative AI and GPU computing are dramatically increasing **power density per rack** (from 5–10 kW traditional to 40–100+ kW for AI inference). This means:
- Each MW of new AI-capable DC capacity requires more cooling infrastructure, more power, more sophisticated facilities
- Older DCs are being retrofitted or decommissioned as AI workloads demand newer, denser specs
- New-build DCs targeting AI workloads command higher rental rates per kW

### Key Operators Active in Malaysia (as of 2026)

| Operator | Type | Malaysia Presence |
|----------|------|-------------------|
| **Digital Edge** | Asian DC operator | Singapore-based; exploring US$10B sale (Apr 2026); regional presence |
| **Equinix** | Global colocation | KL presence |
| **ST Telemedia (STT GDC)** | Singapore-linked | Cyberjaya |
| **Nxera** (ex-GDS/Bridge DC) | Pan-Asian | JB focus |
| **AIMS** | Malaysia-based | Cyberjaya |
| **Microsoft / Google / AWS** | Hyperscalers | Major capex commitments in JB (2023–2026) |
| **TIME dotCom / Maxis / TM** | Telco-linked | Neutral carrier hotels across KL/Cyberjaya |

*Note: Hyperscaler-owned DCs are proprietary infrastructure — not available for external investment.*

---

## Key Risks

- **Grid bottleneck**: Up to 8-year wait for TNB grid connection in core markets — the single biggest development risk
- **Technology obsolescence**: AI demand shifts power density requirements rapidly; today's spec may be insufficient in 5 years
- **Cooling infrastructure**: Higher power density requires more advanced (and costly) liquid cooling; older air-cooled DCs face upgrade capital
- **Regulatory changes**: Government may introduce stricter environmental requirements (water usage, energy efficiency) for large DC operators
- **Concentration risk**: JB is highly dependent on Singapore's appetite as overflow; if Singapore expands domestic DC capacity, JB demand could moderate
- **Geopolitical risk**: Malaysia's neutrality is an asset; any shift in US-China tech decoupling policies could affect hyperscaler siting decisions

---

## Related Concepts

- [[concepts/foreign-ownership-rules]] — Foreign companies can fully own industrial land for DC in most Malaysia states
- [[entities/areas/johor]] — Primary DC cluster; see JLL 2Q2026 data
- [[entities/areas/sepang]] — Emerging industrial park near KLIA; potential future DC hub
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]] — Johor-specific JLL analysis
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-malaysia-data-centre-national-overview]] — National capacity map and investment thesis

notes.title: DIBS (Developer Interest Bearing Scheme)
notes.content:
# DIBS

## Definition

DIBS (Developer Interest Bearing Scheme) was a financing arrangement where the developer absorbed loan interest payments during the construction period. It was **banned by BNM in 2014** after being blamed for inflating property prices and speculative buying.

## How It Worked (Historical)

- Buyer got a loan; bank paid loan proceeds to developer progressively
- Developer paid buyer's interest to the bank during construction
- Buyer only started servicing the loan after VP
- Net effect: zero outflow for buyer during construction = lower barrier to speculate = price inflation

## Why It Was Banned

- Allowed buyers with no actual holding power to buy multiple properties
- Developers baked DIBS cost into launch prices (inflating nominal prices)
- Created artificial demand — prices didn't reflect real ability to pay
- BNM banned DIBS from 2014 onwards

## Current Relevance

- DIBS is banned for residential under HDA; check if commercial/industrial schemes have analogues
- Watch for "interest rebate" or "cashback" schemes that may approximate DIBS — these exist in grey areas
- Any scheme where developer effectively reduces the true price below list price is worth scrutinising

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]] | [[concepts/hoc]]

notes.title: Foreign Ownership Rules
notes.content:
# Foreign Ownership Rules

## Definition

Malaysia imposes minimum purchase price thresholds and other restrictions on property purchases by non-Malaysian citizens.

## How It Works

### Federal Minimum Price (General)
- **Residential:** RM 1,000,000 minimum purchase price for foreigners (per unit)
- **Commercial / Industrial:** generally no minimum for foreigners at federal level, but state consent may apply

### State-Level Variations (verify current — policies change)
| State | Residential Min | Notes |
|-------|----------------|-------|
| Selangor | RM 2,000,000 | As of 2021 gazette; verify current |
| KL/Federal Territory | RM 1,000,000 | Standard federal floor |
| Penang (Island) | RM 1,000,000 | Heritage zone may have additional restrictions |
| Johor | RM 1,000,000 (general) | Certain Iskandar zones may differ; Forest City had special status (verify current) |
| Other states | RM 1,000,000 | Standard; check each state's EXCO order |

### Process
- Foreign buyer must obtain **State Authority Consent** (Kebenaran Pihak Berkuasa Negeri) — typically 3–6 months
- Paid as part of stamp duty process; consent withholding is rare for eligible properties

### MM2H (Malaysia My Second Home)
- Long-term resident visa; historically gave certain property purchase benefits
- Programme was suspended, revamped, relaunched with stricter criteria (~2021+) — verify current status

## Investor Implications

- RM 1M minimum effectively limits foreign buyers to mid-high and above segments in most states
- Selangor's RM 2M threshold is significant — excludes most mid-market condos for foreigners
- Foreign buyers cannot buy Bumiputera lots or Malay reserve land
- Land / agricultural / industrial: check state-specific rules; commercial generally more accessible
- RPGT at Year 6+ is 10% for foreigners vs. 0% for citizens — factor into exit modelling

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/bumiputera-quota]] | [[concepts/rpgt]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]]

notes.title: Home Ownership Campaign (HOC)
notes.content:
# Home Ownership Campaign (HOC)

## Definition

HOC is a periodic Malaysian government initiative to stimulate property market activity, typically offering stamp duty exemptions and other incentives to buyers of residential properties within a defined price range.

## How It Works

- During HOC periods: exemption on **Memorandum of Transfer (MOT) stamp duty** and **loan agreement stamp duty** for qualifying properties
- Typically targets properties RM 300k–RM 2.5M (ranges vary by HOC iteration)
- Developers usually required to offer minimum discount (e.g. 10%) to participate
- HOC has run in different iterations: 2019, 2020 (COVID extension), 2021, subsequent versions — check current status

## Stamp Duty Savings Example

For a RM 600,000 property:
- MOT stamp duty normally: 1% on first RM 100k + 2% on next RM 400k + 3% on remainder = ~RM 11,000
- Loan stamp duty: 0.5% of loan = ~RM 2,700 (on 90% loan)
- Total saving under full HOC waiver: ~RM 13,700

## Investor Implications

- HOC periods create buying spikes — understand whether you're buying at HOC-inflated prices
- Savings are real but not transformative for investment decisions; don't let HOC be the sole reason to buy
- Developers price HOC discounts into launch prices — the "10% discount" may already be in the list price
- Check if HOC applies to secondary market (usually it does not — typically primary market launches only)

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]] | [[concepts/rpgt]]

notes.title: KL Classes of Use (CULB2025)
notes.content:
# KL Classes of Use (CULB2025)

## Definition

The **Classes of Use of Land and Buildings 2025 (CULB2025)** is the legally gazetted document (28 May 2025, under KLDCP 2024) that classifies all 68 permissible uses for land and buildings in Kuala Lumpur. It replaces earlier use class orders and determines what activities are permitted, permitted with conditions (PC), or not permitted (NP) in each land use zone.

This document is the authoritative reference for the **commercial vs. residential title question** — which directly affects utility tariffs, assessment tax, loan eligibility, and strata rules.

---

## The 10 Main Categories

| Category | Code | Examples |
|----------|------|---------|
| Commercial | A | Retail, office, SOHO, serviced apartment, hotel, F&B, entertainment |
| Residential | B | Condominium, apartment, terrace, semi-D, SOHO (residential occupation) |
| Industrial | C | Factory, warehouse, distribution, R&D |
| Institution | D | Government offices, NGO headquarters |
| Community Facility | E | Hospital, school, mosque, market |
| Cemetery | F | Cemetery, crematorium, funeral |
| Open Space | G | Parks, sports facilities, golf, urban farming |
| Infrastructure/Utility | H | Power, water, waste management |
| Transport | J | Bus terminal, park & ride, parking, airport |
| Forest Reserve | — | Protected forest |

---

## Critical Investment Classifications

### Serviced Apartment = Commercial (A10)

> "Accommodation provided in commercial land use zones, usually managed by a third party, consisting of businesses or hotel facilities not available in residential apartments. It does not include hotels, lodges, worker and student dormitories."

**Implication for investors:**
- Serviced apartments are **commercial property** — they attract **commercial utility tariffs** (significantly higher than residential)
- They pay **commercial assessment tax rates** (higher)
- Eligible for **commercial strata title** only
- Banks may apply **commercial loan terms** (lower margin of finance possible)
- **NOT permitted within residential developments** or residential zones
- In Technology Park zones: limited to **10% of total GFA**

### SOHO = Commercial (A11)

> "Office or service space that allows the provision of limited accommodation space in the individual premise."

- Primary purpose is **office** — residential occupation is incidental
- Classified as **Commercial (A)**, not Residential (B)
- SOVO, SOFO are **not distinct legal classes** in CULB2025 — market terms applied to SOHO-type products or TP zone variants
- Permissible office activities: professional services, IT, online business, spa, health clinic, secretariat
- Same commercial tax/utility implications as A10

### Residential Unit = Residential (B1)

> "Any type of residential unit whether landed or multi-storey."

Includes: terrace, semi-D, detached, apartment, condominium, townhouse, villa, cluster, senior living, council home, transit house, micro house.

- **Residential tariff for utilities** (lower)
- **Residential assessment rates** (lower)
- **Residential loan terms** (up to 90% margin for 1st/2nd property)
- **Residential strata rules** apply

---

## Permissibility Matrix (Key Combinations)

| Zone | B1 Residential | A10 Serv.Apt | A11 SOHO | C (Industrial) |
|------|----------------|-------------|---------|---------------|
| CCC (City Centre Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | NP |
| MC (Major Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | NP |
| C (Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | NP |
| MX (Mixed Development) | PC | PC | PC | NP |
| R1 (Landed Residential) | **P** | **NP** | **NP** | NP |
| R3 (Medium-High Residential) | **P** | PC | PC | NP |
| R4 (Affordable Housing) | **P** | PC | PC | NP |
| IP (Industrial Park) | NP | **NP** | **NP** | P |
| MXI (Mixed Industrial) | NP | **NP** | **NP** | P |
| TP (Technology Park) | PC (≤10%) | PC (≤10%) | PC (≤10%) | P |

P = Permitted | PC = Permitted with Conditions | NP = Not Permitted

---

## Key Rules for Specific Classes

### A9 — Tourist Accommodation (Hotels)
- Preferably within TPZ/TIZ and tourist hotspot areas
- **NOT permissible within residential developments**
- Strong planning rationale needed for standalone hotel in non-commercial zones

### A14 — Shopping Mall
- **Conversion of a row of commercial terrace into a shopping mall is NOT permissible**
- Must be in dedicated free-standing building or planned space
- EV charging in parking is required

### A6 — Entertainment / A8 — Betting
- Permissible with conditions in CCC/MC only
- Must be distanced from residential and institutional uses
- Separate access required

### B6 — Home-based Business
- Residential unit with small-scale business
- Maximum **5 employees**
- No disturbance to neighbours
- Permissible with conditions in residential zones

---

## Why This Matters for Investors

- **Due diligence on title:** Always verify whether a property is B1 (residential), A10 (serviced apartment), or A11 (SOHO) — the utility costs, loan terms, and taxes differ materially
- **SOHO and serviced apartments are harder to finance:** Some banks treat them as commercial loans — lower LTV (loan-to-value), higher interest rates
- **Exit market is narrower:** Commercial-titled units (A10/A11) have a smaller buyer pool than B1 residential — foreign buyers, EPF withdrawals, first-timer incentives typically apply to B1 only
- **MXI industrial zone prohibition:** Industrial-to-mixed developments cannot include serviced apartments or SOHOs — check zone before assuming mixed-use potential

---

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/kl-development-control]] | [[concepts/strata-title]] | [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]]

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v1p3-classes-of-use]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v1p1-development-control]]

notes.title: KL Development Control (KLDCP 2024)
notes.content:
# KL Development Control (KLDCP 2024)

## Definition

The KL Development Control Plan (KLDCP) 2024 — formally part of the KL Local Plan (KLLP2040) — is the legally binding document that governs what can be built, where, and at what intensity in Kuala Lumpur. Gazetted 28 May 2025 (P.U. (A) 166 and P.U. (B) 206), it replaces KLDCP2008. All planning applications submitted to DBKL must comply with it.

It has 4 volumes:
- **Vol 1 Part 1:** Development Control Rules (plot ratios, heights, setbacks, incentives)
- **Vol 1 Part 2:** Land Use Zone + Intensity Maps (site-specific)
- **Vol 1 Part 3:** Classes of Use of Land and Buildings (CULB2025)
- **Vol 2:** Promoting City Development (area-specific frameworks)

---

## Plot Ratio (Floor Area Ratio) Rules

Plot ratio is the primary intensity control tool — it determines total permissible GFA relative to land area.

**Plot Ratio = Total GFA ÷ Land Area**
Example: 10,000 sq ft site with plot ratio 1:8 → maximum 80,000 sq ft of GFA

### Base Plot Ratios by Zone

| Zone | Code | Max Plot Ratio |
|------|------|---------------|
| City Centre Commercial | CCC | **1:10** |
| Major Commercial | MC | **1:8** |
| Commercial | C | **1:8** |
| Local Commercial | LC | **1:2** |
| Mixed Development | MX | **1:8** |
| Industrial Park | IP | **1:4** |
| Mixed Industrial | MXI | **1:4** |
| Technology Park | TP | **1:3** |
| Residential 2 | R2 | **1:2.5** |
| Residential 3 | R3 | **1:4** |
| Residential 4 (Affordable) | R4 | **1:6** |
| R1, EH, TV1 (Landed) | — | Height control only |

> ⚠️ These are **maximum caps**. The actual approved plot ratio for a specific site is set in the DCP2 Intensity Map (Vol 1 Part 2), which may be lower. Always check the Intensity Map for the specific parcel.

### Plot Ratios Along Specific Roads (Commercial)

| Road | Plot Ratio |
|------|-----------|
| Jalan Tun Razak | 1:8 |
| Jalan Klang Lama, Jalan Ampang | 1:6 |
| Jalan Cheras | 1:5 |
| Jalan Puchong, Kepong, Ipoh, Syed Putra, Kuchai Lama, Gombak, Pahang, Genting Klang | 1:4 |

---

## Building Height Limits

| Zone / Context | Height |
|---------------|--------|
| CCC general | Up to 14 storeys |
| CCC / MC — Secondary Heritage Zone or heritage shophouses | Up to 7 storeys |
| MC outside City Centre | Up to 8 storeys |
| MC — Special Character Zones (Jln TAR, Masjid India, Chow Kit) | Up to 10 storeys |
| C (Commercial) outside city centre | 2–6 storeys |
| LC (Local Commercial) | Up to 4 storeys |
| R1 / EH / TV1 (Landed residential) | Up to 3 storeys (landed strata: 4 levels) |

Note: Heritage areas have a 200m buffer zone with additional height and character controls.

---

## Mixed Development (MX Zone) Rules

- **Residential is mandatory** in MX zones for large lots (>20,000 sq ft)
- Exception: if commercial does NOT exceed **40% of total GFA**, residential not required
- Beyond 40% commercial on large lot → residential component becomes mandatory
- Typical ratio when both present: **60% residential : 40% commercial**
- For small lots (<20,000 sq ft in residential areas): commercial-only permitted with restricted use classes (A1, A2, A3, A4, A7, A9, A18 only)

---

## Density and Unit Size Rules

- **Minimum unit size (free market):** 450 sq ft (designed for DINK, YUPPIES, seniors)
- **Large unit rule (PPA applies):** Only for >1,600 sq ft units; only at 5 locations:
  - Mont Kiara, Jalan Robson, Jalan U-Thant, Bukit Pantai, Bukit Bangsar

---

## Setback Requirements

| Context | Setback |
|---------|---------|
| Cat I & II rivers | **10m** (+ 7m public easement + 3m transition) |
| Cat III rivers | **7m** (+ 4m public easement + 3m transition) |
| Lakes and ponds | **10m** (+ 7m public easement + 3m transition) |
| No permanent structures or vehicles in Public Easement Zone | Absolute |

---

## Plot Ratio Incentive System (Bonus FAR)

Four categories of incentives are available above the base plot ratio:

### 1. TPZ/TIZ Incentives (Transit-Oriented Development)
See [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]] for full detail.

| Distance from Station | Max Incentive (Cat 1 station) |
|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Within 400m (TPZ) | **+20%** |
| Within 600m (TIZ) | **+15%** |

### 2. Lot Amalgamation Incentive
For sites that merge multiple small lots into a larger development footprint.

### 3. Redevelopment Incentive
For brownfield, greyfield (unviable), or dilapidated residential sites:
- Requirement: at least **30% of entire development** must be mixed-income housing including affordable housing
- Priority given to existing residents

### 4. Affordable Housing (RMM) Incentive
- Applicable in CCC, MC, C, MX, R3, R4 zones
- Minimum site area: 30,000 sq ft
- Unit specs: 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, kitchen, living/dining, 1 parking space
- PA/PPR redevelopment: must increase affordable units by at least **20%** over existing count
- R4 affordable housing: base plot ratio up to **1:6.0**

---

## 7 Planning Control Layers (Overlaid on Base Zoning)

1. Intensity Control of Layout Plan — approved schemes
2. River, Lake and Pond Side Corridors
3. Heritage Areas (200m buffer)
4. Height Control Zone around Istana Negara
5. Line of Sight to Iconic Landmarks (visual corridors)
6. Public Open Space in Private Schemes (OS4)
7. Affordable Housing (RMM) tracking

---

## Investor Implications

- **CCC plots (1:10 plot ratio) near TPZ Cat 1 stations** → potential up to **1:12** with incentives; highly valuable for redevelopment
- **R3 → MX rezoning** near transit nodes is a common developer play; monitor applications
- **River setbacks** directly reduce effective developable area on riverine sites — model carefully
- **Heritage areas** (200m buffer around KLCC, Brickfields, etc.) constrain height — affects uplift potential
- **MX zone commercial cap at 40%** means most "mixed development" projects are primarily residential with a small podium of commercial
- Checking DCP2 (site-specific intensity map) before any acquisition is essential — the base zone may say 1:8 but the site-specific map may say 1:4

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]] | [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]] | [[concepts/strata-title]] | [[concepts/bumiputera-quota]]

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v1p1-development-control]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v1p2-land-use-zone-maps]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v2-promoting-city-development]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-klsp2040]]

notes.title: Leasehold vs Freehold
notes.content:
# Leasehold vs Freehold

## Definition

**Freehold** land is held in perpetuity — the owner retains ownership of both the building and the land indefinitely. **Leasehold** land is held for a fixed term (typically 99 years in Malaysia, sometimes 999 years or 60 years for industrial), after which ownership reverts to the state unless renewed.

---

## How It Works in Malaysia

### Freehold
- Owner holds title indefinitely; no expiry
- Can be passed to heirs without restriction
- Generally commands a **premium** of 10–20% over equivalent leasehold in the same area
- Predominantly found in older KL areas, parts of Penang island, and older Selangor townships

### Leasehold (99-year)
- Countdown starts from the date of alienation (grant), NOT from when you buy it
- A leasehold property built in 1980 on a 1970 alienation has only ~44 years left as of 2026
- Banks become more conservative on loan margin as the unexpired term shortens
- Below **30 years unexpired:** most banks will not finance; secondary market liquidity collapses
- Renewal is possible but not guaranteed — requires application to the State Authority (SUK)

### 999-Year Leasehold
- Effectively freehold in practice; found mostly in Penang
- Banks treat it the same as freehold
- Negligible discount to freehold

### Industrial Leasehold (60-year)
- Common for industrial land in established parks
- Does not affect yield materially if held for normal investment horizons
- Watch remaining term carefully at time of purchase

---

## Valuation Haircut Rules of Thumb

| Unexpired Lease | Typical Haircut vs Freehold Equivalent |
|----------------|---------------------------------------|
| 70+ years | ~5–10% discount |
| 50–70 years | ~10–20% discount |
| 30–50 years | ~20–40% discount; bank financing restricted |
| <30 years | >40% discount; financing near-impossible; avoid |

---

## Key States Where Leasehold is Common

- **KL (DBKL land):** Majority of government-built housing (PA, PPR) is leasehold; many commercial titles in prime areas are also leasehold
- **Selangor:** Large portions of suburban KV (Shah Alam, Subang, Klang) are 99-year leasehold
- **Johor/Iskandar Malaysia:** Much of the new development land is leasehold 99-year
- **Penang Island:** Mix; some freehold in older Georgetown areas; new reclaimed land is leasehold

---

## Investor Implications

- Always check the **original alienation date** and calculate actual remaining term — don't assume from construction year
- Leasehold at ~80+ years remaining is serviceable for most investment horizons; the haircut is modest
- Refinancing leasehold properties becomes harder as they age — factor into exit planning
- In KL specifically, many major developments (MRT corridor condos, Sentul regeneration, Bandar Malaysia) are on leasehold or state land — understand the title before buying
- DBKL land in KL is often alienated as leasehold to developers; check individual title

---

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/strata-title]] | [[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]] | [[concepts/rpgt]]

## Sources

*No sources ingested yet — seed knowledge.*

notes.title: Loan Margin and DSR
notes.content:
# Loan Margin and DSR

## Definition

**Loan Margin** is the percentage of property price a bank will finance. **DSR (Debt Service Ratio)** is the borrower's total monthly debt obligations as a percentage of gross income — the key determinant of loan eligibility.

## Loan Margin Rules (Malaysia)

| Property | Margin |
|----------|--------|
| 1st and 2nd residential property | Up to 90% (10% down) |
| 3rd residential property and beyond | Up to 70% (30% down) |
| Commercial / industrial | Up to 85–90% depending on bank |
| Foreigner buyers | Typically 60–80%; varies by bank |

**Note:** BNM's macro-prudential rules — 3rd property onwards at 70% was introduced to cool speculation. Check current BNM guidelines.

## DSR

- Most banks approve loans where total monthly commitments ≤ 60–70% of gross income
- Includes all existing loans: car, personal loan, credit card minimum payments, existing mortgages
- Some banks use net income (after EPF deduction); others gross
- Income documentation: salary slip + EA form + EPF statement (employment); bank statements + tax returns (self-employed)

## OPR and Mortgage Rates

- Malaysia's **Overnight Policy Rate (OPR)** is set by Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM)
- Variable mortgage rates are typically: OPR + 1.5–2.0% per annum
- Fixed rate packages available; usually slightly higher than variable
- Check BNM website for current OPR before modelling any deal

## Practical Implications

- **Leverage** is powerful for capital appreciation plays — but carry cost (instalment) must be covered by rental or self-funded
- High DSR kills loan eligibility — pay down high-interest consumer debt before applying
- CCRIS report (BNM credit bureau) is checked by all banks; late payments stay 12 months
- Stress-test loans at OPR + 1–2% higher than today for sensitivity

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/rental-yield-calculation]] | [[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/rpgt]]

notes.title: Property Overhang
notes.content:
# Property Overhang

## Definition

A **property overhang** refers to completed but unsold residential or commercial units that have been on the market for more than 9 months. It is a key indicator of supply-demand imbalance and is tracked quarterly by **NAPIC (National Property Information Centre)**, under JPPH (Valuation and Property Services Department, Ministry of Finance Malaysia).

---

## How NAPIC Tracks It

- **NAPIC** publishes the *Property Market Report* (full year) and *Property Market Status Report* (half-year)
- **Overhang** = completed units unsold for ≥ 9 months
- **Unsold under construction** = units not yet complete, booked but unsold
- **Unsold not constructed** = approved projects not yet started

These three together form the total "supply pipeline" picture.

**Where to find NAPIC data:** [jpph.gov.my](https://www.jpph.gov.my) or via NAPIC portal — free download, quarterly updates.

---

## Malaysia Overhang Context

### Residential Overhang (recent history, verify with current NAPIC)
- Peak overhang: ~30,000–40,000 units nationally around 2018–2020
- KL and Johor historically the worst affected states
- Serviced apartments and SOHOs the most overhung sub-type in KL city centre
- Selangor + KL together typically account for ~40% of national overhang

### Commercial Overhang
- Retail and office space facing structural headwinds (e-commerce, WFH)
- KL city centre office vacancy elevated post-2023 with TRX/Merdeka 118 new supply coming online

### Industrial
- Consistently low overhang due to strong demand from logistics/data centres/manufacturing FDI

---

## Why Overhang Matters for Investors

- High overhang in a segment = **pricing pressure on resale and rental** in that micro-market
- Developers in overhung segments offer heavy rebates, furniture packages, and deferred payment schemes — these inflate nominal list prices vs. actual transaction prices
- NAPIC transaction prices (actual) vs. list prices can diverge by 10–20% in overhung markets
- **Yield compression:** High vacancy in overhung buildings pushes rental income down; gross yield figures based on asking rent may be misleading

### Red Flags
- SOHO/serviced apartment segments in KL city centre — structurally challenged
- Johor high-rise condos — persistent overhang linked to speculative buying during Iskandar hype cycles
- Any project with high unsold-under-construction ratio signals developer cashflow pressure

---

## Useful NAPIC Metrics to Track

| Metric | What it tells you |
|--------|-----------------|
| Overhang volume (units) | Scale of unsold completed stock |
| Overhang value (RM) | Price segment affected |
| % overhang by state | Which states are worst hit |
| Unsold under construction | Forward supply pipeline risk |
| Transaction volume (deals) | Market activity / liquidity |
| Median transaction price | Actual (not asking) prices |

---

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/rental-yield-calculation]] | [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]] | [[concepts/leasehold-vs-freehold]]

## Sources

*No sources ingested yet. Ingest NAPIC reports to populate with current data.*

notes.title: Quit Rent and Assessment
notes.content:
# Quit Rent and Assessment

## Quit Rent (Cukai Tanah)

**Definition:** Annual land tax payable to the state government on freehold and leasehold land.

- Rate: set by state; typically very low — RM 50–300/year for a residential unit
- Invoiced annually; usually collected by developer/JMB and passed through to owners
- Failure to pay can result in government action (rare but theoretically land reverts to state after 3 years default)
- **Practical impact on yield:** minimal (< RM 300/year for most residential); larger for industrial land holdings

## Assessment (Cukai Taksiran / Cukai Pintu)

**Definition:** Local authority (PBT) tax on property to fund local government services (road, waste, etc.).

- Rate: set as % of Annual Value (estimated rental value) by local council
- Typically 4–8% of annual rental value; billed twice a year
- **Example:** Selangor MBPJ might assess a condo at RM 1,500/month rental value = RM 18,000/year × 4% = RM 720/year (RM 360 per half-year bill)
- Commercial/industrial properties face higher assessment rates than residential
- Payable by owner (not tenant); factor into net yield calculation

## Practical Yield Impact

For a RM 500,000 residential condo rented at RM 2,000/month:
- Quit rent: ~RM 100/year
- Assessment: ~RM 600–900/year
- Total: ~RM 700–1,000/year deducted from gross rental

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/rental-yield-calculation]] | [[concepts/strata-title]] | [[concepts/rpgt]]

notes.title: Rental Yield Calculation
notes.content:
# Rental Yield Calculation

## Definition

Rental yield measures the annual rental income as a percentage of the property purchase price (or current value). It is the primary metric for evaluating a property as an income investment.

## Gross Yield

```
Gross Yield (%) = (Annual Rental Income / Purchase Price) × 100
```

Example: Property bought at RM 500,000, rented at RM 2,000/month
→ (2,000 × 12 / 500,000) × 100 = **4.8% gross yield**

## Net Yield

```
Net Yield (%) = ((Annual Rental − Annual Expenses) / Purchase Price) × 100
```

**Typical annual expenses to deduct:**
- Maintenance/service charges (e.g. RM 0.35 PSF/month × built-up)
- Sinking fund (usually ~10% of maintenance fee)
- Quit rent (cukai tanah) — RM 50–200/year typically
- Assessment (cukai taksiran) — 0.5–2% of annual rental value; check local council
- Property agent renewal fee (typically 1 month rent every 2 years)
- Vacancy allowance (1–2 months per year conservatively)
- Repairs and maintenance (budget 1–2% of property value per year)
- Insurance (fire + landlord — RM 300–800/year typically)
- Income tax on rental income (applicable above RM 34,000 per year net)

## Cash-on-Cash Return (for leveraged purchase)

```
Cash-on-Cash (%) = (Annual Net Cash Flow / Cash Invested) × 100
```

Where:
- Net Cash Flow = Annual Rental − Annual Loan Instalments − Annual Expenses
- Cash Invested = Down payment + Legal/stamp duty costs + Renovation

## Malaysia Benchmarks (approximate, varies by location)

| Segment | Gross Yield | Net Yield |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| KV prime condo | 3.5–5% | 2–3.5% |
| KV mid-range condo | 4–6% | 2.5–4% |
| Penang island condo | 3.5–5% | 2–3.5% |
| JB condo | 4–7% | 2.5–5% |
| Industrial | 5–8% | 4–6.5% |
| Shop lot | 4–6% | 3–5% |

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/quit-rent-and-assessment]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]] | [[concepts/strata-title]]

notes.title: Real Property Gains Tax (RPGT)
notes.content:
# Real Property Gains Tax (RPGT)

## Definition

RPGT is a capital gains tax levied on profits from the disposal (sale) of real property in Malaysia. Administered by LHDN (Inland Revenue Board).

## How It Works (Rate Structure — verify current rates as policy changes)

| Holding Period | Citizens/PR | Non-Citizens | Companies |
|----------------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| Year 1 | 30% | 30% | 30% |
| Year 2 | 20% | 30% | 20% |
| Year 3 | 15% | 30% | 15% |
| Year 4 | 15% | 30% | 15% |
| Year 5 | 5% | 30% | 5% |
| Year 6+ | 0% | 10% (from 2019)* | 10% |

*Rates have changed multiple times. Always verify current LHDN gazette.*

**Key exemptions:**
- One-time lifetime exemption on disposal of a private residence (one property per person per lifetime)
- Gifts between family members — partial exemption available
- Low-cost housing disposals may be exempt

## Malaysia-Specific Context

- Calculated on **net chargeable gain** = disposal price − acquisition cost − permitted expenses (legal fees, renovation, SPA stamp duty, agent fees)
- Seller must submit Form CKHT 1A within 60 days of disposal
- Buyer must withhold 3% of purchase price (from non-citizen sellers) / 3% for citizen sellers if property held < 5 years — paid to LHDN

## Investor Implications

- **Hold past Year 5 (6th year onward):** 0% for citizens — the most common tax planning strategy
- Factor RPGT into exit planning — a property sold in Year 3 at RM 100k gain costs RM 15k in tax
- RPGT policy is politically sensitive and changes periodically — model conservatively
- Non-citizen investors face 10% even after Year 5 — significant cost to model

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/rental-yield-calculation]] | [[concepts/quit-rent-and-assessment]]

notes.title: SPA Process (Sale & Purchase Agreement)
notes.content:
# SPA Process

## Definition

The Sale and Purchase Agreement (SPA) is the legally binding contract between buyer and seller (or developer) for a property transaction. It sets out payment schedule, completion timeline, and obligations of both parties.

## Primary Market (New Launch — Developer SPA)

**Standard Payment Schedule (HDA — Housing Development Act):**

| Stage | % of Price |
|-------|-----------|
| SPA signing (within 30 days of booking) | 10% (less booking fee) |
| Approval of building plans | 5% |
| Completion of earthworks/foundation | 10% |
| Completion of framework/structural | 15% |
| Completion of walls/roof/windows | 10% |
| Completion of internal plumbing/wiring | 10% |
| Completion of road/drainage/sewerage | 5% |
| Completion of carpark | 5% |
| Certificate of Completion & Compliance (CCC) | 10% |
| Notice of VP | 12.5% |
| Strata/individual title delivery | 2.5% |
| **Total** | **100%** |

**Note:** Actual schedule varies; verify against your SPA. Non-HDA projects (commercial, industrial) have different schedules.

## Secondary Market (Sub-Sale SPA)

- Buyer and seller agree on price; deposit typically 10%
- Balance (90% or as agreed) due within 90 days of SPA (standard) — usually funded by bank loan
- Stamp duty on MOT (Memorandum of Transfer): progressive rates on purchase price
- Legal fees: ~1% of first RM 150k + 0.7% on next RM 850k + 0.6% beyond RM 1M (scale fees; verify SRB schedule)

## Key Dates

- **VP (Vacant Possession):** when developer hands keys; DLP starts
- **CCC (Certificate of Completion and Compliance):** issued by local authority; required for final payment
- **Strata title issuance:** separate process; can lag VP by years

## Investor Implications

- Primary market: cashflow impact is progressive — payments over construction period
- DIBS (Developer Interest Bearing Scheme) — if developer pays interest during construction, it's built into price; watch for this
- Late delivery: developer pays LAD (Liquidated Ascertained Damages) at 10% per annum on amounts paid — collect it, don't waive it
- Bumiputera consent and state authority consent should be resolved before SPA is executed

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/strata-title]] | [[concepts/rpgt]] | [[concepts/dibs]] | [[concepts/loan-margin-and-dsr]]

notes.title: Strata Title
notes.content:
# Strata Title

## Definition

A strata title is a form of property ownership for multi-storey buildings (condominiums, serviced apartments, shop offices in a shared building) that grants individual ownership of a unit (parcel) plus shared ownership of common property (lobbies, pools, lifts, etc.).

## How It Works

- **Individual strata title:** the unit owner holds a separate title for their parcel
- **Common property:** owned collectively by all strata owners; managed by Management Corporation (MC) or Joint Management Body (JMB)
- **Share units:** each parcel is assigned a share unit value determining voting rights and contribution to maintenance
- **Maintenance fee:** monthly levy per square foot charged by MC/JMB to cover common area upkeep
- **Sinking fund:** a mandatory reserve fund (at least 10% of maintenance fee) for capital expenditure (lift overhaul, roof repair, etc.)
- Governed by **Strata Management Act 2013 (SMA 2013)** and **Strata Titles Act 1985**

## Key Stages

1. **Vacant Possession (VP)** — developer hands keys; JMB formed
2. **Strata title issuance** — can take years after VP; developer's responsibility
3. **MC takes over** from JMB once strata titles issued; AGM held
4. **Developer's Defect Liability Period (DLP)** — typically 24 months from VP; developer responsible for defects

## Investor Implications

- Check maintenance fee PSF before buying — high fees eat into yield
- Check sinking fund balance — low sinking fund = deferred capex risk = special levy risk
- Delayed strata title issuance is a red flag; affects ability to refinance, sell, subdivide
- Non-paying owners (maintenance fee defaulters) affect building quality — check MC meeting minutes if possible
- SoHo/SOFO/SOVO units are commercially-classified strata — different assessment rates, higher utility tariff

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/spa-process]] | [[concepts/quit-rent-and-assessment]] | [[concepts/rental-yield-calculation]]

notes.title: TPZ/TIZ Transit Planning Zone Incentives (KL)
notes.content:
# TPZ/TIZ Transit Planning Zone Incentives (KL)

## Definition

The **Transit Planning Zone (TPZ)** and **Transit Influence Zone (TIZ)** are KL-specific incentive zones established under KLDCP 2024 to encourage Transit-Oriented Development (TOD). Sites within these zones qualify for **additional plot ratio above the base** if they meet qualifying criteria. The system covers **103 transit stations** across KL.

- **TPZ:** Within **400 metres** of a station box (entrance)
- **TIZ:** Within **600 metres** of a station box

---

## Incentive Rates

| Station Category | TPZ (400m) | TIZ (600m) |
|-----------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Category 1** — Major interchanges | **+20%** of base plot ratio | **+15%** |
| **Category 2** — Interchange stations | **+15%** | **+10%** |
| **Category 3** — Single transit stations | **+10%** | **+7.5%** |
| **Category 4** — Lower category stations | **+5%** | **+5%** |

**Example:** A CCC zone site (base plot ratio 1:8) within 400m of a Category 1 station → maximum plot ratio with incentive: **1:9.6** (+20%)

---

## Qualifying Criteria

**Mandatory (must fulfil):**
- Direct **physical connection** to the station (covered walkway, pedestrian bridge, or underground link)

**Supporting criteria (at least 1 of 9 required; more = higher incentive tier):**
1. Provision of affordable housing or affordable commercial space
2. Functional public open space / plaza accessible to public
3. **Green building certification** (national or global: GBI, GreenRE, LEED, BREEAM, etc.)
4. Green city initiatives (green walls, vertical green, water elements, urban farming)
5. Community facilities on-site
6. Low Carbon City elements (EV charging, energy efficiency, solar PV)
7. Safe City elements (CCTV, bollards, crime-prevention design — CPTED)
8. Smart City elements (IoT, digital management, smart metering)
9. Resilient City elements (SUDS/sponge city drainage, flood resilience)

*(3 of 9 criteria → higher tier incentive; 5 of 9 → maximum incentive)*

---

## Category 1 Stations (Highest Incentive — Up to +20%)

These are the most valuable TOD locations in KL:

**MRT1 (Kajang Line):**
- Muzium Negara, Pasar Seni, Merdeka, Bukit Bintang, **Tun Razak Exchange (TRX)**

**MRT2 (Putrajaya Line / SSP Line):**
- Titiwangsa, Ampang Park, Persiaran KLCC, TRX, Bandar Malaysia North, Bandar Malaysia South

**LRT Kelana Jaya Line:**
- Pasar Seni, Masjid Jamek, KLCC, Ampang Park

**LRT Sri Petaling / Ampang Line:**
- Pudu, Hang Tuah, Plaza Rakyat, Masjid Jamek, Titiwangsa

**Monorail:**
- KL Sentral, Hang Tuah, Bukit Bintang, Titiwangsa

**HSR (planned):**
- Bandar Malaysia

**Integrated hubs:**
- KL Sentral

---

## Investment Implications

- **Buy near Cat 1 stations before intensification:** Sites with existing low plot ratio near a Cat 1 station have the highest redevelopment upside — developers can unlock +20% GFA by meeting the criteria
- **Direct station connection is key:** Simply being within 400m is not enough — a physical walkway/connection is mandatory to qualify. Check if any existing or planned link is documented in the planning application
- **Green certification is increasingly standard:** The fact that GBI/LEED qualifies as a supporting criterion means virtually all new developments will attempt to tick that box — it's table stakes, not a differentiator
- **TRX area is double-stacked (Cat 1 MRT1 + Cat 1 MRT2):** Two separate Cat 1 stations; TRX-adjacent land is among the most intensifiable in KL
- **Wangsa Maju LRT** is Category-listed under the system — new node for carbon-neutral growth centre
- **MRT3 (Circle Line, proposed):** If built, will add ~28 stations within KL, creating entirely new TPZ/TIZ zones in areas currently outside the system (Mont Kiara, Sri Hartamas, Chow Kit, Setapak)

---

## Related Concepts

[[concepts/kl-development-control]] | [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]] | [[concepts/strata-title]]

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v1p1-development-control]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v2-promoting-city-development]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-klsp2040]]

notes.content:
# BRDB Semenyih Mixed Development

## Project Overview

Mixed residential development by BRDB in Semenyih, Selangor, approximately 40 km south of KL city centre near Kajang. The development is split into two parcels with distinct programmes:

- **Parcel 1 – FMH (Free Market Home):** Two residential towers (Tower A + Tower B) over a shared podium carpark (5 levels) and facilities floor. Market-rate units.
- **Parcel 2 – RSKU (Rumah Selangorku):** One affordable residential tower over a podium carpark (6 levels) and facilities floor. State-mandated affordable housing quota under the Selangor Rumah Selangorku programme.

This cost estimate (Report No. 2b / Cost Plan No. 2a) was prepared by JKQS as at September 2024. Contingencies were set at 0% — the estimate is already considered to incorporate sufficient allowance at this stage. Preliminaries are at 7%.

| Component | GFA (m²) | GFA (sqft) |
|---|---|---|
| Parcel 1 – FMH Podium Carpark (L1–L5) | 46,712 | 502,858 |
| Parcel 1 – FMH Facilities (G + L6) | 3,489 | 37,556 |
| Parcel 1 – FMH Tower A (L1–Roof) | 45,286 | 487,507 |
| Parcel 1 – FMH Tower B (L1–Roof) | 41,161 | 443,101 |
| **Parcel 1 Total GFA** | **136,648** | **1,471,022** |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Podium Carpark (L1–L6) | 13,793 | 148,477 |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Facilities (G + L7) | 1,980 | 21,312 |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Tower (G–Roof) | 24,589 | 264,705 |
| **Parcel 2 Total GFA** | **40,362** | **434,494** |
| **Total GFA** | **177,010** | **1,904,543** |
| **Total CFA** | **193,152** | **2,079,069** |
| **Total NSA** | **85,596** | **921,350** |

---

## Cost Summary

### By Parcel / Component

| Component | Total Cost (MYR) | Cost/m² GFA | Cost/sqft GFA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parcel 1 – FMH Podium Carpark | 27,702,514 | 593 | 55.10 |

| **Parcel 1 Total** | **200,620,231** | **1,354 (CFA basis)** | **125.78 (CFA basis)** |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Podium Carpark | 7,119,673 | 516 | 47.95 |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Facilities | 2,287,339 | 1,155 | 107.30 |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU Tower | 22,884,411 | 931 | 86.44 |
| **Parcel 2 Total** | **48,331,375** | **1,075 (CFA basis)** | **99.85 (CFA basis)** |
| **GRAND TOTAL** | **248,951,606** | **1,289 (CFA basis)** | **119.74 (CFA basis)** |

> ⚠️ Note: Per-component costs above are pre-M&E and pre-External Works for individual towers — those categories are reported at project total level only. Parcel totals include all allocated costs. CFA basis used for inter-parcel comparison per JKQS methodology.

---

## Cost Benchmarks

| Metric | This Project | Market Range (2024–2025) | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| FMH Tower A construction cost (incl. prelims, excl. M&E & ext. works) | MYR 107.86 PSF GFA | Mass market high-rise: MYR 150–220 PSF | ⬇ Below range — excludes M&E & external works |
| FMH Tower (all-in, CFA basis) | MYR 125.78 PSF CFA | Mass market high-rise: MYR 150–220 PSF | ⬇ Below range — CFA broader than GFA; Semenyih suburban spec |
| RSKU Tower construction cost | MYR 86.44 PSF GFA | Low-cost/affordable: MYR 80–120 PSF | ✅ Within range |
| Project average (all-in, CFA) | MYR 119.74 PSF CFA | — | Blended affordable + market rate |

**Key context:** Semenyih is a suburban satellite town ~40 km south of KL. Construction costs are expected to be lower than KL city / Klang Valley urban core due to simpler site conditions, lower labour premiums, and mass-market finishes specification. The RSKU component (Rumah Selangorku affordable housing) drives the overall project average down. The individual component figures exclude M&E and External Works — full all-in costs are approximately 25–30% higher per element.

---

## BOQ Highlights

*Detailed quantities (concrete m³, rebar tonnes, etc.) are in the source QS report. The estimate is elemental — unit quantities are in the JKQS piling tender reference (PTE No. 4) for piling and in trade-level sub-schedules not reproduced in this summary.*

Notable allowances from the estimate:
- Piling basis: Referenced to JKQS Piling Tender Estimate No. 4 (not reproduced in this report)
- ID Works: MYR 2,500,000 lump sum allowance across FMH (Parcel 1 only)
- Sports & Equipment: MYR 500,000 allowance (Parcel 1 only)
- Landscape: All provisional
- Ancillary buildings: Guard house, refuse chamber, perimeter fencing, connection bridge (RSKU), Surau & Jenazah, hydrant tank & pump room — all allowance only
- Outside boundary: Sewerage pumping station (5,000 PE), road works, IWSS contribution included

---

## Key Unit Rates

| Upper Floor (Slab) | MYR 140/m² | MYR 133/m² | MYR 135/m² | MYR 117/m² | MYR 104/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/structural]] |
| External Walls & Façade | MYR 10/m² | MYR 48/m² | MYR 49/m² | MYR 9/m² | MYR 29/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/external-finishes]] |
| Windows | MYR <1/m² | MYR 41/m² | MYR 41/m² | MYR <1/m² | MYR 43/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/doors-windows]] |
| Doors | MYR 2/m² | MYR 67/m² | MYR 67/m² | MYR 4/m² | MYR 82/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/doors-windows]] |
| Internal Wall Finishes | MYR 9/m² | MYR 105/m² | MYR 105/m² | MYR 11/m² | MYR 89/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/internal-finishes]] |
| Internal Floor Finishes | MYR 13/m² | MYR 104/m² | MYR 105/m² | MYR 15/m² | MYR 71/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/floor-finishes]] |
| External Finishes | MYR 20/m² | MYR 63/m² | MYR 63/m² | MYR 11/m² | MYR 50/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/external-finishes]] |

| Preliminaries (7%) | MYR 39/m² | MYR 76/m² | MYR 77/m² | MYR 34/m² | MYR 61/m² | [[construction-costs/rates/preliminaries]] |

---

## Contractor & Procurement Notes

- **QS Firm:** JKQS
- **Report reference:** JK506, Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (Cost Plan No. 2a), dated 12 September 2024
- **Piling basis:** Referenced to Piling Tender Estimate No. 4 (PTE No. 4) — separate document

- **Contingencies:** 0% — estimate stated to be sufficiently allowanced at this stage
- **Preliminaries:** 7% of group elemental cost
- **External works:** Both inside-boundary and outside-boundary works included; outside-boundary includes new sewerage pumping station (5,000 PE), major road works, and Integrated Water Supply Scheme (IWSS) corridor contribution — significant infrastructure burden for this suburban site
