notes.content:
# External Works & Ancillary Buildings — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Covers Element 7 in Malaysian QS practice. Split into:
- **Inside Boundary:** Site clearance, earthworks, retaining walls, internal roads & hardstandings, stormwater drainage (with OSD tank), soil drainage, water reticulation, M&E infrastructure (TNB substation, telecoms, compound lighting), landscape, ancillary buildings (guard house, refuse chamber, fencing, surau, pump room)
- **Outside Boundary:** Sewerage pumping station, off-site road works, external stormwater, IWSS contribution and other authority/infrastructure charges

Outside-boundary items are particularly site- and authority-specific — they can add 5–10% to total project cost for suburban developments where offsite infrastructure is required by JBA, JPS, or the local authority.

---

## Rate Table

> Data from one project (BRDB Semenyih, Sep 2024 estimate). All rates are elemental cost per m² CFA (external works are not meaningfully allocable to individual buildings; CFA basis used per JKQS methodology).

### Inside Boundary

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Site Clearance & Earthworks | Clearing, cut & fill, compaction | /m² CFA | 15.76 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Retaining Walls | RC retaining walls; suburban hillside site | /m² CFA | 25.52 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Roads & Hardstandings (internal) | Internal access roads, car park surface, hardscape — allowance only | /m² CFA | 14.85 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Stormwater Drainage (incl. OSD Tank) | JPS-compliant drainage; on-site detention tank | /m² CFA | 16.72 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Soil Drainage | Sub-soil drainage | /m² CFA | 1.13 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Water Reticulation System | Internal water supply pipe network | /m² CFA | 5.38 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| M&E Infrastructure (TNB, Telecoms, Lighting) | Authority connection points, compound lighting | /m² CFA | 4.28 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Soft & Hard Landscape (Provisional) | Landscaping fully provisional; subject to LA design | /m² CFA | 20.23 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Ancillary Buildings (Guard House, Refuse, Fencing, Surau, Pump Room, Bridge) | Allowance only; provisional | /m² CFA | 9.25 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| **Inside Boundary Total** | | /m² CFA | **115.18** | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### Outside Boundary

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sewerage Pumping Station (5,000 PE) | New network SPS required by IWK/authority | item | 1,000,000 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Off-site Roads & Hardstandings | Access road upgrades required by MPSJ/authority | /m² CFA | 37.95 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Off-site Stormwater & Soil Drainage | JPS external drainage contribution | /m² CFA | 31.51 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| IWSS Contribution (Southern Corridor) | Integrated Water Supply Scheme authority charge | item | 1,500,000 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| **Outside Boundary Total** | | /m² CFA | **82.41** | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### Combined External Works

| | MYR | % of Total Building Cost | /m² CFA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inside Boundary | 22,246,953 | 8.94% | 115.18 |
| Outside Boundary | 15,917,100 | 6.39% | 82.41 |
| **Total External Works** | **38,164,053** | **15.33%** | **197.59** |

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (JK506), September 2024. Classified as **estimate**.
- External works represent **15.33% of total building cost** — notably high. The outside-boundary works (6.39%) include significant infrastructure contributions expected of large suburban developments in Selangor (road upgrades, SPS, IWSS). These are site-specific and should not be assumed for urban infill projects.
- Landscape is fully provisional and subject to landscape architect design — actual cost could vary ±30–50%.
- Retaining walls (MYR 25.52/m² CFA) suggest meaningful site topography at Semenyih — typical for foothills area south of KL.
- For urban KL projects, outside-boundary contributions are often lower (existing infrastructure), but authority charges/levies (Prasarana, Jabatan Pengairan, TNB capacity charges) can offset this.
- Escalate from 2024 base at ~5–8% p.a.

---

---

## Landed Housing Infrastructure Benchmarks

> Data from [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] — Raintree Forest Sg Long, Project 4236, dated Oct 2025. Rates are **RM per sqft of house GFA** (not infrastructure area). Infrastructure cost only — excludes superstructure/building cost.

### Total Infrastructure Cost (WWSB + WOSB) by Terrain & Scale

| Phase | Terrain | Units | RM/sqft GFA | RM/unit | RM/acre |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 5A&5B | Hilly (severe) | 58 | **54.12** | 211,424 | 1,109,734 |
| Phase 1 | Hilly ~30m | 106 | **42.40** | 169,344 | 670,796 |
| Phase 1+2 combined | Hilly ~30m | 262 | **38.07** | 144,560 | 769,966 |
| Phase 2 | Hilly ~30m | 156 | **31.49** | 115,332 | 802,129 |
| MBW | — | 222 | **31.59** | 95,673 | 1,536,861 |
| MBW Phase 1&2 | — | 324 | **16.92** | 50,394 | 881,153 |
| Main (township ref) | Flat | 2,091 | **17.50** | 30,216 | 582,533 |

### By Item (RM/sqft GFA) — Hilly vs Flat Comparison

| Item | Hilly Ph 1 | Hilly Ph 2 | Flat (Main) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earthworks | 1.26 | 6.56 | 1.27 | Ph 2 higher — steeper cut & fill |
| Retaining Walls | 2.23 | 2.32 | 0.85 | ~2.6× higher in hilly terrain |
| Roads, Kerbs & Footpath | 11.14 | 6.84 | 7.03 | Roads dominant item |
| Surface Water Drainage | 13.06 | 8.74 | 3.39 | **3–4× higher on hilly** — OSD, large pipes |
| Foul Drainage | 5.03 | 3.70 | 1.28 | Gravity drainage harder uphill |
| Sewerage Treatment Plant | 5.15 | — | — | Self-contained STP for isolated hilly site |
| Water Reticulation | 3.40 | 2.96 | 1.55 | — |
| Fencing & Gate | 1.13 | 0.36 | — | — |
| **Total WWSB** | **42.40** | **31.49** | **16.15** | |

### Terrain Premium Rule of Thumb

| Condition | Infrastructure RM/sqft GFA | vs. Flat Premium |
|---|---|---|
| Flat, large township (>1,000 units) | 17–18 | Baseline |
| Hilly (~30m), large phase (>150u) | 31–38 | +80–120% |
| Hilly (~30m), small phase (<120u) | 42–54 | +140–210% |
| Severe slope / small phase (<60u) | 50–60+ | +200%+ |

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024
- [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] — Project 4236, Infrastructure Cost Comparison, Oct 2025

notes.title: internal-finishes
notes.content:
# Internal Finishes — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Covers Element 3A, 3B, 3C in Malaysian QS practice:
- **3A Internal Wall Finishes:** Plaster, skim coat, paint; ceramic/homogeneous tile for wet areas; feature wall finishes in common areas
- **3B Internal Floor Finishes:** Ceramic/homogeneous tiles, timber strip, vinyl, carpet; screed bed; grouting
- **3C Internal Ceiling Finishes:** Skim coat & paint to soffit; plasterboard or proprietary ceiling in common areas; cornices

These are specification-sensitive. Mass market residential typically uses ceramic tile to wet areas, homogeneous tile to living/dining, and painted plaster to bedrooms. Luxury projects use full-floor marble/engineered stone and feature ceilings throughout.

---

## Rate Table

> Data from one project (BRDB Semenyih, Sep 2024 estimate). All rates are elemental cost per m² GFA.

### 3A Internal Wall Finishes

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Wall Finishes — Residential Tower (FMH mass market) | Plaster + paint; ceramic tile to bathrooms & kitchen | /m² GFA | 105 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Wall Finishes — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Plaster + paint; basic tile spec | /m² GFA | 89 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Wall Finishes — Podium / Carpark | Minimal — paint only, car park is bare concrete | /m² GFA | 9–11 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### 3B Internal Floor Finishes

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Floor Finishes — Residential Tower (FMH mass market) | Homogeneous tile to living/dining; ceramic tile to wet areas; screed bed | /m² GFA | 104–105 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Floor Finishes — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Ceramic tile throughout; basic screed | /m² GFA | 71 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Floor Finishes — Podium / Carpark | Screed only; epoxy paint to carpark deck | /m² GFA | 13–15 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Floor Finishes — Facilities Floor | Higher spec; terrazzo/homogeneous/marble to common areas | /m² GFA | 86 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### 3C Internal Ceiling Finishes

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Ceiling Finishes — Residential Tower (FMH) | Skim & paint to soffit; plasterboard ceiling to common corridors | /m² GFA | 27 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Internal Ceiling Finishes — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Basic skim & paint | /m² GFA | 14 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (JK506), September 2024. Classified as **estimate**.
- FMH vs RSKU differential: FMH floor finishes (MYR 104/m² GFA) are ~47% higher than RSKU (MYR 71/m² GFA), reflecting homogeneous vs ceramic tile and better common area finishes.
- The "per m² GFA" rate dilutes the true tile/paint cost because GFA includes structural elements. Actual area of tiles applied is ~60–70% of GFA for a typical residential tower.
- For mid-range high-rise in KL (e.g. Bangsar, Mont Kiara), expect internal finishes 40–80% higher per m² GFA due to marble, engineered timber, and feature finishes.
- For luxury (KLCC corridor), internal finishes can reach MYR 400–600/m² GFA.
- Escalate from 2024 base at ~5–8% p.a.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024

notes.title: landed-house-building-cost

frontmatter.unit: sqft NFA (Net Floor Area); for landed houses NFA ≈ GFA (no common-area floor area)

notes.content:
# Landed House Building Cost — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Building superstructure cost for Malaysian landed residential developments, expressed as **MYR per sqft NFA**. For landed houses (Semi D, Zero Lot, Villa), NFA = GFA — there is no shared common floor area, so built-up area of the unit is directly comparable.

RSKU (Rumah Selangorku) affordable units may be terraced or low-rise walk-up — NFA is the unit floor area; any common corridor or stairwell is excluded from NFA.

Scope covered: building superstructure only — structural frame, brickwork, finishes, doors/windows, M&E within unit, roof. Does **not** include: infrastructure (roads, drainage, sewerage), M&E infrastructure (TNB substation, water reservoir), landscape, site clearance, or preliminaries. Those are covered in [[construction-costs/rates/external-works]].

Rates are on a **CCE (Concept Cost Estimate)** basis unless stated — tender/contract outturn may vary.

---

## Rate Table

> Data from one project (Raintree Forest Sg Long, Nov 2024 CCE). Three revisions shown: DCS 19B (baseline), CCE No 2, CCE No 2A (most recent). CCE No 2A is the recommended benchmark.

### By Unit Type — MYR/sqft NFA

| Unit Type | DCS 19B | CCE No 2 | CCE No 2A | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Semi D | 194.00 | 204.93 | **208.72** | 2024-Q4 | [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] |
| Zero Lot | 196.00 | 193.68 | **199.14** | 2024-Q4 | [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] |
| Villa | 202.00 | 196.85 | **199.61** | 2024-Q4 | [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] |
| RSKU (affordable) | 199.01 | 224.45 | **224.45** | 2024-Q4 | [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] |
| Clubhouse | 300.00 | 203.48 | **203.48** | 2024-Q4 | [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] |

### NFA by Unit Type (CCE No 2A basis)

| Unit Type | NFA (sqft) | Total Building Cost (MYR) | Cost/sqft NFA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semi D | 829,560 | 173,145,000 | 208.72 |
| Zero Lot | 107,828 | 21,473,000 | 199.14 |
| Villa | 64,600 | 12,895,000 | 199.61 |
| RSKU (affordable) | 216,150 | 48,514,000 | 224.45 |
| Clubhouse | 38,569 | 7,848,000 | 203.48 |
| **Total** | **1,256,707** | **263,875,000** | **209.97 (blended)** |

---

## Building Cost vs Total Development Cost (CCE No 2A)

| Component | MYR | % of Sub-Total |
|---|---|---|
| Building (all unit types + clubhouse) | 263,875,000 | 78.5% |
| Infrastructure within site | 41,374,000 | 12.3% |
| Infrastructure outside site | 4,113,000 | 1.2% |
| M&E Infrastructure | 8,593,000 | 2.6% |
| Landscape | 7,062,000 | 2.1% |
| Other provisions | 11,285,000 | 3.4% |
| **Sub-Total** | **336,302,000** | **100%** |
| Prelims (7.0%) | 23,541,000 | — |
| Contingency (3.0%) | 10,090,000 | — |
| **Grand Total** | **369,933,000** | — |

> All-in development cost (excl. land, professional fees, S&M, financing): **MYR 369.9M** for ~1.26M sqft NFA = **MYR 294/sqft NFA blended all-in**.

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from Raintree Forest Sg Long internal CCE report (Project 4236), dated 26 Nov 2024. Classified as **estimate** (CCE stage — not tender or contract award).
- Location: Sungai Long, Kajang, Selangor (suburban KL, ~30 km south-east of city centre). Hilly terrain.
- DCS 19B = Design Cost Schedule 19 revision B (the project baseline). CCE No 2 and CCE No 2A are later revised estimates.
- **RSKU cost jump** (+12.8%: DCS MYR 199 → CCE2/2A MYR 224): likely reflects revised spec or unit layout change between DCS and CCE stage.
- **Clubhouse scope change**: DCS 10,000 sqft → CCE2/2A 38,569 sqft (nearly 4×). DCS rate of MYR 300/sqft reflected a simpler/smaller facility; CCE2/2A expanded programme brings cost to MYR 203/sqft.
- **Soil theft issue**: DCS earthworks were understated (MYR 1.34M) because soil was removed from site without authorisation before the DCS was prepared. CCE2/2A adds MYR 1.74M for soil replacement and slope strengthening due to erosion.
- **Spec note**: Raintree Forest is a mid-market hilly landed development in an established Kajang township. These rates reflect a mid-range spec — not luxury GnG, not affordable mass market. Semi D at MYR 208/sqft is broadly consistent with Malaysian mid-market landed build costs for 2024–2025.
- **Terrain note**: Hilly site may inflate structural costs slightly (additional reinforcement, slope-adjacent foundations) — flat-site landed builds may come in 3–8% lower.
- Escalate from 2024-Q4 base at ~5–8% p.a.
- For comparison: BRDB Semenyih high-rise (Parcel 1 FMH) costs MYR 130.71/sqft GFA — high-rise is cheaper per sqft GFA than landed per sqft NFA because of the floor area multiplier (GFA of a 30-storey tower spread across many floors vs 1–3 storey landed house).

---

## Context: Malaysia Landed Building Cost Benchmarks (2024–2025 indicative)

| Tier | Type | MYR/sqft NFA (estimate) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Affordable | Single-storey terrace / RSKU | 120–160 | Basic spec, minimal finishes |
| Mass market | Double-storey terrace | 160–200 | Standard spec |
| Mid-range | Semi D / Zero Lot | **195–215** | ← Raintree Forest falls here |
| Upper mid | Larger Semi D / Cluster | 220–280 | Better finishes, larger floor plate |
| Luxury landed | Bungalow / GnG | 350–600+ | High spec, branded fittings |

> ⚠️ These indicative ranges are from training knowledge — not from ingested project data. Treat as directional only until more projects are ingested.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] — Project 4236, CCE No 2A, dated 26 Nov 2024
- [[sources/2026-04-29-raintree-forest-cost-comparison-4236]]

notes.title: mep

frontmatter.unit: m² CFA (M&E reported at project total level in this source)

notes.content:
# Mechanical & Electrical (M&E) Services — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Covers Element 6 in Malaysian QS practice. In residential high-rise, M&E typically includes:
- **Plumbing & Sanitary:** Water supply, hot water, sanitary drainage, sewerage
- **ACMV:** Split unit air-conditioning (for residential), corridor ventilation, pressurisation, car park ventilation
- **Electrical:** HV/LV switchgear, main distribution, unit distribution boards, wiring, lighting, power points, earthing
- **Fire Protection:** Sprinkler, hose reel, fire alarm, BOMBA requirements
- **Lifts & Escalators:** Passenger lifts, service lifts, fireman's lift
- **ELV Systems:** CCTV, access control, intercom, PA system, structured cabling
- **BAS/BMS:** Building Automation System (for common area services)

M&E is typically contracted as a package to a specialist M&E contractor (NSC or domestic sub-contractor). In Malaysian QS practice, a 2% profits & attendance on M&E is added to the main contractor's sum.

---

## Rate Table

> ⚠️ M&E in this source is reported as a single project-level lump sum — not broken down by sub-trade. The rate below is a blended M&E cost per m² CFA. Sub-trade breakdown will be added when a detailed M&E schedule is available.

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M&E Works — Mixed Residential (FMH + RSKU, Semenyih) | Includes plumbing, ACMV, electrical, fire, lifts, ELV. Standard high-rise residential spec. Suburban Selangor. | /m² CFA | 167.87 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Profits & Attendance on M&E (2%) | Main contractor's uplift on NSC M&E works | /m² CFA | 3.36 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| **M&E Total (incl. P&A)** | | /m² CFA | **171.23** | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

**By parcel (CFA basis):**

| Parcel | M&E Total (MYR) | CFA (m²) | Rate (MYR/m² CFA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parcel 1 – FMH | 25,079,760 | 148,182 | 169.25 |
| Parcel 2 – RSKU | 7,993,740 | 44,970 | 177.76 |
| **Total** | **33,073,500** | **193,152** | **171.23** |

> The RSKU M&E rate (MYR 178/m² CFA) is marginally higher than FMH (MYR 169/m² CFA), likely due to the smaller scale of Parcel 2 reducing economies of scale in procurement.

**M&E as % of total building cost:** 13.29%

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (JK506), September 2024. Classified as **estimate**.
- M&E at MYR 168/m² CFA for standard mass market residential is consistent with Malaysian industry norms. Expect:
  - Low-cost/affordable: MYR 80–120/m² CFA
  - Mass market high-rise: MYR 130–180/m² CFA
  - Mid-range / luxury: MYR 200–350/m² CFA
  - Ultra-luxury: MYR 400–600+/m² CFA
- Sub-trade breakdown for this project is not available in this report. Key value drivers in residential M&E are lifts (10–20% of M&E cost) and ACMV (20–30% of M&E).
- Profits & attendance at 2% is standard Malaysian QS practice for M&E sub-contracts.
- Escalate from 2024 base at ~5–8% p.a.; M&E also sensitive to copper and aluminium commodity prices.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024

notes.title: piling-foundation

frontmatter.unit: m² GFA (elemental cost basis; unit pile rates in MYR/m pending future BOQ upload)

notes.content:
# Piling & Foundation — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Covers all substructure work from piling through to ground-floor slab. In Malaysian QS practice this element (Element 1) typically includes:
- **1A Piling & Pile Caps:** Bored piles, precast driven piles, or micropiles; pile caps; ground beams
- **1B Work Below Lowest Floor Finish:** Ground floor slab, hardcore fill, waterproofing below slab, lift pit

Piling cost is driven primarily by pile type, diameter, depth, and ground conditions. In Selangor suburban sites (e.g. Semenyih), granite bedrock is typically shallower than KL city centre, which can reduce bored pile depths but increases drilling difficulty.

---

## Rate Table

> ⚠️ Data currently from one project only (BRDB Semenyih, Sep 2024 estimate). Rates are **elemental cost per m² GFA** — not unit pile rates (MYR/linear m). Unit pile rates will be added when detailed BOQ is uploaded.

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piling & Pile Caps — Residential Tower (mass market) | FMH towers, Selangor suburban, basis: PTE No. 4 | /m² GFA | 103–104 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Piling & Pile Caps — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | State affordable housing spec, lighter loads | /m² GFA | 75–80 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Piling & Pile Caps — Podium Carpark (FMH, 5 levels) | Lower load than tower; carpark slab loads | /m² GFA | 91 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Work Below Lowest Floor Finish — Residential Tower | Includes ground slab, hardcore, below-slab WP | /m² GFA | 8–9 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Work Below Lowest Floor Finish — Podium Carpark | Higher allowance due to basement-level slab | /m² GFA | 60 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

**Substructure element total (piling + below-FF) by component:**

| Building Component | Cost/m² GFA (MYR) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| FMH Podium Carpark | 151 | High work-below-FF due to podium slab arrangement |
| FMH Tower A | 103 | Mid-rise residential, suburban Selangor |
| FMH Tower B | 105 | Same spec as Tower A |
| RSKU Podium | 80 | Lighter carpark |
| RSKU Tower | 80 | Affordable spec, lower loads |

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (JK506), September 2024. Classified as **estimate** (not tender or final account).
- Piling quantities and unit rates (MYR per linear metre of pile) are referenced to a separate Piling Tender Estimate (PTE No. 4) not included in this report.
- Site: Semenyih, Selangor — suburban, granite geology typical of this corridor.
- Rates should be escalated forward from 2024 if used for 2025–2026 projects (apply ~5–8% p.a. construction cost escalation for Malaysia).
- For KL city centre or elevated sites (limestone/soft ground), expect piling costs to be 20–40% higher per m GFA due to deeper piles or specialist pile types.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024

notes.title: preliminaries

frontmatter.unit: % of Group Elemental Cost; MYR/m² CFA (derived)

notes.content:
# Preliminaries — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Preliminaries (Element 8 in Malaysian QS) cover the main contractor's general costs of running the site, not attributable to any specific trade. Typically includes:
- Site establishment (site office, welfare facilities, hoarding, fencing)
- Supervision (project manager, site engineers, QS, safety officer)
- Tower cranes and hoisting equipment
- Temporary works (falsework, formwork not in trade rates, temporary power/water)
- Insurance (CAR, public liability, workmen's compensation)
- Performance bond (usually 5% of contract sum)
- Testing and commissioning
- As-built drawings, O&M manuals
- Cleaning and making good on completion

In Malaysian practice, Preliminaries are commonly expressed as a **percentage of the Group Elemental Cost (pre-prelims)** and tendered as a lump sum, then converted to a %. Market range is typically **6–10%** for high-rise residential; complex or remote sites attract higher prelims.

---

## Rate Table

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preliminaries — Mixed Residential (FMH + RSKU) | Standard suburban site; 2 parcels | % of elemental cost | 7.0% | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — FMH Podium Carpark | | /m² GFA | 39 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — FMH Tower A | | /m² GFA | 76 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — FMH Tower B | | /m² GFA | 77 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — RSKU Podium | | /m² GFA | 34 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — RSKU Tower | | /m² GFA | 61 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Preliminaries — Project Total | MYR 16,286,554 / CFA 193,152 m² | /m² CFA | 84.32 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

**Contingencies:** 0% applied (not standard; indicates estimate is considered sufficiently robust).

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- 7% is at the low-to-mid end of the Malaysian market range for high-rise residential (6–10%). This likely reflects:
  - Suburban site (lower traffic management and hoarding complexity vs. urban KL)
  - Estimate stage — prelims % may increase at tender
  - Relatively straightforward twin-tower programme
- For comparison: KL city centre projects with tight urban sites, restricted working hours, and complex hoarding often attract 9–12% prelims.
- Contingencies at 0% is unusual — typically 3–5% is included at estimate stage. The report note states the estimate already incorporates sufficient allowance.
- Prelims % should be reviewed at tender stage; actual tendered prelims often deviate from estimate.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024

notes.title: structural
notes.content:
# Structural — Rate Reference

## Category Overview

Covers Element 2 (Superstructure) in Malaysian QS practice, specifically the structural frame and floor plates:
- **2A Frame:** RC columns, beams, shear walls, structural steel if applicable
- **2B Upper Floor:** RC flat slab, PT slab, suspended slabs at all levels above ground
- **2C Roof:** Roof structure, deck, waterproof membrane, drainage, cladding
- **2D Stairs:** Stair RC structure, finishes, balustrades & handrails

In Malaysian high-rise residential, the typical structural system is RC flat plate / post-tensioned slab with RC shear walls. Podium carparks are commonly flat slab or PT slab at 200–250mm thickness.

---

## Rate Table

> Data from one project (BRDB Semenyih, Sep 2024 estimate). All rates are elemental cost per m² GFA.

### Frame (RC columns, beams, shear walls)

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RC Frame — Residential Tower (mass market) | ~20–30 storeys, Selangor suburban | /m² GFA | 303–306 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| RC Frame — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Lighter spec, lower rise | /m² GFA | 226 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| RC Frame — Podium Carpark, FMH (5 levels) | Lower frame % vs tower; PT slab typical | /m² GFA | 154 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| RC Frame — Podium Carpark, RSKU (6 levels) | Similar to FMH podium | /m² GFA | 195 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### Upper Floor (Suspended Slabs)

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upper Floor Slab — Residential Tower (FMH) | RC/PT flat slab typical | /m² GFA | 133–135 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Upper Floor Slab — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Lighter spec | /m² GFA | 104 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Upper Floor Slab — Podium Carpark (FMH) | PT slab, carpark loading | /m² GFA | 140 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Upper Floor Slab — Facilities Floor | Higher finish/loading | /m² GFA | 519 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### Roof

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Roof — Residential Tower (FMH) | Roof structure + coverings + drainage | /m² GFA | 35–37 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |
| Roof — Affordable Tower (RSKU) | Simpler spec | /m² GFA | 28 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

### Stairs

| Item | Spec / Notes | Unit | Rate (MYR) | Date | Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stairs (structure + finishes + balustrade) — Tower | RC stairs, standard fire stair spec | /m² GFA | 13 | 2024-Q3 | [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] |

---

## Notes & Assumptions

- All rates from JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b (JK506), September 2024. Classified as **estimate**.
- Frame costs are heavily influenced by structural system: PT slab systems are more efficient per m² GFA than conventional beam-and-slab but require specialist PT contractor (NSC typically).
- RSKU (affordable) tower frame rate (MYR 226/m²) is ~25% lower than FMH towers (MYR 303–306/m²), reflecting fewer floors and simpler structural arrangement.
- Podium carpark frame (MYR 154–195/m²) is ~50% of residential tower frame — expected, as carpark has larger column grid, no shear walls.
- Escalate from 2024 base at ~5–8% p.a. for use in 2025–2026 projects.
- KL city centre high-rise (40–50 storeys) expect frame costs 30–50% higher due to structural system complexity and wind/seismic demands.

---

## Sources

- [[construction-costs/projects/brdb-semenyih]] — JKQS Cost Estimate Report No. 2b, Sep 2024

notes.title: Ampang
notes.content:
# Ampang

## Overview

Ampang straddles the KL–Selangor border and covers a broad geography: the inner Ampang corridor (adjacent to the Embassy Row and U-Thant belt) is part of KL, while the outer reaches (Ulu Kelang, Taman Permata, Taman Ulu Kelang) fall under Ampang Jaya Municipal Council (MPAJ) in Selangor. The area is characterised by its hilly, green topography — the southern edge of the Titiwangsa Range runs through here — making it one of the most naturally wooded residential corridors in the Greater KL area.

**Key submarkets:**
- **Inner Ampang / Jalan Ampang corridor:** Embassy-belt adjacent; KL-side; older condos and expatriate-grade villas; connected to KLCC via Jalan Ampang
- **Ampang Hilir:** Premium residential; low-density; established expat community; close to international schools
- **Taman Permata / Ulu Kelang / Taman Ulu Kelang:** MPAJ jurisdiction; mid-market; hilly topography; access via MRR2, AKLEH, DUKE
- **Pandan Jaya / Taman Pandan:** Older residential; LRT Ampang Line connectivity
- **Wangsa Maju–Ampang corridor:** Growth area per KLSP2040

**Connectivity:**
- MRR2 (Middle Ring Road 2): main arterial connecting Ampang to Cheras, Kepong, DUKE, AKLEH
- LRT Ampang Line: serves Ampang Jaya / Pandan corridors (Dato Keramat, Ampang Park, Cempaka, Sri Petaling junction)
- MRT2 Ampang Park station: new connectivity into city via Putrajaya Line

---

## Residential Market

- **Inner Ampang / Ampang Hilir:** RM 700–1,200 PSF; villas and low-rise condos; expat rental market; freehold parcels; very limited new supply
- **Ulu Kelang / Taman Permata:** RM 400–700 PSF; mid-range condos and landed; predominantly Selangor (MPAJ); some freehold pockets
- **Pandan corridor:** RM 350–550 PSF; older stock; LRT connected; improving with urban renewal

**Key dynamic:** Ampang's natural topography is its differentiator. The Titiwangsa mountain range creates a permanent greenbelt, constraining developable land and supporting long-term values for appropriately sited projects. The area benefits from proximity to international schools (Alice Smith, Sayfol, Fairview) and embassies, sustaining expat rental demand in the inner belt.

### Featured Project (2026): Botanik Point
- **Developer:** Urban Hallmark Properties Sdn Bhd
- **Location:** Taman Ulu Kelang, Ampang
- **Tenure:** Freehold, Strata
- **Total units:** 46 (4 semi-D >5,500 sqft with private lifts; 42 Townvillas)
- **Concept:** Boutique low-density development built around a preserved nature reserve (~30% of site)
- **Sustainability:** GreenRE Platinum (Provisional) certified; solar energy, rainwater harvesting, EV charging
- **Design features:** Passive ventilation, skylights, airwells, expansive glass façades
- **Connectivity:** 400m to MRR2; near DUKE and AKLEH; Melawati Mall, KL East Mall, international schools within short drive
- **Launch:** June 2026 (expected); Completion: 36 months from launch
- **Marketing agent:** Henry Butcher Malaysia (exclusive)
- **PSF:** Not publicly disclosed
- *Note: Developer deliberately capped at 46 units despite approval for up to 108 — premium low-density strategy*

---

## Commercial Market

Ampang is predominantly residential. The Ampang Point shopping centre and Jalan Ampang corridor provide retail services. No significant Grade A office presence.

---

## Industrial Market

Not applicable — Ampang is residential/mixed-use corridor.

---

## Key Developments

- **Botanik Point** (Urban Hallmark) — June 2026 launch; boutique freehold with nature reserve; see above
- **KL East Mall** (ARA Asset Management / UEM Sunrise) — established retail anchor for Ulu Kelang; drives amenity value for surrounding residential

---

## Yield Snapshot

| Asset Class | Gross Yield Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Residential condo (inner Ampang) | 4–6% | Expat rental belt; good demand from embassy community |
| Residential landed / villa | 3–4.5% | Capital appreciation play; limited stock |
| Mid-range condo (Ulu Kelang / Pandan) | 4–6% | More accessible entry; higher yield on lower capital value |

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- Hillside development risk — some sites in Ulu Kelang are on steep terrain; slope stability and drainage are relevant due diligence items
- Traffic congestion on MRR2 and Jalan Ampang corridors during peak hours
- MPAJ (Selangor) vs DBKL (KL) split — planning standards and assessment rates differ; always verify jurisdiction
- Expat rental demand correlated with global corporate relocation appetite — sensitive to Malaysia's relative attractiveness vs Singapore
- Supply competition from neighbouring U-Thant/Embassy Row belt (more prestigious, lower density)

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]

notes.title: Bangsar / Bangsar South
notes.content:
# Bangsar / Bangsar South

## Overview

Bangsar is one of KL's most established premium residential and F&B suburbs, ~3 km from KL city centre. Bangsar South (formerly Kampung Kerinchi) is the adjacent commercial/mixed-use district transformed by SP Setia and others into a high-rise business-and-residential district, anchored by Telekom Malaysia's HQ and connecting to Universiti LRT and Kerinchi LRT. Both are designated **Secondary Growth Centres** under KLDCP 2024 Vol 2.

---

## Residential Market

**Bangsar (established residential):**
- Price range: **RM 800–1,500 PSF** for condos; landed houses command significant premiums (RM 2M–8M+)
- Character: Low-rise shophouses, boutique condos, landed bungalows/semi-Ds — limited new supply
- Strong rental demand from expatriates, professionals, and KL Sentral commuters
- Bangsar LRT station (Sri Petaling line) provides transit connectivity
- Zone: Mix of R1 (landed), C/MC (commercial), EH (established residential) — height-controlled

**Bangsar South:**
- Price range: RM 600–900 PSF for high-rise condos
- Business district character; offices + high-rise residences
- Connected to Universiti LRT and Kerinchi LRT
- Developers: SP Setia (Bangsar South / Setia Federal Hill), KL Eco City (IGB + WCT)

---

## Commercial

- Bangsar: Jalan Maarof, Telawi — prime F&B and boutique retail strip; one of KL's strongest F&B corridors
- Bangsar South: Telekom Malaysia HQ campus; KL Eco City (office + retail + serviced residences); Angkasapuri (RTM) site targeted for redevelopment
- **KLDCP Vol 2 target:** Green corridor connecting Telekom Malaysia Tower and Angkasapuri; redevelopment of Taman Sri Angkasa Flats planned

---

## Key Planning Facts (from KLDCP 2024)

- **Secondary Growth Centre** designation (Vol 2) — medium-high development intensity
- Connectivity: Improve links to KL Sentral, Setia Federal Hill, Bangsar 61, KL Eco City, Mid Valley
- LRT stations: Bangsar, Universiti, Kerinchi — TPZ/TIZ applies
- **Multi-choice residential** to increase urban population density (KLSP2040 goal)

---

## Yield Snapshot

| Segment | Gross Yield | Notes |
|---------|------------|-------|
| Bangsar condo | 3.5–5% | Strong rental demand; limited supply |
| Bangsar South high-rise | 4–6% | Higher yield on lower entry vs Bangsar proper |
| Bangsar shophouse / retail | 4–5% | F&B strip premium; good occupancy |

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- Bangsar village / low-rise character is heritage-sensitive; height restrictions apply
- Bangsar South office market faces KL-wide office vacancy headwind
- Traffic congestion on Jalan Bangsar / Jalan Maarof remains severe

---

## 2025 Launch PSF Data

**1 project** launched in Bangsar in 2025 (highrise): **MYR 1,200–1,300 psf**. This confirms Bangsar's position as a premium KL submarket second only to KLCC in PSF terms. Supply is tight — only 1 launch across all of 2025.

*Source: [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]*

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-klsp2040]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-kldcp-v2-promoting-city-development]]
- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]

notes.title: Damansara Heights (Bukit Damansara)
notes.content:
## Overview

Damansara Heights (also known as Bukit Damansara) is one of Kuala Lumpur's most affluent residential enclaves, situated between [[entities/areas/mont-kiara|Mont Kiara]], TTDI, [[entities/areas/bangsar|Bangsar]], and [[entities/areas/klcc-bukit-bintang|KLCC]]. The area is characterised by low-density landed housing interspersed with premium high-rise developments, flanked by two large green buffers (Taman Duta and Bukit Kiara hills). The median household income in the study boundary is RM 24,000/month — more than double the KL district average of RM 10,272. The population is predominantly Chinese (42%, above KL average) with a high proportion of non-Malaysians (14%).

The nearest MRT station is Semantan (MRT Sungai Buloh–Kajang Line). The incoming MRT Circle Line will connect the area to Mont Kiara via Bukit Kiara Selatan station.

Key amenities include Pantai Hospital, KPJ Damansara Specialist Hospital, HELP University, Perdana University, and international schools (Stella Maris, Sri Cempaka within 2 km). Travel time to Bangsar is 15 min, Mont Kiara 18 min, and Bukit Bintang 22 min (8am outbound).

Population (2020 Census): 324,647 for the study boundary. Base case 2030 projection: 364,152 (CAGR 1.15%), indicating weak population growth.

## Residential Market

Damansara Heights commands a significant price premium over neighbouring submarkets for large highrise units (≥1,500 sf):

**Subsale Market (2021–2024 JPPH data):**
- Median subsale price (all highrise): RM 1.1 million
- Median subsale price (≥1,500 sf): RM 1.7 million; 19% transacted above RM 2M
- Median subsale price (≥2,000 sf): RM 2.2 million; 29% above RM 3M
- Annual subsale volume: ~1,300 highrise units; RM 1.7 billion/year
- Bukit Damansara PSF: close to double Mont Kiara and 50% higher than KLCC for ≥1,500 sf units under 20 years old

**New Launch Market (2021–2025):**
- Median new sale price (all): RM 1.3 million (20% premium over subsale)
- Median new sale price (≥1,500 sf): RM 1.9 million; 41% above RM 2M
- Bukit Damansara new launch PSF (≥1,500 sf): RM 1,400–1,700 PSF
- KLCC new launch PSF (≥1,500 sf): RM 2,300 PSF
- Mont Kiara new launch PSF (≥1,500 sf): under RM 1,000 PSF
- Average RM 3.1 billion of private highrise sold annually (2021–2025)

**Price Trends:**
- XL-sized (≥2,000 sf) units have the highest appreciation since 2020
- Units <10 years old carry a 10% premium over 10–15 year old units
- Units <20 years old carry a 20% premium over 20+ year old units

**Supply & Demand (2025–2028):**
- Annual household formation (base case): 2,169 families
- Estimated additional demand for highrise: 1,518 units/year (70% of households)
- Average annual highrise supply: 5,294 units — supply significantly outpaces organic demand
- Total incoming residential: 29,414 units (24% increase); 99% highrise
- With APDL: 18,549 units; with DO only: 8,629 units
- Cancelled projects providing relief: Taman Rimba Kiara, Malola Garden Bukit Nanas

**Absorption (≥1,500 sf, 2021–2024):**
- Total annual absorption: 1,147 units
- New sales: 517/589 available = 87% take-up
- By price band: RM 1–2M (581/yr), RM 2–3M (257/yr), RM 3–5M (113/yr), >RM 5M (63/yr)

## Commercial Market

### Retail

The study boundary has a mature retail market with 12+ shopping malls, but is already stressed at 7.4 sf per capita (vs Singapore 5.8 sf). An additional 4.6 million sf of retail NLA is in the pipeline.

Key malls near Damansara Heights:
- **Pavilion Damansara Heights (Phase 1):** 533,361 sf NLA, completed 2023, only 53% NLA occupied, 121,650 monthly footfall — still ramping up
- **Plaza Damansara Shopoffice:** 527,850 monthly footfall, attracts 34% T5 visitors
- **DC Mall:** 170,000 sf NLA, 41% NLA occupancy — structurally underperforming
- **Plaza Batai:** 184,350 monthly footfall, highest T5 visitor share (37%)

Bukit Damansara and Bangsar malls attract higher-income visitors (T10/T5) compared to Mont Kiara malls, but with lower footfall volumes. The catchment extends 6–9 km for Bukit Damansara retail vs 3–6 km for Mont Kiara.

Retail performance is highly sensitive to age, with peak footfall at 10–15 years post-opening. Eateries (especially non-halal) and Health & Beauty dominate successful tenant mixes.

### Strata Offices

- Pavilion Bukit Damansara Heights towers: RM 7.50 PSF asking rent (highest in study area)
- Most strata offices: under RM 5.00 PSF due to oversupply
- Newer (post-2015) strata offices: 20% rental premium but only ~4.5% yield vs ~6% for older units
- Office yields match 6% only if purchase price stays below RM 850 PSF
- West zone asking price: RM 708 PSF; East zone: RM 850 PSF
- Rental inversely correlated with distance to nearest mall (RM 1 PSF premium per 1.5 km closer)

138 Bursa-listed companies are headquartered in the study boundary, holding 45.21% of total KLSE market capitalisation (RM 828 billion). Top sectors: Financial Services (RM 314B), Utilities (RM 182B).

## Key Developments

### Active/Incoming Residential Projects

- **[[entities/developers/e-and-o|The Peak Villa]]** (E&O) — 54 units, 3,455–4,500 sf, FH, RM 5.5M+ / RM 1,592 PSF, 74% sold — ultra-luxury low-density
- **The Cedar** (Satin Magic) — 70 units, 2,782–16,248 sf, FH, RM 4.45M+ / RM 1,599 PSF, 73% sold
- **111 Menerung** — visible in RM 2–3M and >RM 5M price maps
- **Windsor @ Pavilion D'Sara Heights** — large luxury units (L-size offering)
- **Pavilion Damansara Heights (Phase 1)** — retail + residential mixed-use, completed 2023

### Pipeline (DO only, not yet APDL)
- Steady Paramount Senior Living Apartments (26 units)
- Axventure Condo (42 units, XL-size)
- AikBee Development Condo (15 units)
- SDB Damansara Condo (60 units) — *unlikely to proceed* due to 3-storey gazette vs 7-storey developer request
- Eden @ Taman Duta (146 units)

## Yield Snapshot

| Asset Class | Gross Yield Range | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Residential (highrise, ≥1,500 sf) | 2.5–4.0% | Luxury segment; low yield but strong capital appreciation |
| Strata Office (post-2015) | ~4.5% | At current asking prices |
| Strata Office (pre-2015) | ~6.0% | If purchased below RM 850 PSF |
| Retail (mall NLA) | Varies | Highly dependent on mall age and occupancy |

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Oversupply risk:** 29,414 incoming units vs ~1,518 annual organic demand is a 19:1 ratio — though absorption from upgraders and investors supplements organic demand
- **Retail saturation:** 18.3 million sf of retail within 5 km by 2029; Pavilion D'Sara Heights Phase 1 only at 53% occupancy after 2 years
- **Strata office oversupply:** 1.8 million sf incoming NLA; most existing stock under RM 5 PSF rental
- **MRT Circle Line timing:** Construction progress will affect connectivity and property premiums
- **SDB Damansara uncertainty:** Developer-government impasse on building height
- **Slow population growth:** CAGR 1.15% (base case) — demand growth is tepid

## Sources

- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-residential]]
- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-retail-offices]]

notes.title: Johor / Johor Bahru
notes.content:
# Johor / Johor Bahru

## Overview

Johor Bahru is Malaysia's second city and one of the most tracked property markets due to its proximity to Singapore. The market is deeply influenced by the MYR/SGD exchange rate — Singaporeans (and Singapore PRs/EPHolders) have historically been significant buyers. JB has experienced repeated boom-bust cycles tied to cross-border sentiment. The Iskandar Malaysia economic corridor was launched in 2006 as a development masterplan covering ~2,217 sq km south of JB.

**Key submarkets:**
- **JB City / Bukit Chagar:** CBD, older stock, some new high-rises; Singapore-facing
- **Medini (Iskandar Puteri):** planned district; Legoland, EDL; mixed results on yield
- **Danga Bay / Permas Jaya:** waterfront; some oversupplied projects
- **Nusajaya / Iskandar Puteri:** government/education anchor (EduCity); AEON; mixed
- **Forest City:** massive reclamation project; struggling with occupancy; policy uncertainty for foreign buyers
- **Senai:** industrial cluster; near airport; strong logistics and manufacturing demand
- **Kota Tinggi / Kluang / Batu Pahat:** secondary towns; much cheaper; limited liquidity

---

## Residential Market

- **JB City / Bukit Chagar / Taman Daya:** RM 250–500 PSF condos; significant overhang; Singaporean buyers re-entering as RTS Link nears
- **Medini / Iskandar Puteri condos:** RM 300–550 PSF; mixed track record on delivery and yield
- **Forest City:** deeply discounted; RM 200–400 PSF; significant uncertainty on foreign buyer policy; avoid until clearer
- **Landed (Johor Bahru):** RM 300–700k for terrace; relatively affordable vs KV; strong local demand
- **RTS Link effect (2025–2026+):** Expect JB properties within 1–3 km of Bukit Chagar station to be most impacted

**Key dynamic:** JB has significant overhang in high-rise segment. Fundamentals need occupancy recovery before capital appreciation. Rental yields can be attractive on low entry prices.

---

## Commercial Market

- JB City shop lots: RM 400–900 PSF; city centre relatively active
- Iskandar Puteri retail: Aeon, Paradigm — suburban retail; moderate yields
- Office market: limited Grade A; most businesses use shopoffices

---

## Industrial Market

- **Senai Industrial Park / Senai Airport City:** strongest industrial cluster in Johor; aerospace, electronics, logistics
- **Pasir Gudang Industrial Estate:** petrochemical, port-related; east JB
- **Nusajaya Tech Park:** newer; targeting technology and electronics
- **Tanjung Pelepas (PTP):** port-adjacent; logistics and warehousing
- **Kulai / Kluang:** lower-cost industrial land; EV supply chain manufacturers looking here
- **Data centre push:** Johor attracted massive data centre investment from hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, etc.) in 2023–2024; industrial land values spiked around Nusajaya and Senai

### Data Centre — JLL 2Q2026 Update (April 2026)

Johor Bahru is now the **#1 data centre market in Asia Pacific** by growth rate:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Completed DC capacity (JB) | **850 MW** |
| Under construction | **1,800 MW** |
| Pipeline beyond 2026 | **2,700 MW** |
| 5-year CAGR since 2020 | **~5,262%** (JLL) |

JB ranks in the **top 3 Apac DC markets** alongside Tokyo and Beijing.

**Industrial land prices (JLL, 2025 national average):** **RM86 psf**, up **8.4% YoY** — Johor's premium nodes (Nusajaya, Senai) likely above this average.

Key industrial demand drivers cited by JLL: aerospace, data centre, data supply chain, cross-border logistics, life sciences, medical, oil & gas.

**Grid constraint risk:** Waiting time to connect to the grid is increasing — 24 months for emerging markets, up to 8 years for core markets. Data centre operators are exploring alternative/solar power solutions to bypass grid delays.

See [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]] for full analysis and [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-malaysia-data-centre-national-overview]] for national context. Asset class framework: [[concepts/data-centre-real-estate]].

---

## Key Developments / Projects

*To be populated as sources are ingested.*

---

## Yield Snapshot

| Asset Class | Gross Yield Range | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Residential (condo) | 4–7% | Higher on low entry prices; but vacancy risk |
| Residential (landed) | 3–5% | Local demand; stable |
| Commercial (shop lot) | 4–6% | City centre stronger |
| Industrial | 5–8% | Very strong with data centre + logistics demand |

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Forest City** — significant policy and reputational risk; very careful assessment needed
- High-rise condo overhang — JB had one of Malaysia's worst residential overhang situations
- RTS Link delays — timeline slippage could dampen sentiment
- Dependency on Singapore economy — SGD/MYR rate swings affect demand
- Foreign ownership rules: some JB zones have higher minimum price thresholds for foreigners
- Reclaimed island projects — engineering risk, title complexity

---

## 2025 Market Data (NAPIC)

| Sub-sector | Volume Change | Value Change |
|---|---|---|
| Overall | -2.3% | +4.4% |
| Residential | 0.0% | +1.4% |
| Commercial | -11.8% | **+23.8%** |
| Industrial | +1.4% | **+44.1%** |

Johor's 2025 story is bifurcated: residential volume stagnated (flat YoY) while industrial and commercial values surged significantly on fewer but larger transactions. The industrial +44.1% value growth on only +1.4% volume growth strongly suggests a few major deals (likely data centre land transactions or large factory/warehouse sales) driving up average values.

**Office concern:** Johor office occupancy sits at **55.1%** (well below the national average of 71.9%) and new supply is still being added. This is a material oversupply risk for JB commercial office.

**New residential launch share:** Johor contributed 17.3% of national new residential launches in 2025 (down from 18.7% in 2024) but had the **best sales take-up at 55.3%** (up from 53.2%), suggesting genuine end-user demand — particularly for landed residential.

**Residential overhang:** Johor has the 2nd-highest completed residential unsold stock at 12.1% of the national unsold total. Terraced houses in outer Iskandar townships are the primary concern.

*Source: [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]*

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]
- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — JLL 2Q2026 data centre/industrial data (p. 5)

notes.title: Klang Valley
notes.content:
# Klang Valley

## Overview

The Klang Valley (KV) is Malaysia's largest and most liquid property market, encompassing Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya, and the urbanised areas of Selangor. Home to ~8 million people (KL city: 1.98M in 2020; broader KV ~8M), it is the primary destination for domestic migration and foreign investment. The market is driven by employment concentration, well-developed public transport (MRT, LRT, monorail, KTM), and a diverse range of price points from mass-market affordable housing to ultra-luxury.

**Key submarkets within KL:** KLCC/Bukit Bintang (luxury), Mont Kiara/Sri Hartamas (expat), Bangsar/Damansara Heights (premium), Sentul (regeneration), Wangsa Maju (growth), Cheras/Ampang, Kepong, Seputeh/Bukit Jalil (fastest-growing).

**KL sub-regional population breakdown (2040 targets, KLSP2040):**
- Bukit Jalil–Seputeh: 500,500 (21.3%) — fastest growing
- Sentul–Menjalara: 493,500 (21.0%)
- Wangsa Maju–Maluri: 470,000 (20.0%)
- Bandar Tun Razak–Sungai Besi: 347,800 (14.8%)
- City Centre: 260,900 (11.1%)
- Damansara–Penchala: 277,300 (11.8%)

---

## Planning Framework — KL Specific

KL (DBKL jurisdiction) has its own distinct planning framework under KLDCP 2024 (KLLP2040), gazetted May 2025. This does NOT apply to Selangor portions of the KV — those fall under MBPJ, MBSA, MPSJ, MPKlang etc.

**Key planning documents:**
- [[concepts/kl-development-control]] — plot ratios, height limits, setbacks, incentives
- [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]] — TOD bonus plot ratio system (103 stations)
- [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]] — CULB2025; defines residential vs commercial use class

**KL 2040 headline numbers (KLSP2040):**
- Population target: **2.35 million**
- GDP target: **RM 680 billion** (RM 289,400 per capita)
- Total housing units target: **762,500** (only 500,803 today)
- Affordable housing target: **305,000 units = 40%** (currently only 40,022 = 8% — massively undersupplied)
- Developable land in KL: only **554.5 ha** barrier-free — KL is land-scarce; high-density mandatory

**KL Development Zones (Vol 2 — 3 Control Zones):**
- Control Zone 1: City Centre — highest intensity
- Control Zone 2: Along MRT3 corridor — 26 transit stations, 73 targeted redevelopment areas; affordable housing emphasis
- Control Zone 3: Outside MRT3 — lower intensity, neighbourhood focus

---

## Residential Market

### Price Benchmarks (KL)
- **KLCC/Bukit Bintang:** RM 1,000–2,500+ PSF; high-end condos; foreign buyer magnet; vacancy concern in some towers
- **Mont Kiara / Sri Hartamas:** RM 700–1,200 PSF; expat rental market; good yield historically 4–5%; MRT3 (proposed) will add two stations here
- **Bangsar / Damansara Heights:** RM 800–1,500 PSF; premium; limited new supply; landed housing commands premiums
- **Sentul:** Regeneration play; prices rising; MRT2 Sentul Barat and KTM Sentul; DBKL secondary growth centre designation
- **Wangsa Maju:** LRT connected; designated Carbon Neutral Growth Centre; District Growth Centre
- **Bukit Jalil / Seputeh:** Fastest-growing planning area in KL per KLSP2040; Pavilion Bukit Jalil is District Growth Centre
- **Petaling Jaya (Selangor, not DBKL):** RM 400–700 PSF; mature; not subject to KLDCP
- **Shah Alam / Subang (Selangor):** RM 300–500 PSF; strong landed demand; not subject to KLDCP

**Official 2020 benchmark prices (KLSP2040 data):**
| Type | 2020 Average | 2015 Average |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| High-rise (condo/apt) | RM 489,600 | RM 390,949 |
| Terrace | RM 867,321 | RM 767,669 |
| Semi-detached | RM 2,542,725 | RM 1,931,047 |
| Detached | RM 3,494,059 | RM 3,735,281 |

**Affordable price ceilings by income group (KLSP2040, 2019 income data):**
| Group | Affordable House Price |
|-------|----------------------|
| B1–B4 (B40) | RM 350,000 – RM 650,000 |
| M1–M4 (M40) | RM 750,000 – RM 1,200,000 |
| T2 | RM 1,500,000 |
| T1 | RM 2,500,000 |

**SOHO / Serviced Apartment note:** Under CULB2025, both are classified as **Commercial (A)**, NOT Residential (B). They attract commercial utility tariffs, commercial assessment rates, and commercial loan terms. Significant overhang in city-centre SOHO segment persists from 2022–2024 era.

**Overhang risk:** City-centre serviced apartments and SOHOs — significant unsold inventory; watch for recovery signals from vacancy surveys and NAPIC data.

---

## Commercial Market

- Shop lots: RM 500–1,500 PSF depending on township and frontage (Jln TAR/Chow Kit/Masjid India: special character zones; heritage height restrictions apply)
- Office space: soft market; KL city office vacancy elevated due to TRX and Merdeka 118 new supply
- Prime malls (Pavilion KL, KLCC, The Exchange TRX): resilient; TRX is new Cat 1 TPZ anchor
- Suburban malls: challenged; conversion of commercial terrace rows into malls is **not permitted** under CULB2025

**Financial activity zones (DBKL designated):**
1. Jalan Ampang – Jalan Tun Razak (banking corridor)
2. Jalan Sultan Ismail – Jalan Raja Chulan (existing banking hub)
3. Jalan Tun Razak – TRX (new financial + fintech hub; MRT2 connected)

---

## Industrial Market

- Shah Alam (Sections 15, 16, 23, 26): established; RM 100–220 PSF for semi-D factory
- Bukit Raja / Klang: RM 80–160 PSF; logistics-heavy
- Sepang / KLIA corridor: aerospace and logistics hub
- Subang SkyPark: MRO cluster; aviation
- **Data centre demand (2023–2025):** Shah Alam and Cyberjaya corridors — Mah Sing's ex-glove factory sites, hyperscaler land acquisitions
- Senai (Johor) competes for industrial FDI but KV (especially Shah Alam / Selangor) remains preferred for proximity to KL port and workforce

---

## Key Development Areas (KLDCP 2024 Designated)

### Kampong Bharu (Special Development Area)
- 123.2 ha; 1,384 lots; ~90% small lots — individual development is extremely difficult
- Malay Agricultural Settlement; governed by Act 733 (Kampong Bharu Development Corporation)
- Height controlled to preserve KLCC views; Islamic Malay architecture guidelines
- Integration with MRT3 (proposed: Kampong Bharu station), Dang Wangi LRT, Chow Kit Monorail
- Lot amalgamation is the key unlock — watch for Corp-facilitated amalgamation deals

### Bandar Malaysia
- Former RMAF Sungai Besi site; massive mixed-use redevelopment
- Future KL–JB HSR terminal (350 km; 90 min)
- MRT2 Bandar Malaysia North and South stations — Cat 1 TPZ
- Sponge city / green development guidelines
- Connects to Razak City, River of Life corridor

### TRX (Tun Razak Exchange)
- Financial hub; The Exchange TRX mall + TRX Residences
- Dual Cat 1 TPZ stations (MRT1 TRX + MRT2 TRX)
- TRX Development Code (separate design control document) applies
- One of the highest-incentive redevelopment zones in Malaysia

### Sentul
- Secondary Growth Centre designation; MRT2 Sentul Barat + KTM Sentul
- Focus on high-value employment; affordable housing emphasis in redevelopment
- Significant regeneration activity ongoing

### Wangsa Maju
- **Carbon Neutral Growth Centre** designation (first in KL)
- District Growth Centre; LRT connected
- 400m radius from LRT station targeted for quality housing

---

## Infrastructure (Rail Network in KL)

**Existing (2024):** 82 stations; ~128 km total in KL
- LRT (Kelana Jaya + Sri Petaling/Ampang), KTM Komuter, Monorail, MRT1 (Kajang Line), ERL

**MRT2 (Putrajaya Line / SSP Line):** Now operational; 21 stations in KL
- Key new stations: Titiwangsa, Ampang Park, Persiaran KLCC, TRX, Bandar Malaysia North/South

**MRT3 (Circle Line — Proposed):** ~42 km / 28 stations in KL
- Would link: Mont Kiara, Sri Hartamas, Titiwangsa, Setapak, Cheras, Bangsar
- Status: funding and timeline uncertain — monitor closely; enormous impact on property values along corridor

**KL–JB HSR:** Starts at Bandar Malaysia; status: planning stage, multiple past revivals

**Modal split target 2040:** 70% public transport : 30% private vehicle (from current ~20:80)

---

## Yield Snapshot

| Asset Class | Gross Yield Range | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Residential condo (B1 titled) | 3.5–5.5% | Higher in expat belt (Mont Kiara); lower in oversupplied segments |
| Residential landed | 2.5–4.0% | Capital appreciation play; lower yield |
| Serviced apartment (A10 titled) | 3.5–5.5% | Commercial tariff adds ~RM 2–4k/year to costs; reduces net |
| Commercial shop lot | 4–6% | Location-dependent; heritage zone height restrictions affect new supply |
| Industrial (Shah Alam / Selangor) | 5–8% | Tight supply; data centre + logistics demand surge |

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- Condo and SOHO overhang in KL city centre — monitor NAPIC quarterly reports
- Rising maintenance fees in older high-rises (strata Act enforcement improving)
- Leasehold land in certain Selangor areas — haircut vs freehold
- SOHO/serviced apartment commercial title — commercial tariffs, narrower buyer pool
- MRT3 funding delay risk — entire Circle Line corridor investment thesis depends on it
- Kampong Bharu individual lots remain difficult to unlock without Corp-led amalgamation
- Heritage zone height restrictions limit redevelopment potential in 200m buffers
- Massive affordable housing gap (305,000 needed vs 40,022 existing) — government may impose developer obligations in future reviews

---

## 2025 Market Data (NAPIC / HB Research)

### KL vs Selangor Performance (Residential, 2025 vs 2024)
- **KL:** +8.7% transaction volume, **+26.4% transaction value** — outperforming; high-value segments driving
- **Selangor:** -5.6% volume, -3.7% value — softening; correction from 2024 peak

### Klang Valley New Residential Launches 2025 (HB Research)
The KV primary market pulled back significantly:

| Metric | 2024 | 2025 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Projects launched | 79 | 66 | -16.5% |
| Units launched | 45,422 | **27,660** | **-39.1%** |
| Selangor share (projects) | 65% | 71% | +6pp |
| KL share (projects) | 35% | 29% | -6pp |
| Highrise share (units) | ~89% | 89% | ~flat |

**Project types (2025):** Serviced residences/apts (20 projects), terrace/superlink (17), condominiums (10), semi-D (10).

**Pricing shift:** In 2024 the RM401k–600k bracket was most common (47% of projects). In 2025, **>RM1M is the most common** (57% of projects). Developers are moving upmarket.

**Shah Alam** was the most active Selangor submarket with 12 new launches.

### 2025 Launch PSF by KL Submarket
| Area | Type | PSF Range (MYR) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| KLCC | Highrise | 1,500–3,600 | 2 projects |
| Bangsar | Highrise | 1,200–1,300 | 1 project |
| Mont Kiara | Highrise | 800–850 | 1 project |
| Old Klang Road | Highrise | 800–900 | 1 project |
| Bukit Jalil | Highrise | 700–1,200 | 2 projects |
| Cheras | Highrise | 700–1,200 | 4 projects |
| Cheras | Landed | 550–650 | — |
| Titiwangsa | Highrise | 700–750 | 1 project |
| Segambut | Highrise | 600–800 | 2 projects |
| Sentul | Highrise | 600–700 | 2 projects |
| Setapak | Highrise | 600–650 | 1 project |
| Sungai Besi | Highrise | 450–550 | 1 project |
| KL Sentral | Highrise | 400–450 | 1 project |

### 2025 Launch PSF by Selangor Submarket
| Area | Type | PSF Range (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| Petaling Jaya | Landed | 950–1,000 |
| Subang Jaya | Highrise | 650–950 |
| Taman Melawati | Highrise | 650–900 |
| Taman Melawati | Landed | 800–900 |
| Puchong | Highrise | 700–800 |
| Puchong | Landed | 750–850 |
| Petaling Jaya | Highrise | 650–800 |
| Shah Alam | Highrise | 250–850 |
| Shah Alam | Landed | 300–800 |
| Kajang | Landed | 500–700 |
| Klang | Landed | 500–600 |
| Cheras (Sel) | Highrise | 500–550 |
| Sungai Buloh | Landed | 450–550 |
| Gombak | Landed | 450–550 |

| Semenyih | Landed | 400–450 |

| Puncak Alam | Landed | 300–450 |

*Source: [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]; data reflects primary market launches surveyed by HB Research*

---
