notes.title: the-cedar
notes.content:
# The Cedar — Satin Magic

## Overview

An ultra-luxury boutique residential development by Satin Magic in Bukit Damansara. 70 units with an exceptional size range: 2,782 to a remarkable 16,248 sf — suggesting penthouse or sky villa units at the top end — at a nett starting PSF of MYR 1,599, matching The Peak Villa as the highest PSF in the Bukit Damansara cohort. Take-up is 73%.

Satin Magic is a boutique developer not widely profiled; the project's strong absorption (73%) at extraordinary unit sizes suggests a high-conviction product that has found its buyer.

---

## Unit Mix

| Size Range (SF) | Nett Start PSF (MYR) | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| 2,782–16,248 | ~1,599 | Includes potential sky villa / penthouse up to 16,248 sf |

*Detailed unit type breakdown not available in current sources. The 16,248 sf upper bound is extraordinary and likely a sky villa or merged penthouse.*

---

## Sales Status

- **Take-up: 73% SPA signed** (UrbanMetry data, as at mid-2025)
- 70 units; approx. 51 units sold; ~19 units remaining
- Comparable to The Peak Villa's 74% — both projects are in the same ultra-luxury tier with similar absorption dynamics

---

## Key Features

- Extreme unit size range: 16,248 sf upper end implies sky villa or duplex penthouse product — extremely rare in KL
- Freehold tenure; Bukit Damansara address — highest-PSF achievable location in study area outside of KLCC
- Boutique scale: 70 units only — high exclusivity
- UrbanMetry explicitly calls out The Cedar alongside The Peak Villa as evidence of "a larger number of over 2,000 sf units in Damansara Heights which have achieved strong sales despite the price tag"

---

## Competitive Positioning

The Cedar sits at the very top of the Bukit Damansara market by PSF and unit size. Its 73% take-up despite units starting at MYR 4.45M (and potentially extending beyond MYR 25M for the largest) is a strong demand signal. The project proves that even niche/boutique developers can achieve premium pricing and take-up in the right location with the right product.

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Developer profile**: Satin Magic is relatively obscure — limited publicly available track record; buyer due diligence on delivery capability is important
- **~19 remaining units**: Includes the very large sky villa / penthouse tier which represents the hardest-to-sell inventory — may require patient timeline
- **Ultra-large unit liquidity**: The resale market for 10,000–16,000 sf condos in KL is extremely thin globally — exit strategy is important for buyers

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-residential]]

notes.title: the-minh
notes.content:
# The Minh — UEM Sunrise

## Overview

A two-tower freehold residential project by UEM Sunrise in Mont Kiara. Tower A has 245 units and Tower B has 251 units (total 496 units), both offering 1,607–3,010 sf at a nett starting PSF of MYR 809. Take-up is 87% for Tower A and 90% for Tower B — solid mid-range absorption for the Mont Kiara segment.

The project is notable for its Vietnamese-influenced naming and branding ("The Minh") — UEM Sunrise's contemporary positioning strategy in a submarket with high expat / international buyer composition.

---

## Unit Mix

| Tower | Units | Size Range (SF) | Nett Start PSF (MYR) | Take-Up |
|-------|-------|-----------------|----------------------|---------|
| Tower A | 245 | 1,607–3,010 | ~809 | 87% |
| Tower B | 251 | 1,607–3,010 | ~809 | 90% |
| **Total** | **496** | — | — | **~88.5% avg** |

*Detailed unit type breakdown not available in current sources.*

---

## Sales Status

- **Take-up: Tower A 87%, Tower B 90%** (UrbanMetry data, as at mid-2025)
- ~439 units sold (of 496 total)
- Consistent performance across both towers; Tower B slightly ahead (possibly launched later with earlier absorbers already in Tower A)

---

## Key Features

- Freehold tenure; Mont Kiara address
- Mid-large unit format (1,607–3,010 sf) — above the compact segment but below the ultra-large Bukit Damansara tier
- UEM Sunrise is a Khazanah-linked tier-1 developer; delivery track record is solid
- Contemporary "The Minh" branding — differentiates from generic Mont Kiara offerings

---

## Competitive Positioning

At MYR 809 PSF with ~88% take-up, The Minh occupies a well-executed mid-tier position. The 1,607 sf minimum unit size is above the smallest units at Papyrus (1,141 sf) — The Minh deliberately targets the more committed owner-occupier segment rather than the compact investor market.

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **~55 unsold units across both towers**: Remaining inventory needs absorption at original or adjusted pricing
- **Mont Kiara incoming supply**: UEM Sunrise's own pipeline and competitor supply could pressure resale values
- **Rental competition**: 496 units entering rental market simultaneously will compete with existing Mont Kiara supply

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-residential]]

notes.title: the-ophera-klgcc
notes.content:
# The Ophera @ KLGCC — Sime Darby Property

## Overview

An ultra-luxury leasehold residential offering by Sime Darby Property within the KLGCC precinct. 150 units of very large format (3,079–7,523 sf) at MYR 1,429 PSF nett starting price, implying total unit prices of MYR 4.4M to over MYR 10M. Take-up is 47% SPA signed — the slowest in the Damansara Heights competitor set by a significant margin, reflecting the thin buyer pool at this absolute price level.

Companion project to Jendela @ KLGCC (520 units, MYR 947 PSF, 98% take-up) — the contrast illustrates that the demand fall-off at MYR 4M+ is steep even within the KLGCC estate.

---

## Unit Mix

| Size Range (SF) | Nett Start PSF (MYR) | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| 3,079–7,523 | ~1,429 | Ultra-large; leasehold; golf estate |

*Detailed unit type breakdown not available in current sources.*

---

## Sales Status

- **Take-up: 47% SPA signed** (UrbanMetry data, as at mid-2025)
- 150 units; approx. 71 units sold — slowest absorption in the D'Heights competitor set
- Contrast with Jendela (98% on smaller/cheaper units) highlights price sensitivity above MYR 4M in the leasehold segment

---

## Key Features

- Ultra-large unit format (up to 7,523 sf) — targets HNW owner-occupiers
- KLGCC precinct; golf course access, resort environment
- Leasehold residential title (vs Jendela's commercial title) — slightly better for financing vs Jendela
- Part of the broader Sime Darby KLGCC estate development

---

## Competitive Positioning

At 47% take-up vs 98% for the adjacent Jendela, The Ophera illustrates a clear price/size ceiling in the KLGCC leasehold market. The Cedar (MYR 1,599 PSF, 70 units, 73% sold) and The Peak Villa (MYR 1,592 PSF, 54 units, 74% sold) are achieving better absorption despite higher PSF — attributable to their freehold residential title and Bukit Damansara address premium over KLGCC.

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Slow absorption**: 47% take-up leaves ~79 units unsold; developer may need to adjust pricing or offer enhanced terms
- **Leasehold + large units**: Financing above MYR 5M on leasehold is constrained; buyer pool is narrower
- **Long sales cycle**: Ultra-luxury product typically requires 2–4 years to fully sell in KL; patience required

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-residential]]

notes.title: the-peak-villa
notes.content:
# The Peak Villa — E&O (Eastern & Oriental)

## Overview

An ultra-luxury boutique residential development by E&O in Bukit Damansara (Damansara Heights). Only 54 units of very large format (3,455–4,500 sf) at a nett starting price of MYR 5.5M / MYR 1,592 PSF. Take-up is 74% — strong performance given the absolute price level (all units above MYR 5M).

E&O is renowned for luxury property delivery (Andaman at Quayside, Gravit8, The Mews) and brings significant brand credibility to the Bukit Damansara luxury market. Alongside The Cedar (Satin Magic, 73% take-up at MYR 1,599 PSF), The Peak Villa validates that the Bukit Damansara >MYR 5M segment has genuine depth.

---

## Unit Mix

| Size Range (SF) | Nett Start PSF (MYR) | Nett Start Price | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|-------|
| 3,455–4,500 | ~1,592 | MYR 5.5M+ | Ultra-large; luxury villa-style; 54 units only |

*Detailed unit type breakdown not available in current sources.*

---

## Sales Status

- **Take-up: 74% SPA signed** (UrbanMetry data, as at mid-2025)
- 54 units; approx. 40 units sold; ~14 units remaining
- Strong absorption for a product where every unit exceeds MYR 5M — validates the E&O brand at the top of the Bukit Damansara market

---

## Key Features

- Ultra-low density: only 54 units — villa-like exclusivity within a highrise development
- E&O's luxury delivery hallmarks: curated finishes, award-winning design, attentive service
- Freehold tenure; Bukit Damansara (the highest-premium address in the study area)
- Very large unit sizes (3,455 sf+) target the same landed-home downsizer / HNW owner-occupier pool as BRDB's Langgak Golf project in U-Thant

---

## Competitive Positioning

The Peak Villa (MYR 1,592 PSF, 74% take-up) and The Cedar (MYR 1,599 PSF, 73%) are near-identical in price performance and form a distinct ultra-luxury tier within Bukit Damansara. Both outperform Sime Darby's The Ophera (MYR 1,429 PSF, 47%) which is hampered by its leasehold tenure despite being in the same price range.

**Takeaway**: Freehold + boutique scale + strong developer brand = best-in-class absorption even at MYR 5M+ price points in Bukit Damansara.

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Thin buyer pool**: ~14 remaining units at MYR 5M+ require patient sales; sales cycle may extend 1–3 years for the remaining inventory
- **E&O delivery**: E&O has a strong track record; the primary risk is macro-level demand cooling rather than developer execution
- **Resale market**: At 54 units, the secondary market is thin — low liquidity but also low resale competition

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2025-07-17-urbanmetry-damansara-heights-residential]]

notes.title: u-thant-langgak-golf
notes.content:
# U-Thant (Langgak Golf) — BRDB

## Overview

BRDB's planned ultra-low-density boutique residential development on a 1.54-acre freehold site (Lot 171, 175, 176, 186 & 188, Seksyen 89) fronting the Langgak Golf course along Jalan U-Thant, Kuala Lumpur. With only 85 units — despite potential to build more — BRDB is positioning this as a deliberate scarcity play in KL's premier embassy district.

Plot ratio: 3.84. Brand concept: "Rhythm-Inspired Living" anchored on golf course landscape and Mid-Century Modern aesthetics. GEO Certified sustainability framework. Planning approval (DA) status not yet confirmed as at April 2026 — expected launch H2 2026 or 2027.

---

## Unit Mix

| Type | Units | Built-Up (SF) | Avg Price | Bedrooms | Baths | Car Parks |
|------|-------|---------------|-----------|----------|-------|-----------|
| A    | 40    | 2,080         | MYR 3.12M | 3+1      | 4     | 3         |
| B    | 29    | 2,850         | MYR 4.27M | 4+1      | 5     | 3         |
| C    | 11    | 3,800         | MYR 5.70M | 4+1+1    | 6     | 4         |
| D    | 5     | 5,000         | MYR 6.40M | 4+1+1    | 6     | 4 (garden terrace) |

**Total**: 85 units | Average ~MYR 1,500 PSF

---

## Sales Status

- Status: **Pre-launch** as at April 2026
- Internal marketing brief (brainstorming) circulated February 2026, revised April 2026
- No SPA signed yet; sales gallery location not yet confirmed
- Marketing strategy: sell through exclusivity, relationships, lifestyle aspiration — in-house or boutique agent model (not mass-market agency)

---

## Key Features

**Design & Concept**
- Concept-led branding: "Rhythm-Inspired Living" inspired by golf rhythm and Mid-Century Modern architecture
- Golf course frontage: views across Langgak Golf / Royal Selangor Golf Club landscape
- Unit D: garden terrace (ground-floor); semi-detached villa character

**Fit-Out Specification** (planned)
- A/C: Ducted VRF (hybrid acceptable); smart home ready
- Kitchen: Gaggenau (dry kitchen), Bosch (wet kitchen)
- Sanitary: Hansgrohe fittings, Villeroy & Boch bathrooms
- Flooring: Herringbone solid timber (bedrooms), compressed stone (living/dining)
- Glazing: Low-E glass
- Sustainability: GEO Certified framework; native planting, water recycling, passive solar design

**Target Buyers**
Owner-occupiers, expats, landed-home downsizers, design-conscious HNW buyers (architects, creatives, senior professionals)

---

## Competitive Positioning

| Metric | U-Thant (Langgak Golf) | The Ashwood | Impression U-Thant | Skyline Embassy |
|--------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Developer | BRDB | Paramount | Yong Tai | TS Law Land |
| Units | 85 | 354 | 108 | 1,296 |
| Size range (sf) | 2,080–5,000 | 1,400–3,000 | 931–1,632 | 521–976 |
| PSF (MYR) | ~1,500 | 1,200–1,300 | 1,300–1,500 | 1,350 |
| Tenure | FH | FH | FH | FH |
| Take-up | Pre-launch | 100% | 95% | 95% |
| Storeys | Low-rise | 49 (2 blk) | 10 (1 blk) | 65 (3 blk) |
| Concept | Golf boutique | Mid-luxury | Compact luxury | High-rise mass |

BRDB occupies the most differentiated position: largest unit sizes, lowest density, highest absolute prices, golf frontage. Proven demand exists — corridor absorption has been strong (Ashwood 100%, Impression 95%). Key risk is the thin buyer pool at MYR 3.12M–6.40M total per unit.

---

## Risks & Watch Points

- **Planning/DA**: Approval status not confirmed in available sources; plot ratio 3.84 noted but OC/launch timeline not stated
- **Pricing execution**: At MYR 1,500 PSF and large unit sizes, targeting a very thin HNW buyer pool — macro sensitivity is high
- **Construction overlap**: Impression U-Thant (China contractors) and Skyline Embassy construction during BRDB's critical launch period could affect site environment and buyer perception
- **Architect/contractor**: Not yet disclosed — quality of delivery is TBC
- **Sales channel**: In-house or boutique model is right for brand but limits reach; delays possible if launches slip into 2027

---

## Sources

- [[sources/2026-04-28-brdb-u-thant-marketing-brief]]

notes.title: log
notes.content:
# Malaysia Property Wiki — Log

> Append-only audit trail. Every ingest, query, and lint is recorded here.
> Format: `## [YYYY-MM-DD] ACTION | Title`
> Parse recent entries: `grep "^## \[" log.md | tail -10`

---

- Avaland Taman U-Thant: 7,613 sqm freehold, RM86.04M (RM1,050 psf land), GDV ~RM700M — Bursa confirmed
- Tropicana Langkawi: 24.15 acres, RM195.88M (~RM186 psf land area)
- UEM Sunrise book value RM1.36/share; market cap RM3.37B at 66.5 sen (51% discount to book)
- KLCCP FY2025: net profit RM1.28bn (+25.95%); revenue RM1.74bn; 47 sen dividend record
- BNM SME Stabilisation Relief Facility: RM5bn, up to RM750K/SME, 3.75% p.a., open May 15–Dec 31 2026

---

notes.title: Malaysia Property Market 2025 — Annual Review (NAPIC/JPPH)

frontmatter.title: Malaysia Property Market 2025 — Annual Review (NAPIC/JPPH)
frontmatter.question: What happened to the Malaysian property market in 2025 and what are the key takeaways by sub-sector?

notes.content:
## Overview

Malaysia's property market achieved a decade-high in **total transaction value** in 2025 at **MYR 241.87 billion (+4.1% YoY)**, though transaction volume eased marginally by 1% to **416,413 units**. This divergence between value growth and volume decline signals a maturing market where higher-value assets are driving momentum while affordable-end activity is normalising.

The industrial sub-sector was the clear standout of 2025, growing value by 21.3%. Residential and commercial segments were more subdued, with residential volume declining -1.5% and commercial transaction growth decelerating sharply from the prior year's pace. The headline narrative: the market is stable but entering a slower, more selective phase.

---

## National Picture

### Transaction Value & Volume (2025 vs 2024)

| Metric | 2025 | Change |
|---|---|---|
| Total transaction value | MYR 241.87 billion | +4.1% |
| Total transaction volume | 416,413 units | -1.0% |
| Residential volume share | 61.6% | ~flat |
| Residential value share | 44.8% | -1.2pp (from 46%) |
| Commercial volume share | 11.2% | ~flat |
| Commercial value share | 24.3% | -0.7pp |
| Industrial volume share | 2.1% | ~flat |
| Industrial value share | 14.0% | up from prior year |

### Regional Concentration

The Central region (KL, Selangor, Putrajaya) punches well above its weight. It generates just 23.9% of transaction volume but **44.3% of total value (MYR 107.09 billion)**. The Southern region (Johor, etc.) leads by volume at 26.1% but generates only 28.5% of value. The Northern region (Penang, Perak, Kedah) contributes 25.1% of volume but only 15.4% of value. This concentration of value in the Central region reflects the premium pricing of KL and Selangor assets relative to the rest of the country.

### Best and Worst Performing States (Overall)

**Growth across both volume and value (2025 vs 2024):**
- **Negeri Sembilan:** +11.2% volume, +26.6% value — the top all-round performer; positive growth across ALL sub-sectors
- **Kuala Lumpur:** +14.2% volume, +6.0% value
- **Kelantan:** +24.3% volume, +11.9% value
- **Kedah:** +2.6% volume, +16.3% value

**States declining in both volume and value:**
- **Pahang:** -12.2% volume, -8.0% value
- **Penang:** -3.9% volume, -5.0% value

---

## Residential Sub-Sector

### Market Summary

Residential remains the dominant sub-sector by volume (61.6% of all transactions) but showed mild softness in 2025 with a -1.5% volume decline and a modest +1.3% value gain. The value gain despite volume decline confirms the ongoing shift toward higher-value transactions.

**Key structural dynamics:**
- The **secondary (sub-sale) market** now accounts for **84.5% of all residential transactions** (up from 83% in 2024). The primary market is softening.
- New launches fell sharply: **64,487 units in 2025 vs 75,784 in 2024 (-14.9%)**. Developer caution is evident.
- **Sales take-up dropped to 35.5%** (from 37.3%), meaning roughly 2 in 3 units launched go unsold in the primary market.
- The **Malaysian House Price Index (MHPI) grew 2.6%** — modest but positive across all house types.

### Price Segment Breakdown (2025)

| Price Band | Share of Transactions | YoY Trend |
|---|---|---|
| ≤ MYR 300,000 | 52.3% | Stable (same as 2024) |
| MYR 300,001–500,000 | 24.9% | Stable |
| MYR 500,001–1,000,000 | 16.9% | Stable |
| > MYR 1,000,000 | 5.9% | **+6.5% — fastest growing** |

The affordable segment (≤RM300k) still dominates by volume, but the > RM1M segment is growing fastest. Luxury demand from KL and Selangor is the driver.

### Property Type Demand (2025)

| Type | Share of Transactions |
|---|---|
| Terraced houses | 41.3% |
| Vacant plots | 18.1% |
| High-rise (condo/apartment) | 14.3% |
| Low-cost/flat | 10.6% |
| Semi-detached | 7.3% |

The landed preference is clear: Malaysians continue to prefer terraced and semi-D homes where affordable and accessible. High-rise remains a secondary choice nationally but dominates in urban KL.

### MHPI by House Type (2025)

| Type | Annual Growth |
|---|---|
| Terraced | +3.3% |
| Semi-detached | +2.6% |
| Detached | +2.4% |
| High-rise | +0.6% |

High-rise appreciation is notably lagging landed residential — relevant context for condo investors.

### Residential Overhang (Unsold Completed Units)

States with the highest unsold completed residential stock:
- Perak: 12.9%
- Johor: 12.1%
- Selangor: 11.6%
- Penang: 10.8% (unsold under construction)

**Product type overhang:** Condos/apartments = 47.1% of total unsold completed stock; terraced houses = 30.5%. High-rise developers face the most pressure in clearing inventory.

**Price range overhang:**
- Below RM300k = 37.7% (largest share)
- RM300k–500k = 27.5%
- RM500k–1M = 24.3%
- > RM1M = 10.5%

### State Residential Performance (2025 vs 2024)

| State | Volume | Value | Commentary |
|---|---|---|---|
| KL | +8.7% | +26.4% | Strong recovery; high-value units driving |
| Negeri Sembilan | +14.7% | +0.6% | High volume growth; spillover from KV |
| Sabah | +4.6% | +4.5% | Steady |
| Kedah | +4.0% | +15.7% | Underrated market |
| Selangor | -5.6% | -3.7% | Softening; new launches pulled back |
| Penang | -4.9% | -7.0% | Both volume and value declining |
| Perak | -7.2% | -10.9% | Weak; high overhang |
| Pahang | -14.6% | -17.4% | Significant decline |
| Johor | 0.0% | +1.4% | Flat volume; value holding |

---

## Commercial Sub-Sector

### Market Summary

Commercial transactions posted marginal growth (+1.4% volume, +1.1% value) — a marked deceleration from the 2024 boom (+13.6% volume, +51.6% value). The market is normalising after a strong 2024.

**Transaction composition:**
- Shop transactions (mainly 2 & 2.5-storey shops): 41.9% of commercial volume, 33.8% of value
- Service apartments: 32% of volume, 22% of value (concentrated in KL, Selangor, Johor)

### Office Market

Office buildings have defied expectations with **resilient occupancy** — most states above 80%. However, the three largest urban centres are below this threshold:

| Market | Office Occupancy 2025 |
|---|---|
| Terengganu | 92.5% (highest) |
| **National average** | **71.9%** |
| Kuala Lumpur | 70.8% |
| Johor Bahru | 55.1% (at-risk of oversupply) |

**Risk watch:** Significant new office supply is coming to KL and JB from projects currently under construction. If demand does not accelerate, vacancy rates will rise and rental rates will come under pressure. JB at 55.1% occupancy is already weak and faces further supply additions.

### Retail Market

Shopping complex occupancy ticked up to **78.9%** (from 78.8%). Rentals are generally stable, with popular, well-positioned complexes in KV, Penang, and Johor managing small rental increases. The retail market is resilient but not growing — a function of stable consumer confidence against moderate new supply.

### Commercial State Performance (Selected, 2025 vs 2024)

| State | Volume | Value |
|---|---|---|
| KL | +24.2% | -9.5% |
| Johor | -11.8% | +23.8% |
| Penang | -2.0% | **-24.9%** |
| Negeri Sembilan | +23.1% | +18.5% |
| Sabah | +14.3% | +20.2% |

KL commercial volume surged but value fell — suggestive of more lower-value transactions (shophouses, service apartments) vs fewer large office deals. Johor saw value surge on flat volume — a few large deals driving the number. Penang's -24.9% value decline is a red flag.

---

## Industrial Sub-Sector

### Market Summary

Industrial was the standout performer of 2025. Volume grew modestly (+1.4%) but **value surged 21.3%** — building on the 16.4% value growth in 2024. Strong manufacturing fundamentals, foreign direct investment inflows (data centres, semiconductor, EV supply chain), and the continued attraction of Malaysia as a production base all supported this.

**Transaction composition:**
- Terraced warehouses: 33.3%
- Vacant industrial plots: 27.1%
- Semi-detached factories: 21.8%

### State Industrial Performance (2025 vs 2024)

| State | Volume | Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Selangor** | +8.8% | +11.0% |
| **Johor** | +1.4% | **+44.1%** |
| Negeri Sembilan | +12.2% | **+151.9%** (low base) |
| Kelantan | +27.8% | **+62.5%** (low base) |
| Terengganu | +6.1% | **+50.9%** |
| Penang | -2.0% | +5.2% |
| KL | +12.9% | -20.5% |
| Perak | -33.7% | +11.5% |

Selangor maintains its dominance at **35.7% of national industrial volume** (+8.76% vol, +10.98% value), driven by terraced warehouses (+33.3%), vacant plots (+27.1%), and semi-D factories (+21.8%). Johor's 44% value growth reflects increasing investor interest in Iskandar Malaysia for logistics and manufacturing. Note that the large percentage increases in smaller states (Negeri Sembilan, Kelantan, Terengganu) are partly a low-base effect — actual dollar volumes are much smaller than Selangor/Johor.

### Industrial Overhang

- Completed overhang: +2.4% volume, +12.8% value
- Under-construction unsold: +27.1% (rising — bears watching)
- Not-yet-constructed unsold: -34.7% (improving)

---

## Klang Valley New Launches — Detailed 2025 Data

Henry Butcher's own research tracked all new residential launches in the Klang Valley. The data reveals a significant supply pullback:

| Metric | 2024 | 2025 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total projects | 79 | 66 | -16.5% |
| Total units | 45,422 | 27,660 | **-39.1%** |
| Selangor share (projects) | 65% | 71% | +6pp |
| KL share (projects) | 35% | 29% | -6pp |
| Selangor share (units) | 46% | 50% | +4pp |
| KL share (units) | 54% | 50% | -4pp |
| High-rise share (units) | ~89% | 89% | ~flat |

The -39% unit decline is the most striking figure. It signals developers substantially reducing supply in response to slower absorption, accumulating inventory, and global macro uncertainty.

**Project type mix (2025):**
- Serviced residences/apartments: 20 projects (dominant)
- Terrace/super-link: 17 projects
- Condominiums: 10 projects
- Semi-detached: 10 projects

**Pricing shift:** In 2024, the RM401k–600k bracket was most common (47% of projects). In 2025, **>RM1M is the most common** price bracket (57% of projects). This reflects both genuine demand at the top end and developers avoiding the overcrowded affordable mid-market.

**PSF shift:** RM501–750 psf remains the most common PSF band (46% of projects), but the >RM1M total price point reflects larger unit sizes pushing total values up even at similar PSF.

### KL Submarket Launch PSF (2025)

| Area | PSF Range (MYR) |
|---|---|
| KLCC | 1,500–3,600 |
| Bangsar | 1,200–1,300 |
| Old Klang Road | 800–900 |
| Mont Kiara | 800–850 |
| Bukit Jalil | 700–1,200 |
| Cheras (highrise) | 700–1,200 |
| Titiwangsa | 700–750 |
| Segambut | 600–800 |
| Sentul | 600–700 |
| Setapak | 600–650 |
| Cheras (landed) | 550–650 |
| Sungai Besi | 450–550 |
| KL Sentral | 400–450 |

### Selangor Submarket Launch PSF (2025)

| Area | Type | PSF Range (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| Petaling Jaya | Landed | 950–1,000 |
| Subang Jaya | Highrise | 650–950 |
| Taman Melawati | Both | 650–900 |
| Puchong | Both | 700–850 |
| Petaling Jaya | Highrise | 650–800 |
| Shah Alam | Both | 250–850 |
| Kajang | Landed | 500–700 |
| Klang | Landed | 500–600 |
| Cheras (Sel) | Highrise | 500–550 |
| Sungai Buloh | Landed | 450–550 |
| Gombak | Landed | 450–550 |
| Cheras (Sel) | Landed | 400–500 |
| Rawang | Landed | 400–550 |
| Sepang | Landed | 400–500 |
| Semenyih | Landed | 400–450 |
| Cyberjaya | Landed | 350–650 |
| Puncak Alam | Landed | 300–450 |

---

## 2026 Outlook

Based on HB's commentary:

- Market expected to be **generally stable but more subdued** in 2026
- **Key downside risk:** Middle East conflict (Iran-US-Israel) — Straits of Hormuz closure → oil price spike → inflation → global supply chain disruption → reduced investor confidence
- **Malaysia buffer:** Net energy exporter = relatively less impacted than oil-importing nations; benefiting from tourist diversion away from conflict zones
- Industrial sector to remain the strongest performer; residential growth to slow
- Office oversupply risk in KL and JB if pipeline completions proceed as planned
- Developer caution likely to persist — expect fewer new launches in 2026

---

## Confidence Notes

- **High confidence:** All NAPIC/JPPH transaction data (government statistics, reconstructed by HB from official charts)
- **High confidence:** HB's own KV new launch data (proprietary research, project-by-project tracking)
- **Medium confidence:** PSF ranges per submarket — these are launch ranges across multiple projects, not averages
- **Qualitative/estimates:** 2026 outlook commentary is HB editorial opinion, not hard data
- **Low base caveat:** Negeri Sembilan, Kelantan, Terengganu industrial % gains are inflated by tiny 2024 bases

## Sources Used

- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]]

notes.title: Malaysia Construction Cost Inflation 2026 — Economy Minister Warning

frontmatter.title: Malaysia Construction Cost Inflation 2026 — Economy Minister Warning
frontmatter.question: How much have construction costs risen in Malaysia as of 2026, and what is the impact on property development?

notes.content:
# Malaysia Construction Cost Inflation 2026

## Context

Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir issued a public warning on April 28, 2026, during a live Facebook briefing on global supply chain pressures. The minister flagged that construction costs have risen significantly, driven primarily by energy-related cost pass-throughs from the ongoing West Asia (Iran war) conflict, which has disrupted global supply chains and pushed up diesel, fuel, and commodity prices.

This follows a period where Brent crude has traded between US$109–113/barrel (April 20–27, 2026), up from pre-war levels.

---

## Key Data

### Headline: +12.6% Average Increase in Construction Costs

Building material costs rose by an **average of 12.6%** as of April 2026. The minister specifically cited higher prices for:
- Diesel
- Bitumen
- Logistics costs
- Other inputs (raw materials, production, transport, distribution)

### Materials as Share of Construction Cost

Materials account for **over 60%** of total construction costs in Malaysia. This structural fact means commodity price shocks pass through quickly and heavily into project budgets.

### Implications for Ongoing Projects

- Infrastructure projects most immediately at risk (government committed to continue roads, schools, clinics, basic infrastructure)
- Private sector residential and commercial projects face margin compression or price increase pressure
- The minister stressed monitoring upstream factors — fertiliser, diesel, energy, raw materials, production, transport, distribution — not just retail prices

### Labour Market Pressure

Labour market pressure is expected to intensify in **2Q2026** due to the delayed effects of the global energy crisis. Perkeso data:
- Unemployment benefit applications: 7,512 (Feb) → 5,855 (Mar) → 5,734 (early Apr)
- Most affected sectors: manufacturing, services, ICT, hospitality, transport

Government response: focus on social protection, job training, gig worker support, youth development, SME resilience.

---

## Oil Price Context (April 2026)

| Date | Brent Crude (US$/barrel) |
|------|--------------------------|
| Week of Apr 20–24 | 109.94 |
| April 24 | 112.92 |
| April 27 | 113.34 |

Oil prices "remain volatile but slightly easing" (week-on-week Brent fell 6.7% to US$109.94 before rising again). The instability reflects the Strait of Hormuz disruption from the Iran war — ~1/3 of global seaborne crude oil trade passes through the strait.

---

## Impact on Malaysian Property Development

### Cost PSF Benchmarks — Upward Pressure

The wiki's baseline construction cost benchmarks (set in 2024–2025) should be adjusted upward by approximately **10–15%** for projects tendered or under construction in 2026. Reference:

| Segment | 2024–2025 Baseline | 2026 Estimated (post +12.6%) |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Low-cost / affordable | MYR 80–120 PSF | MYR 90–135 PSF |
| Mass market high-rise | MYR 150–220 PSF | MYR 170–248 PSF |
| Mid-range high-rise | MYR 220–350 PSF | MYR 248–394 PSF |
| Luxury high-rise | MYR 350–600 PSF | MYR 395–676 PSF |

*These are indicative estimates. Actual impact depends on project-specific contract type, timing of tender, and procurement strategy. Final-account data from 2026 projects will be more definitive.*

### Developer Margin Pressure

- Developers with large unbilled sales (fixed SPA prices) will absorb the cost increase directly into margins if material cost clauses are not built into contracts
- KLCCP (see [[entities/areas/klcc-bukit-bintang]]) flagged cost pressures on its retail and hotel operations — a bellwether for KL commercial real estate
- Infrastructure-heavy projects (industrial parks, landed townships) more exposed to bitumen and diesel cost increases than pure high-rise residential

### Government Infrastructure Commitment

Despite cost pressures, the government has pledged to continue key public developments (roads, schools, clinics). This implies:
1. Government construction contracts will likely face cost variation claims from contractors
2. Public sector capex budget may face overruns
3. This can indirectly support the construction sector's order book and employment

---

## Confidence Notes

- **High confidence**: 12.6% average construction cost increase — directly cited by Economy Minister in official briefing (April 28, 2026)
- **High confidence**: Materials = >60% of construction cost — standard industry figure, corroborated by minister
- **Medium confidence**: PSF estimate adjustments above are indicative — no project-level tender data from 2026 yet ingested
- **Low confidence**: Labour pressure outlook — Perkeso data is directional but Q2 data not yet available

---

## Sources Used

- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — Economy minister construction cost warning (pp. 1, 3)
- [[construction-costs/rates/structural]] — baseline rate reference
- [[construction-costs/rates/piling-foundation]] — baseline rate reference

notes.title: Johor Data Centre Boom & Industrial Land Prices 2026 — JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 Report

frontmatter.title: Johor Data Centre Boom & Industrial Land Prices 2026 — JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 Report
frontmatter.question: What is the state of Johor's data centre market and industrial land prices in 2026?

notes.content:
# Johor Data Centre Boom & Industrial Land Prices 2026

## Source

JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 report, reported in The Edge on April 28, 2026. Data compiled as at 2Q2026.

---

## Malaysia Data Centre Capacity — National Overview

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Current national capacity | ~1,020 MW (mid-2026) |
| End-2026 projected capacity | **2,055 MW** |
| 5-year CAGR (since 2020) | ~70% national |
| Further pipeline beyond 2026 | 3,500 MW |

Growth driven by AI adoption, 5G rollout, and expansion of data-intensive applications. The 70% CAGR since 2020 is described by JLL as making Malaysia one of the fastest-growing data centre markets in Asia Pacific.

---

## Johor Bahru — Apac's Data Centre Powerhouse

JB has added **574 MW over the past five years**, placing it in the **top 3 markets in Apac** alongside Tokyo and Beijing.

| Metric | Johor Bahru |
|--------|-------------|
| Completed capacity | 850 MW |
| Under construction | 1,800 MW |
| Total pipeline | 2,700 MW |
| 5-year CAGR (since 2020) | ~5,262% |

JLL capital markets senior director Keith Eng: *"Comparing Malaysia to the region, we see Johor Bahru at a 5,262% growth. That is the highest in Apac."*

### Why JB?
- Proximity to Singapore — hyperscalers seeking lower-cost alternative to island nation
- Land availability at significantly cheaper rates than Singapore
- Existing industrial and logistics infrastructure
- Strong government support within Iskandar Malaysia framework
- Singapore–Johor border provides technical proximity for latency-sensitive workloads

---

## Industrial Land Prices

| Metric | 2024 | 2025 | Change |
|--------|------|------|--------|
| National industrial land price | n/a | **RM86 psf** | **+8.4% YoY** |

- This is the JLL-reported national average for industrial land in 2025
- Johor is implied to be at or above this level given its data centre premium
- Key demand sectors: aerospace, data centre, data supply chain, cross-border logistics, life sciences, medical, oil & gas

---

## Grid Connectivity Risk — Key Constraint

The rapid growth is creating infrastructure strain, specifically on grid connectivity:

| Market Type | Grid Wait Time |
|-------------|----------------|
| Emerging/smaller markets | ~24 months |
| Core markets | Up to **8 years** |

JLL's Eng: *"With such growth, we see an increase in the waiting time for the connectivity of connecting to the grid… up to eight years in certain core markets."*

**Investor implication**: Data centre operators are exploring alternative locations and solar/renewable energy solutions to bypass grid constraints. This creates opportunity for sites with existing power infrastructure or self-generation capability.

---

## Office Market — Singapore Spillover to Johor

JLL is seeing early signs of Singapore office occupiers exploring relocation to JB:
- Rent differential is the primary driver (Singapore vs JB)
- Popular Grade A Johor Bahru office buildings: **Mid Valley Southkey, JBCC Tower, Menara MBJB, Medini 9**
- JLL describes this as "early spillover demand" — not yet a dominant trend

This is relevant for JB commercial property investors: Grade A office buildings with sustainable spec are the primary beneficiaries.

---

## Quality Shift — Flight to Green

JLL notes a global and Malaysia-specific trend:
- "Flight-to-quality, flight-to-green" — occupiers shifting to higher-quality, more sustainable assets
- JLL managing director Jamie Tan: "developments that focus more on sustainability would be more future-proof and would have better life-cycle-costing"
- Bifurcation of market: sustainable assets vs. older stock — key risk for buyers of older industrial buildings

---

## Investment Implications

1. **Industrial land in Johor is a structural bull trade** — data centre demand is durable (AI capex cycle early innings), logistics demand is strong, Singapore proximity creates a ceiling on available options for hyperscalers
2. **Grid access is the gating factor** — sites near existing substations or with dedicated power supply command premium
3. **RM86 psf national average** is likely a floor for high-demand Johor nodes; expect premium pricing around Nusajaya, Senai, Iskandar Puteri
4. **Singapore office relocation theme** is early-stage — monitor take-up rates at JB Grade A buildings as a lead indicator
5. **Self-generation + solar** increasingly important for data centre site selection — reduces grid dependency risk

---

## Confidence Notes

- **High confidence**: Capacity figures, CAGR, RM86 psf — directly from JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 press conference
- **High confidence**: Grid wait time ranges — from JLL presentation data
- **Medium confidence**: JB CAGR of 5,262% is extraordinary — likely reflects near-zero base in 2020; directionally correct as JB had minimal data centre capacity before the hyperscaler wave
- **Medium confidence**: Singapore office spillover — described as "early" with no hard take-up data provided

---

## Sources Used

- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 data (p. 5)
- [[entities/areas/johor]] — area context
- [[concepts/data-centre-real-estate]] — asset class framework and key metrics
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-malaysia-data-centre-national-overview]] — national capacity map across all Malaysian DC hubs

notes.title: Johor Data Centre Land — Investor Research Brief (April 2026)

frontmatter.title: Johor Data Centre Land — Investor Research Brief (April 2026)
frontmatter.question: Where and how should I invest in data centre land in Johor?

notes.content:
# Johor Data Centre Land — Investor Research Brief (April 2026)

## Sources

- JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 report (via The Edge, April 2026)
- Reed Smith LLP: JS-SEZ Data Centres analysis (June 2025)
- TM Nxera / TNB 280MW supply agreement (January 2026)
- Sunway → Equalbase RM380M land transaction (Johor, 2024)
- NTT DATA / Tropicana land acquisition (~USD88.5M)
- Stack Infrastructure 220MW campus announcement
- Fortune / IBTEC launch (December 2025)
- IMFC-J committed investment figures (2025)

---

## The Macro Case

JB has gone from near-zero data centre capacity in 2020 to **850 MW completed + 1,800 MW under construction** (JLL 2Q2026), a 5,262% CAGR — the highest in Asia Pacific. This is not speculative: hyperscalers are in the ground, TNB has signed power agreements, and the Johor–Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) provides bilateral policy anchoring.

### Structural demand drivers

- **Singapore arbitrage:** Power tariffs ~13.5 sen/kWh in Johor vs. ~23.9 sen/kWh in Singapore (~half). Land is a fraction of the cost. Hyperscalers need Singapore latency without Singapore land constraints.
- **AI capex supercycle:** Every major hyperscaler (Microsoft, Google, Equinix, NTT, Vantage, YTL) is in JB or actively acquiring. Multi-year build-out early innings.
- **JS-SEZ policy tailwind:** Agreement signed January 7, 2025. MYR 16.7B committed, MYR 26.2B under review. Bilateral framework makes it harder to unwind than unilateral policy.
- **RTS Link (end-2026):** Bukit Chagar–Woodlands rapid transit; 40,000 daily passengers projected. Reduces cross-border worker friction.

---

## Land Zones & Pricing

| Zone | Land Type | Price (2025–2026) | DC Suitability | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Iskandar Puteri / Nusajaya Tech Park** | Industrial freehold | **RM130–140 PSF** | ★★★★★ | Anchored by Sunway → Equalbase RM380M / 64ac = RM136 PSF; power-ready scarce |
| **Sedenak Tech Park** | Industrial | RM90–120 PSF (est.) | ★★★★☆ | 745-acre JLand Group designated tech park; significant pipeline |
| **Senai Airport City** | Industrial | RM70–100 PSF (est.) | ★★★★☆ | Airport logistics synergy; further from SG; more competitive pricing |
| **Medini** | Mixed / some leasehold | RM100–130 PSF | ★★★☆☆ | Leasehold risk; mixed-use planning; less pure-play industrial |
| **Pasir Gudang** | Industrial (port-adj.) | RM50–80 PSF (est.) | ★★★☆☆ | Petrochemical heritage; less preferred for hyperscale DC |

### Key benchmark transactions

- **Sunway → Equalbase (Singapore):** 64 acres in Sunway City Iskandar Puteri for RM380M (RM5.94M/ac, ~RM136 PSF) — data centre development. The clearest price discovery for the premium tier.
- **NTT DATA → Tropicana:** ~USD88.5M (~MYR395M) for a 6-building DC campus in JB. Dec 2024.
- **Iskandar Puteri appreciation trajectory:** From ~RM65 PSF (2015) to ~RM130 PSF (2024) — doubled in 10 years. NAPIC shows 18 industrial transactions in the corridor in Q3–Q4 2024 at a median of RM520,000/acre (~RM11.9 PSF) — note: these reflect a broader industrial average, not DC-specific premium sites.
- **JLL national industrial average (2025):** RM86 PSF, +8.4% YoY. JB data centre nodes command a significant premium above this floor.

---

## Power & Grid: The Gating Factor

This is the single most critical DD item for a data centre land play.

| Metric | Detail |
|---|---|
| Grid wait time (emerging markets) | ~24 months |
| Grid wait time (core markets) | Up to **8 years** |
| TNB Green Lane Pathway | Compresses connection to **12 months** for qualifying DC projects |
| Largest single power deal | TM Nxera: 280MW from TNB (Jan 2026) for Iskandar Puteri campus |

**Practical rule:** A parcel with confirmed TNB substation allocation or pre-qualified under the Green Lane One-Stop Centre is worth materially more than a raw parcel where the buyer joins the queue. Verify TNB connection status before committing. Data centre operators are also exploring on-site solar/renewable to reduce grid dependency.

---

## Major Operators in the Ground

| Operator | Capacity | Location | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft** | Southeast Asia 3 cloud region (3 AZs) | JB | Expanding; Nov 2025 announcement |
| **YTL** | Hyperscale campus | JB | Operational |
| **TM Nxera** (TM + Nxera JV) | 64MW → 200MW (phase 1: 2H 2026); 280MW TNB secured | Iskandar Puteri | Phase 1 H2 2026 |
| **Stack Infrastructure** | 220MW campus, 10.8 ha | JB | Phase 1: Q4 2026 |
| **Vantage** | 300MW (acquired) | JB | Active |
| **Equinix** | Colocation | JB | Active |
| **NTT DATA** | 6-building campus | JB (Tropicana land) | First building ~2027 |
| **Empyrion Digital** | Next-gen hyperscale campus | JB | Announced |

---

## JS-SEZ & IBTEC — Policy Anchors

- **Johor–Singapore SEZ:** Signed January 7, 2025. Bilateral framework targeting high-value FDI — data centres, AI, renewables, life sciences, logistics. MYR 16.7B committed, MYR 26.2B in pipeline (IMFC-J, 2025).
- **IBTEC (Innovation, Business & Technology Ecosystem Cluster):** 7,300-acre innovation sandbox launched December 2025. Formally designated for data centres, medtech, logistics, agritech. This is the designated zone for high-value tech land within the JS-SEZ framework.
- **Nusajaya Tech Park:** 509 acres. Established DC and tech-adjacent industrial zone.
- **Sedenak Tech Park:** 745 acres. Newer; JLand Group developer.

---

## Investment Framework

**Nature of the play:** This is a capital appreciation and option value trade, not a current income play. Industrial DC land in JB produces minimal yield until leased to an operator. The value thesis is:

1. Buy entitled land in the right zone
2. Secure or facilitate TNB power allocation (via Green Lane)
3. Exit to a DC operator/hyperscaler or JV with a DC developer

**The land-to-operator exit:** The Sunway → Equalbase transaction exemplifies this. Sunway sold at RM136 PSF to a Singapore DC developer. The value creation was in holding well-located industrial land long enough for DC demand to price it at a premium.

**Minimum viable parcel:** Hyperscale requires 10–100+ contiguous acres. Parcels < 5 acres are edge/colo plays with limited hyperscaler exit optionality. For a direct hyperscaler sale, 30–100 acres is the sweet spot.

**Return expectation (based on available data):**
- Iskandar Puteri premium nodes: 18–25% p.a. appreciation (2024 data; may moderate as pricing matures)
- JLL national industrial floor: RM86 PSF, +8.4% YoY — this is the slow-money baseline
- DC-specific premium land in right location with power: outperforms significantly

---

## Risks & Watch Points

| Risk | Severity | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Grid constraint** | 🔴 High | Raw land without TNB allocation may wait years. Verify Green Lane eligibility pre-purchase. |
| **Land title complexity** | 🔴 High | Johor has Bumi lot, leasehold, and state land nuances. Full legal DD mandatory. |
| **AI capex cycle reversal** | 🟡 Medium | Hyperscaler demand could slow if model efficiency gains reduce compute needs. |
| **DC oversupply (MW)** | 🟡 Medium | 2,700 MW pipeline is enormous. Not all will be absorbed cleanly. Operators may not close all land buys. |
| **Water supply** | 🟡 Medium | DC cooling is water-intensive. Johor-Singapore water interdependency adds complexity. |
| **FDI/policy shift** | 🟡 Medium | JS-SEZ and Malaysia's openness to foreign DC investment is the current regime. Political change could alter conditions. |
| **Concentration risk** | 🟡 Medium | JB thesis relies on a handful of hyperscalers. Demand is deep but concentrated. |

---

## Confidence Notes

| Item | Confidence |
|---|---|
| JB capacity figures (850MW + 1,800MW pipeline) | **High** — JLL 2Q2026 report |
| Iskandar Puteri land price RM130–140 PSF | **High** — anchored by Sunway/Equalbase transaction |
| Senai / Sedenak pricing estimates | **Medium** — estimated from range data; less direct transaction evidence |
| TNB Green Lane 12-month timeline | **High** — TNB official announcement |
| JS-SEZ MYR 16.7B committed investment | **High** — official IMFC-J figure |
| 18–25% p.a. appreciation rate | **Medium** — secondary market data; not verified NAPIC figures |
| Grid wait times | **High** — JLL 2Q2026 press conference |

---

## Sources Used

- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]] — base analysis (JLL 2Q2026)
- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — JLL data centre/industrial p.5
- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]] — NAPIC 2025 Johor industrial value data
- [[entities/areas/johor]] — area context
- External: Reed Smith JS-SEZ analysis; TM Nxera/TNB 280MW deal (Jan 2026); Fortune/IBTEC launch (Dec 2025); Sunway FMT land deal; Stack Infrastructure / Mingtiandi JB campus

notes.title: KL Luxury Residential Prices & Malaysia UHNWI Growth 2025–2026 — Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026

frontmatter.title: KL Luxury Residential Prices & Malaysia UHNWI Growth 2025–2026 — Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026
frontmatter.question: How did KL luxury residential prices perform in 2025, and what is the UHNWI outlook for Malaysia?

notes.content:
# KL Luxury Residential Prices & Malaysia UHNWI Growth 2025–2026

## Source

Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026 (press statement, April 28, 2026), reported in The Edge.

---

## KL Luxury Residential Price Performance

### 2025 Capital Appreciation: +1.1%

Luxury residences in Kuala Lumpur showed stable appreciation of **+1.1% in 2025**. This is in line with the overall residential segment's steady performance.

**Context vs. regional peers:**
| Market | 2025 Luxury Price Change |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Kuala Lumpur | **+1.1%** |
| Hong Kong | -2.1% |
| Singapore | Record highs (>US$6,000 = RM23,782 psf) |

KL outperformed Hong Kong while remaining accessible vs. Singapore's record-setting levels. The stability reflects:
- Supportive housing policy reforms (proposed Real Property Development Act)
- TEDUH platform (Transforming and Empowering Data Usage in Housing) boosting market sentiment
- Transactions volume growth: 399,008 units (2023) → **420,545 units (2024)**, +5.4%
- KL positioned as safe haven for Asian UHNWI amid geopolitical uncertainty

Knight Frank executive director Adrian Yeoh: *"We're looking at a mixed outlook in 2026, as supportive housing reforms such as the proposed Real Property Development Act and Transforming and Empowering Data Usage in Housing (TEDUH) platform serve to buoy dampened market sentiment amid wider uncertainty."*

---

## Malaysia UHNWI Population Outlook

Ultra-high-net-worth individuals (UHNWI) defined as those with net assets **>US$30 million** (>RM112 million at RM3.73/USD).

### 2026 → 2031 Projections (Knight Frank)

| Country | 2026 UHNWI | 2031 UHNWI | Growth |
|---------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Malaysia** | **1,566** | **1,881** | **+315 (+20%)** |
| Singapore | 7,171 | 10,495 | +46% |
| Indonesia | 3,833 | 6,966 | +82% |
| Thailand | 2,853 | 3,582 | +26% |
| Philippines | 1,910 | 2,844 | +49% |
| Vietnam | 1,233 | 1,960 | +59% |
| Japan | 18,914 | 21,634 | +14% |
| China | 121,677 | 144,602 | +19% |
| US | 251,352 | 387,422 | +54% |

Malaysia is projected to:
- Add **5 more billionaires** by 2031 (to 18 total)
- Add **315+ UHNWI** with >US$30M net worth
- Growth attributed to Malaysia's "strong economic expansion despite global uncertainty and energy price fluctuations, supported by the ringgit's performance and an active capital market"

---

## Direct Investment Abroad (DIA) Signal

Malaysia DIA rose **65% quarter-on-quarter** to **RM2.8 billion** in 4Q2025.

This is a critical metric for prime property:
- Rising DIA from Malaysia → Malaysian UHNWI increasingly looking outward for investment diversification
- But KL prime residential assets "continue to hold their value" — local safe haven demand remains
- Institutional investors may look to diversification amid volatility

Ultra-mobility trend among UHNWI:
- Super-prime rentals growing as UHNWI become more mobile (multiple residences)
- KL benefits as a UHNWI-friendly hub (relatively low tax burden, no capital gains on long-term property, good lifestyle infrastructure)

---

## Implications for KL Prime Residential

1. **+1.1% appreciation is modest but stable** — KL luxury is not in a boom; it is a steady compounder with low downside
2. **Volume recovery is the real story** — 420,545 transactions in 2024 (+5.4%) suggests broad market health supporting the premium segment
3. **UHNWI growth supports super-prime demand** — 1,881 UHNWI by 2031 means growing pool of buyers for >RM5M properties
4. **Singapore comparison is key** — KL prime at RM1,000–1,500 PSF vs Singapore >US$6,000 PSF = extraordinary value gap; cross-border capital seeking diversification
5. **TEDUH and Real Property Development Act** — policy tailwinds if enacted; watch for legislative timeline
6. **Risk**: DIA outflow rising 65% QoQ suggests some capital finding better returns abroad; if this trend continues, it could dampen KL prime demand at the margins

---

## Confidence Notes

- **High confidence**: +1.1% appreciation figure — directly from Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026 press release
- **High confidence**: UHNWI population figures — Knight Frank proprietary data, widely cited
- **High confidence**: Transaction volume 420,545 (2024) — NAPIC-sourced via Knight Frank
- **Medium confidence**: UHNWI 2031 projections — forward estimates; subject to macroeconomic conditions
- **Medium confidence**: DIA figure (RM2.8bn 4Q25, +65% QoQ) — consistent with DOSM capital account data; specific quarter may have been unusual

---

## Sources Used

- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026 (p. 11)
- [[entities/areas/klcc-bukit-bintang]] — KLCC prime area context
- [[entities/areas/u-thant-embassy-row]] — U-Thant prime area context
- [[entities/areas/damansara-heights]] — DH prime area context

notes.title: Malaysia Data Centre Market — National Overview 2026

frontmatter.title: Malaysia Data Centre Market — National Overview 2026
frontmatter.question: What is the state of Malaysia's data centre market nationally, and where are the key investment hubs?

notes.content:
# Malaysia Data Centre Market — National Overview 2026

## Headline Numbers (JLL Malaysia 2Q2026)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Current national capacity | ~1,020 MW |
| End-2026 projected capacity | **2,055 MW** |
| 5-year national CAGR (since 2020) | **~70%** |
| Further pipeline beyond 2026 | 3,500 MW |
| Industrial land price (2025 national avg) | **RM86 psf** (+8.4% YoY) |

Malaysia is one of the fastest-growing data centre markets in Asia Pacific. The 2,055 MW target by end-2026 represents a **70% compound annual growth rate** since 2020 — outperforming the 17% regional (Asia Pacific) CAGR over the same period.

---

## National Capacity Map

### Hub 1: Johor Bahru — The Hyperscaler Overflow Market

The dominant hub. Full analysis in [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]].

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Completed | **850 MW** |
| Under construction | **1,800 MW** |
| Pipeline | **2,700 MW** |
| 5-year CAGR | ~5,262% (near-zero 2020 base) |
| Apac ranking | **Top 3** (alongside Tokyo, Beijing) |

**Why JB dominates:** Singapore land scarcity + Malaysia cost advantage + cross-border latency within tolerance (~5–10ms). Microsoft, Google, and other hyperscalers committed major capex in JB during 2023–2025. JB's growth is structural — not a short-term speculative build.

**Key constraint:** Grid wait times up to 8 years in core JB zones. Operators pursuing dedicated substation deals with TNB and solar/renewable PPAs.

---

### Hub 2: Cyberjaya / Klang Valley — The Established Colocation Market

The original hub; mature and stable rather than high-growth.

**Why Cyberjaya:** MSC Malaysia designation (1997); purpose-built digital infrastructure zone; strong fibre connectivity; established neutral carrier hotels; government-linked operators (TM, Maxis, Celcom/Digi, AIMS).

**Character:** More retail colocation and telco-neutral (vs. JB's wholesale hyperscaler-dominated build). Enterprises and mid-sized companies (not hyperscalers) are the primary tenants.

**Estimated capacity:** Not broken out separately in JLL 2Q2026 report; assumed to contribute 100–200 MW of the national base capacity. New-build activity is lower than JB.

**Key operators:** AIMS, ST Telemedia (STT GDC), Equinix, Time dotCom, TM ONE

---

### Hub 3: Emerging — Penang, Selangor (Non-Cyberjaya), Sabah/Sarawak

**Penang:**
- Driven by semiconductor and electronics manufacturing ecosystem
- Edge DC demand from tech manufacturers (Intel, Micron, Broadcom)
- Not a hyperscaler market; more enterprise/industrial edge compute
- Land prices elevated but less constrained than JB

**Shah Alam / Subang / Sepang (Selangor):**
- Proximity to KLIA makes Sepang Aeropolis corridor potentially interesting for edge DC nodes (low-latency connectivity for air cargo logistics, aerospace data)
- SD Guthrie's 2,500-acre Sepang industrial park ([[entities/areas/sepang]]) could attract DC operators as a tenant class
- No major announced DC projects as of April 2026

**Sabah / Sarawak:**
- Submarine cable landing stations create natural connectivity nodes
- State government interest in DC development for e-government workloads
- Very early stage; limited hyperscaler interest given distance from Singapore and KL

---

## Demand Drivers

### 1. AI Compute — The New Demand Multiplier

Generative AI and large language model inference is driving a step-change in power demand per rack:

| Workload Type | Power Density (kW/rack) |
|---------------|------------------------|
| Traditional enterprise IT | 5–10 kW |
| Cloud compute (pre-AI) | 10–20 kW |
| AI inference (2024–2026) | 40–100+ kW |
| Next-gen AI clusters | 100–300+ kW |

Every MW of AI-capable capacity generates significantly more DC revenue per MW than traditional IT — driving both new-build demand and retrofit capex.

### 2. Singapore Overflow

Singapore has effectively capped large-scale DC development due to land constraints and energy considerations. Malaysian land — especially JB — is the primary relief valve for Singapore-based hyperscalers and enterprises seeking physical infrastructure within latency tolerance.

### 3. 5G and Edge Compute

5G rollout requires distributed edge nodes (smaller DCs close to population centres) for low-latency applications (autonomous vehicles, industrial IoT, gaming). Malaysia's 5G deployment (single-entity model via DNB) is creating edge DC demand in major cities.

### 4. Digital Economy Policy

12th Malaysia Plan (2021–2025) and ongoing 13th Malaysia Plan framework emphasise digital infrastructure investment. Government commitments to cloud adoption (cloud-first policy) increase public sector workload placed in DCs.

### 5. Cross-Border Data Sovereignty

Regional data localisation policies are driving companies to build Malaysian DCs for Malaysian data — rather than routing through Singapore. PDPA (Personal Data Protection Act) compliance requirements are a modest but growing driver.

---

## Key Operators in Malaysia

| Operator | Type | Primary Hub | Notes |
|----------|------|-------------|-------|
| **Hyperscalers** (Microsoft, Google, AWS, Alibaba) | Build-to-own | JB | Massive capex 2023–2026; proprietary (not investable) |
| **Nxera** (ex-GDS/Bridge DC) | Wholesale colo | JB | Pan-Asian operator; aggressive JB expansion |
| **Digital Edge** | Regional colo | Singapore-based | Exploring US$10B sale (Apr 2026); may have Malaysian exposure via regional platform |
| **Equinix** | Global colo | KL / Cyberjaya | Established retail colo |
| **ST Telemedia GDC** | Wholesale | Cyberjaya | Singapore-linked; enterprise focus |
| **AIMS Data Centre** | Retail colo | Cyberjaya | Malaysia's original independent DC operator |
| **TM ONE / TIME** | Telco-linked | Cyberjaya / KL | Carrier hotel + managed DC |
| **YTL Power / YTL Green Data Centre** | Conglomerate | JB (Johor) | YTL committed major Johor DC investment; green-powered |

---

## Investment Thesis — Summary

### Bull Case

1. **Structural demand**: AI capex cycle is early innings; hyperscalers have committed to multi-year, multi-billion investment programmes in SEA
2. **Land scarcity in Singapore**: JB's comparative advantage is durable — Singapore cannot physically expand
3. **Government support**: Malaysia DC investment welcomed across federal and state levels; incentives available
4. **Power cost advantage**: TNB industrial tariffs remain competitive vs. Singapore; renewable energy growing
5. **UHNWI and DIA growth** ([[market-analysis/2026-04-29-kl-luxury-residential-uhnwi-2025-2026]]): Wealth creation → enterprise tech adoption → DC demand

### Bear Case / Risks

1. **Grid bottleneck** — up to 8 years for TNB connection in core markets; real development pipeline constrained
2. **Technology risk** — AI workloads evolve rapidly; today's DC spec may need expensive retrofit in 5–10 years
3. **Singapore DC policy reversal** — if Singapore relaxes DC restrictions, some JB demand could return to the island
4. **Overbuilding** — pipeline of 3,500 MW beyond 2026 is very large; absorption depends on continued hyperscaler capex
5. **US-China tech decoupling** — if Chinese hyperscalers (Alibaba, ByteDance) face restrictions in Malaysia, one demand source diminishes
6. **Cooling water usage** — growing ESG scrutiny on water consumption for cooling; may trigger regulatory limits

---

## Market Intelligence — What to Watch

- **TNB grid capacity releases**: Announcements of new substations or capacity upgrades in JB/Cyberjaya are leading indicators of new DC supply coming to market
- **Hyperscaler CAPEX announcements**: Microsoft, Google, AWS quarterly CAPEX guidance contains SEA data centre signals
- **Industrial land transactions in JB**: Large parcels (>10 acres) transacting at high PSF suggest DC pre-commitment
- **Digital Edge sale process**: If US$10B transaction completes, new owner's strategy for Malaysia assets will clarify the market's institutional pricing
- **YTL Green DC milestones**: YTL's Johor facility is the largest single commitment; progress signals real absorption

---

## Confidence Notes

- **High confidence**: National MW capacity figures from JLL Malaysia 2Q2026 press conference
- **High confidence**: JB hub metrics (850 / 1,800 / 2,700 MW) — JLL direct data
- **Medium confidence**: Cyberjaya capacity estimate — not broken out by JLL; inferred from market knowledge
- **Medium confidence**: Operator list — reflects general market knowledge as of 2026; individual project details may vary
- **Low confidence**: Penang/Sabah edge DC estimates — no quantitative data ingested

---

## Sources Used

- [[sources/2026-04-29-the-edge-ceo-morning-brief-issue-1144]] — JLL 2Q2026 data (p. 5)
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]] — Johor deep-dive
- [[concepts/data-centre-real-estate]] — Asset class framework
- [[entities/areas/johor]] — Johor area context
- [[entities/areas/sepang]] — Emerging Selangor industrial corridor

notes.title: overview
notes.content:
# Malaysia Property Market — Overview

> High-level synthesis of the Malaysian property market. Updated by the LLM when major new sources are ingested or when you run "Overview update".

Last updated: 2026-04-29
Source count: 8 (KLSP2040 + KLDCP 2024 Vols 1 Part 1/2/3 + Vol 2 + UrbanMetry D'Sara Heights Resi + Retail/Offices + HB Advisor 2Q26 + Avaland U-Thant)

---

## Market Structure

Malaysia's property market is fragmented by state, regulated at both federal and state levels, and heavily influenced by government intervention (HOC campaigns, bumiputera quotas, RPGT policy). The three dominant urban markets are:

- **[[entities/areas/klang-valley|Klang Valley]]** — the largest, most liquid market; driven by employment, infrastructure (MRT/LRT), and foreign buyer activity in the RM 1M+ segment
- **[[entities/areas/penang|Penang]]** — undersupply of land on the island creates premiums; George Town heritage zone adds complexity; mainland offers more affordable industrial and residential land
- **[[entities/areas/johor|Johor/JB]]** — heavily influenced by Singapore spillover demand; Iskandar Malaysia a recurring theme; currency differential makes it attractive to Singaporeans

---

## Macro Themes (as of 2026)

### Demand Drivers
- Urbanisation continues to push demand in KV and JB
- Singapore-Malaysia RTS Link (opening expected 2026/2027) boosting JB corridor demand
- Data centre investment wave driving industrial land demand in Selangor, Johor
- Affordable housing segment (below RM 500k) consistently undersupplied

### Supply Concerns
- High-rise condo overhang persists in KL city centre and Johor Bahru
- SOHO and serviced apartment glut in some KV submarkets
- Industrial: tight supply in established parks (Shah Alam, Bukit Raja, Nusajaya)

### Policy Environment
- RPGT: 0% for disposals after Year 5 for Malaysian citizens/PR (as of 2024 onwards) — verify current regime
- Foreign buyer threshold: RM 1M (residential) for most states; RM 2M in certain Johor areas
- Bumiputera lots: discount 5–15%, release mechanism varies by state/developer
- HOC: check current status — has been reactivated periodically

### Financing
- OPR (Overnight Policy Rate): check current BNM rate — affects mortgage rates
- Typical mortgage rate: OPR + 1.5–2.0% (floating), or fixed packages from select banks
- DSR (Debt Service Ratio) cap: typically 60–70% depending on bank and income level
- Maximum loan margin: 90% for first two properties, 70% for third and beyond

---

## Asset Class Snapshot

### Residential
- **KV city condos** (broad KV range): RM 700–1,500 PSF (prime — KLCC/Bukit Bintang reaches RM 1,000–2,500+ PSF); RM 400–700 PSF (mid — Bangsar/PJ); RM 250–450 PSF (mass market — Shah Alam/Cyberjaya)
- **[[entities/areas/damansara-heights|Damansara Heights]] luxury segment** (UrbanMetry July 2025): New launches ≥1,500 sf at RM 1,400–1,700 PSF; subsale median RM 1.7M for ≥1,500 sf. Commands ~2x Mont Kiara PSF and ~1.5x KLCC PSF. XL units (≥2,000 sf) show highest capital appreciation since 2020. 87% new launch take-up rate. Study area has 29,414 incoming units — significant supply pipeline.
- **KV landed (freehold)**: premium; mid-valley terraced from RM 800k–2M+
- **Penang island condos**: RM 600–1,200 PSF (prime); landed very limited and expensive
- **JB/Iskandar condos**: RM 300–600 PSF; oversupplied at midrange; Forest City struggling
- **Gross yields**: typically 3–5% (KV/Penang), 4–6% (JB); net yields subtract 1.5–2.5%

### Commercial (Retail/Shop Lots)
- Shop lots in established KV townships: RM 500–1,500 PSF depending on location
- Retail space: challenging post-e-commerce; location and anchor tenant critical; Greater KL at 7.4 sf/capita retail (vs Singapore 5.8 — oversupplied)
- Mall performance highly sensitive to age (peak at 10–15 years post-opening) and non-halal eatery mix (UrbanMetry 2025)
- Strata offices: newer (post-2015) buildings yield ~4.5% vs ~6% for older stock; Pavilion D'Sara Heights highest at RM 7.50 PSF rent; most stock under RM 5 PSF
- Gross yields: 4–6% for well-located shop lots

### Industrial
- Shah Alam / Klang industrial: RM 80–200 PSF (building) depending on spec
- Iskandar industrial parks: RM 50–120 PSF
- Penang industrial (Batu Kawan, Prai): competitive with Singapore satellite manufacturing
- Demand surge from data centres, EV supply chain, semiconductor expansion
- Gross yields: 5–8% for industrial; tight supply driving capital appreciation

### Land
- Development land pricing highly location-dependent and less liquid
- Industrial land: RM 15–60 PSF in established corridors
- Residential development land: RM 10–50 PSF (outskirts KV)

---

## KL Planning Framework (from KLDCP 2024 ingest — May 2025 gazette)

KL is now governed by a comprehensively updated development control framework. Key investor-relevant data:

- **KL 2040 population target:** 2.35 million; **housing target:** 762,500 units (40% affordable)
- **Affordable housing gap:** Only 40,022 affordable units today vs 305,000 target — massive undersupply
- **Land scarcity:** Only 554.5 ha of barrier-free developable land vs 1,149 ha needed — high-density is the only viable path
- **Max plot ratio:** CCC 1:10; MC/C/MX 1:8; R3 1:4; R4 1:6
- **TPZ bonus:** Up to +20% additional plot ratio within 400m of Cat 1 transit stations (KL Sentral, TRX, Bukit Bintang MRT, KLCC, Pasar Seni, Merdeka)
- **SOHO & Serviced Apartments are Commercial class (A10/A11)** — not residential; higher utility and tax costs
- **MRT3 Circle Line (proposed):** 42 km / 28 stations in KL; biggest untapped TOD play if funded

See [[concepts/kl-development-control]], [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]], [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]] for full detail.

---

## Watch List

- [ ] **MRT3 funding and timeline** — enormous impact on property values along corridor (Mont Kiara, Titiwangsa, Setapak, Cheras)
- [ ] **Bandar Malaysia developer status** — former RMAF Sungai Besi; future HSR terminal; Cat 1 TPZ
- [ ] **KL–JB HSR** — current status; multiple past revivals; Bandar Malaysia as terminal
- [ ] **Kampong Bharu amalgamation** — Corp-led lot consolidation deals; watch for announcements
- [ ] Data centre land demand — track which areas developers are buying (Shah Alam, Cyberjaya, Johor)
- [ ] RTS Link effect on JB residential (Forest City, Medini, Danga Bay)
- [ ] Industrial park vacancy rates in Shah Alam / Bukit Raja
- [ ] Penang island land scarcity vs. mainland growth (Batu Kawan, Seberang Prai)
- [ ] HOC reactivation — check current status
- [ ] BNM OPR direction — rate cut cycle implications for property
- [ ] Affordable housing price caps under current government policy (KLSP2040 references B40/M40 bands but current RM caps subject to change)

---

## Related Pages

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]] | [[entities/areas/damansara-heights]] | [[entities/areas/mont-kiara]] | [[entities/areas/penang]] | [[entities/areas/johor]]
- [[concepts/rpgt]] | [[concepts/bumiputera-quota]] | [[concepts/foreign-ownership-rules]]
