notes.title: UrbanMetry Market Intelligence — Damansara Heights Residential (17 July 2025)

frontmatter.title: UrbanMetry Market Intelligence — Damansara Heights Residential (17 July 2025)

notes.content:
## Summary

A comprehensive 93-page market intelligence report by UrbanMetry covering the Damansara Heights study boundary (including Mont Kiara, Taman Duta, Bukit Damansara, TTDI, Bukit Kiara, Bangsar, and KLCC). The report analyses demographics, T10 geo-behaviour patterns using mobile data, amenities, residential stock (existing and incoming), supply-demand dynamics, price trends by size segments, product analysis of competing developments, and a detailed competitor comparison of 12 incoming condominiums. The study area is an affluent enclave with median household income of RM 24,000/month (double KL average), predominantly Chinese population (42%), and a clear premium for Damansara Heights over Mont Kiara and KLCC in PSF pricing for large units.

## Key Data Points

### Demographics
- Study area population (2020 Census): 324,647; Base case 2030 projection: 364,152 (CAGR 1.15%)
- Median household income: RM 24,000/month (vs KL district RM 10,272)
- T10 threshold: RM 21,500/month; T5: RM 28,047; T1: RM 47,686
- Chinese population: 42% (higher than KL average); non-Malaysians: 14%

### Supply
- Total incoming residential units: 29,414 (24% increase over next 6 years)
- 99% of incoming stock is highrise; only 1% landed
- Incoming highrise with APDL: 18,549 units; with DO only: 8,629 units
- Cancelled projects relieving pressure: Taman Rimba Kiara, Malola Garden Bukit Nanas
- Annual household formation (base case 2025–2028): 2,169 households
- Average annual highrise supply (2025–2028): 5,294 units — supply outpacing demand significantly

### Demand & Absorption (≥1,500 sf, 2021–2024)
- Annual all private highrise absorption: 1,147 units
- Annual new sales absorption: 517 units (87% take-up rate)
- Annual subsale absorption: 630 units
- RM 1M–2M segment: 581 units/year (49% new vs subsale)
- RM 2M–3M segment: 257 units/year (49% new)
- RM 3M–5M segment: 113 units/year (56% new)
- >RM 5M segment: 63 units/year (40% new)

### Pricing
- Subsale median (all private highrise): RM 1.1 million
- Subsale median (≥1,500 sf): RM 1.7 million; 19% above RM 2M
- New launch median (all): RM 1.3 million (20% premium over subsale)
- New launch median (≥1,500 sf): RM 1.9 million; 41% above RM 2M
- Damansara Heights PSF premium: close to double Mont Kiara, 50% higher than KLCC for ≥1,500 sf units <20 years old
- Bukit Damansara new sales: RM 1,400–1,700 PSF (≥1,500 sf)
- KLCC new sales: RM 2,300 PSF (≥1,500 sf)
- Mont Kiara new sales: under RM 1,000 PSF (≥1,500 sf)
- XL-sized (≥2,000 sf) units have highest appreciation since 2020
- Units <10 years have 10% premium over 10–15 year old; <20 years have 20% premium over 20+ years

### Competitor Projects (Selected, with APDL)

| Project | Developer | Tenure | Units | Size Range (sf) | Nett Start / PSF | SPA Signed |
|---------|-----------|--------|-------|-----------------|------------------|------------|
| Jendela @ KLGCC | Sime Darby Property | LH/Commercial | 520 | 1,324–2,863 | RM 1.25M / RM 947 | 98% |
| The Ophera @ KLGCC | Sime Darby Property | LH/Residential | 150 | 3,079–7,523 | RM 4.4M / RM 1,429 | 47% |
| Papyrus North Kiara | Kerjaya Prospek | FH/Residential | 454 | 1,141–5,523 | RM 770k / RM 675 | 46% |
| Kiaramas Dedaun Ph2 | Asia Quest Holdings | FH/Residential | 223 | 1,313–2,777 | RM 1.05M / RM 797 | 31% |
| Kiaramas Dedaun Ph1 | Asia Quest Holdings | FH/Residential | 436 | 1,313–4,693 | RM 1.03M / RM 784 | 68% |
| Pavilion Mont Kiara | WCT Land | FH/Residential | 336 | 1,001–2,530 | RM 1.2M / RM 1,199 | 99% |
| The Minh Tower A | UEM Sunrise | FH/Residential | 245 | 1,607–3,010 | RM 1.3M / RM 809 | 87% |
| The Minh Tower B | UEM Sunrise | FH/Residential | 251 | 1,607–3,010 | RM 1.3M / RM 809 | 90% |
| Miranda Hill | BRDB | FH/Residential | 552 | 943–2,200 | RM 657k / RM 697 | 89% |
| The Peak Villa | E&O | FH/Residential | 54 | 3,455–4,500 | RM 5.5M / RM 1,592 | 74% |
| The Cedar | Satin Magic | FH/Residential | 70 | 2,782–16,248 | RM 4.45M / RM 1,599 | 73% |
| Bon Kiara | Bon Estates | FH/Residential | 410 | 2,081–6,226 | RM 1.3M / RM 625 | 90% |

### Travel Times (from site, 8am outbound)
- Bangsar: 15 min; Mont Kiara: 18 min; Bukit Bintang: 22 min; Batu Caves: 26 min; Subang Jaya: 40 min

## Notable Quotes

- "The Damansara Heights premium for units over 1,500 psf is apparent in this map where the prices for subsale units are close to double that in Mont Kiara and 50% higher than those in KLCC."
- "XL-sized (≥ 2,000 sf) units have the highest appreciation in the area since 2020."
- "There are a larger number of over 2,000 sf units in Damansara Heights, provided by The Cedar and The Peak Villa, which have achieved strong sales despite the price tag."
- "Several large projects in the pipeline have been cancelled, relieving the pressure in the housing market, namely: Taman Rimba Kiara [and] Malola Garden Bukit Nanas."
- "SDB Damansara development in the landed enclave is unlikely to proceed at the gazette for a maximum of three stories, while the developer has requested 7 stories."

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/damansara-heights]] — new page created
- [[entities/areas/mont-kiara]] — competitor projects, pricing context
- [[entities/developers/sime-darby-property]] — Jendela, The Ophera projects
- [[entities/developers/uem-sunrise]] — The Minh project
- [[entities/developers/e-and-o]] — The Peak Villa
- [[entities/developers/wct-land]] — Pavilion Mont Kiara
- [[entities/developers/kerjaya-prospek]] — Papyrus North Kiara
- [[entities/developers/brdb]] — Miranda Hill
- [[overview]] — Damansara Heights pricing data

## Open Questions

- What is the exact site being studied? Report references "Plaza D'Sara" at 1.6 km and benchmarks competitors by distance from it — likely a new development site near Plaza Damansara
- What are the latest MRT Circle Line construction timelines for Bukit Kiara Selatan station?
- What is the current occupancy rate of Pavilion Damansara Heights Phase 1 (only 51% SPA signed)?
- Will SDB Damansara proceed given the 3-storey gazette vs 7-storey request?

notes.title: UrbanMetry Market Intelligence (Commercial) — Damansara Heights Retail & Offices (17 July 2025)

frontmatter.title: UrbanMetry Market Intelligence (Commercial) — Damansara Heights Retail & Offices (17 July 2025)

notes.content:
## Summary

A 47-page commercial market intelligence report by UrbanMetry covering the Damansara Heights study area, focused on two segments: mall/retail analysis and strata office analysis. The retail section uses mobile footfall data to evaluate 12+ shopping malls, finding that retail performance is highly sensitive to mall age (peak at 10–15 years), with an inverse correlation between footfall and competition density within 5 km. Bukit Damansara and Bangsar malls attract higher-income (T10/T5) visitors but with lower footfall volume vs Mont Kiara. The office section maps 138 Bursa-listed companies within the study boundary (45% of total KLSE market cap), analyses strata office rental trends (west zone RM 4.50 PSF, east zone RM 4.81 PSF), and documents en bloc office lease rates from RM 3.80 to RM 12.50 PSF across KL.

## Key Data Points

### Retail/Mall Analysis

**Market Context:**
- Greater KL retail space: 7.4 sf per capita (vs Singapore 5.8 sf/capita) — already stressed
- Incoming retail NLA in pipeline within study area: 4.6 million sf
- Site would face total 18.3 million sf of retail NLA within 5 km by 2029

**Footfall Estimates for Site:**
- Estimated 270,000 monthly footfall based on 18.3M sf competition within 5 km
- Estimated maximum absorbable retail NLA: 170,000 sf (to match BSC performance) to 427,000 sf (average performer)

**Mall Performance Rankings (by monthly footfall):**

| Mall | Avg Monthly Footfall | Weekday-only % | Weekend-only % | Both % |
|------|---------------------|----------------|----------------|--------|
| Plaza Arkadia | 928,950 | 45% | 55% | 21% |
| Sunway 163 Mall | 675,750 | 48% | 52% | 22% |
| 1 Mont Kiara | 619,800 | 53% | 47% | 19% |
| Hartamas Shopping Centre | 618,750 | 53% | 47% | 16% |
| The Waterfront | 569,400 | 42% | 58% | 15% |
| Plaza Damansara Shopoffice | 527,850 | 56% | 44% | 13% |
| Bangsar Shopping Centre | 525,150 | 55% | 45% | 13% |
| Bangsar Village | 443,850 | 49% | 51% | 13% |
| DC Mall | 199,350 | 63% | 37% | 9% |
| Plaza Batai | 184,350 | 51% | 49% | 12% |
| Pavilion D'Sara Heights (Ph1) | 121,650 | 60% | 40% | 23% |
| The Five @ KPD | 100,650 | 86% | 14% | 6% |

**Mall Affluence (T5 % of visitors):**
- Plaza Batai: 37% T5 (highest); Plaza Damansara: 34%; Bangsar Shopping Centre: 33%
- Pavilion D'Sara Heights: 18% T5; Mont Kiara malls: 21–24% T5
- Higher-income malls have lower travel distance (local catchment), except Bukit Damansara extends to 6–9 km

**Mall NLA & Occupancy:**

| Mall | NLA (sf) | Completion | Occupancy (NLA) | Anchor |
|------|----------|------------|------------------|--------|
| Pavilion D'Sara Heights (Ph1) | 533,361 | 2023 | 53% | Food Merchant |
| Plaza Arkadia | 430,000 | 2016 | 90% | Village Grocer |
| Nexus Bangsar South | 350,000 | 2014 | 91% | The Oak Room |
| Publika | 335,000 | 2011 | 83% | B.I.G. |
| BSC | 330,000 | 1990 | 94% | BSC Fine Foods |
| Bangsar Village I & II | 292,000 | 2004 | 88% | Village Grocer |
| Sunway 163 Mall | 255,535 | 2018 | 95% | Jaya Grocer |
| 1 Mont Kiara | 241,781 | 2010 | 75% | Village Grocer |
| Hartamas Shopping Centre | 200,000 | 2006 | 83% | Village Grocer |
| DC Mall | 170,000 | 2017 | 41% | Jaya Grocer |
| The Waterfront | 124,471 | 2007 | 99% | Aeon MaxValu Prime |
| The Sphere | 110,000 | 2010 | 81% | Aeon MaxValu Prime |

**Key Retail Insight:** Higher percentage of non-halal eateries correlates with higher occupied NLA; higher eatery NLA correlates with more repeat visitors.

### Office Analysis

**Listed Company Concentration:**
- 138 Bursa-listed companies in study boundary with RM 828 billion market cap (45.21% of total KLSE)
- Top sectors: Financial Services (RM 314B), Utilities (RM 182B), Consumer Products (RM 78B), Technology (RM 63B)

**Strata Office Stock:**
- Completed strata office NLA: ~11.8 million sf
- Incoming pipeline to 2029: 1.8 million sf NLA
- Key incoming: Plaza Conlay (2026/2028), D'Immersione Office Suites (2027), Solaris Parq (2029)

**Strata Office Rental & Pricing:**
- Pavilion Bukit Damansara Heights: RM 7.50 PSF (highest in study area)
- Mont Kiara strata offices: RM 5.00 PSF
- Downtown KL / Bangsar: RM 5.00 PSF
- Most older buildings: under RM 5.00 PSF (oversupply)
- West zone asking rental: RM 4.50 PSF; East zone: RM 4.81 PSF
- West zone asking price: RM 708 PSF; East zone: RM 850 PSF
- Newer (post-2015) strata offices: 20% rental premium, but yield only ~4.5% vs ~6% for older units
- Office yields can match 6% if selling prices stay below RM 850 PSF
- Rental inversely correlated with distance to nearest mall (RM 1 PSF premium per 1.5 km closer)

**En Bloc Office Lease Rates (selected):**

| Building | NLA (sf) | Occupancy | Rental (RM PSF) |
|----------|----------|-----------|------------------|
| Menara PJD | 445,111 | 100% | 3.80 |
| Menara TM | 989,202 | >95% | 5.20 |
| Menara UOA Bangsar | 400,000 | 94% | 6.00 |
| Menara Prudential @ TRX | 560,000 | 90% | 8.00 |
| Menara Maybank | 585,527 | 100% | 8.80 |
| Menara Maxis | 528,000 | >90% | 9.00 |
| Petronas Twin Towers | 2,170,000 | 100% | 10.80 |
| Merdeka 118 | 1,700,000 | 70% | 11.50 |
| Exchange 106 (TRX) | 2,600,000 | 62% | 12.50 |

## Notable Quotes

- "Greater Klang Valley already experiences stress in the shopping mall stock market with 7.4 sf per capita as compared to Singapore at 5.8 sf per capita."
- "For retail centres within the study boundary, foot traffic needs time to build up, with peak performance expected around 10-15 years after opening."
- "Malls with higher % of T20 visitors has lower home travel distances."
- "Office unit yields can match the 6% yield expectations if selling prices remain below RM 850 psf."
- "For every 1.5 km distance to a shopping mall, office rentals are higher by RM 1 psf."

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/damansara-heights]] — retail, office, mall data
- [[entities/areas/mont-kiara]] — mall footfall, strata office context
- [[entities/areas/bangsar]] — mall performance data (BSC, Bangsar Village)
- [[overview]] — commercial market data enrichment

## Open Questions

- What is the expected completion date for Pavilion Damansara Heights Phase 2?
- Will DC Mall's 41% NLA occupancy improve or is it structurally challenged?
- What are current en bloc rental rates for newer buildings like Menara IGB and Naza Tower vs 2023 data cited?
- How will the MRT Circle Line impact strata office demand in Bukit Damansara?

notes.title: BRDB U-Thant (Langgak Golf) — Marketing Brief (Brainstorming)
notes.content:
## Summary

Internal marketing brainstorming brief prepared by BRDB for their upcoming **U-Thant (Langgak Golf)** project — a planned 85-unit, low-density, low-rise freehold residential development on a 1.54-acre site at Seksyen 89, Jalan U-Thant, Kuala Lumpur. Dated 6 February 2026 (revised 22 April 2026). The brief covers a competitor market survey of 15 projects in the U-Thant / Embassy Row corridor, proposed unit mix with pricing at ~MYR 1,500 PSF, target buyer profiling, design fit-out specifications, and brand strategy built around "Rhythm-Inspired Living" anchored on golf course landscape.

## Key Data Points

### Project Fundamentals
- **Site**: Lot 171, 175, 176, 186 & 188, Seksyen 89, Jalan U-Thant, KL
- **Land size**: 1.54 acres, Freehold, Residential title
- **Plot ratio**: 3.84
- **Total units**: 85 (low-density, low-rise)
- **Pricing target**: ~MYR 1,500 PSF

### Unit Mix

| Type | Units | Built-Up (SF) | Avg Price | Bedrooms | Baths | Car Parks |
|------|-------|---------------|-----------|----------|-------|-----------|
| A    | 40    | 2,080         | MYR 3.12M | 3+1      | 4     | 3         |
| B    | 29    | 2,850         | MYR 4.27M | 4+1      | 5     | 3         |
| C    | 11    | 3,800         | MYR 5.70M | 4+1+1    | 6     | 4         |
| D    | 5     | 5,000         | MYR 6.40M | 4+1+1    | 6     | 4 (garden terrace) |

### Competitor Survey (Ongoing)

| Project | Developer | Units | Sizes (SF) | PSF (MYR) | Tenure | Take-Up | Completion | Storeys | Maint (PSF) |
|---------|-----------|-------|-----------|-----------|--------|---------|------------|---------|-------------|
| Impression U-Thant | Yong Tai | 108 | 931–1,632 | 1,300–1,500 | Freehold | 95% | Dec 2025 | 10 (1 blk) | 0.77 |
| Skyline Embassy | TS Law Land | 1,296 | 521–976 | 1,350 | Freehold | 95% | 2027 | 65 (3 blk) | 0.50 |
| The Ashwood | Paramount | 354 | 1,400–3,000 | 1,200–1,300 | Freehold | 100% | 2029 | 49 (2 blk) | 0.39 |

### Competitor Survey (Other projects surveyed — completed or pipeline)
The Atrium (Paramount), Residensi R8 (Revival Capital), Pavilion Embassy (Pavilion), 18 Madge (KSL), At 6 Residence (LQS), U-Thant Place (YTL), Rimbun Embassy Row (Amphil), Dedaun (Sime Darby), Noble Crest (Yong Tai), Madge Mansions (Gamuda), Sastra U-Thant (CapitaLand), Seri Ampang Hilir (Tan & Tan)

### Design & Fit-Out Specs
- A/C: Ducted VRF (hybrid acceptable); smart home ready
- Kitchen appliances: Gaggenau (dry), Bosch (wet)
- Sanitary: Hansgrohe fittings, Villeroy & Boch bathrooms
- Flooring: Herringbone solid timber (bedrooms), compressed stone (living/dining)
- Glazing: Low-E glass
- Sustainability: GEO Certified framework; native planting, water recycling, passive solar design
- Branding: Mid-Century Modern; "Rhythm-Inspired Living"; concept-led (not marketing-led)

### Target Buyers
Owner-occupiers, expats, landed-home downsizers, design-conscious minimalists (architects, creatives, senior professionals)

## Notable Quotes

> "Sell through exclusivity, relationships and lifestyle aspiration." — Marketing brief tagline

> "An 85-unit low-density, low-rise development designed around golf course landscape, set within the quiet elegance of the embassy district"

> "Design vision is concept-led, not marketing-led. Golf rhythm provides a strong narrative backbone. Sustainability is embedded, not branded."

## Updates To

- [[entities/developers/brdb]] — added U-Thant (Langgak Golf) as active project; updated overview
- [[entities/areas/u-thant-embassy-row]] — NEW page created with competitor survey data and pricing benchmarks

## Open Questions

- Is planning approval (DA) secured for U-Thant (Langgak Golf)? Plot ratio 3.84 noted but OC/launch timeline not stated.
- Who is the appointed architect?
- Has a sales gallery location been confirmed?
- Will BRDB appoint external marketing agents or sell exclusively in-house?
- What is the launch date? (Brief dated Apr 2026 — likely H2 2026 or 2027)
- What is the status of Dedaun (Sime Darby) in this corridor?
- Full pricing data for the 12 completed/pipeline projects beyond the 3 highlighted in the ongoing survey.

notes.title: HB Advisor — April–June 2026 (2Q26)
notes.content:
## Summary

Henry Butcher Malaysia's Q2 2026 quarterly newsletter leads with a detailed review of NAPIC/JPPH's Property Market Report for 2025. Malaysia's property market hit a decade-high total transaction value of MYR 241.87 billion (+4.1% YoY) despite a slight volume dip of 1% to 416,413 transactions. The industrial sub-sector was the standout performer with value up 21.3%, driven by strong manufacturing fundamentals and Selangor's logistics dominance. Residential and commercial segments were more subdued. The newsletter also provides a granular breakdown of new residential launches in the Klang Valley across 2024 vs 2025, showing a significant 39.1% decline in units launched. A feature article on Botanik Point (Ampang) and director profiles for Henry Butcher's Pahang, Sabah, and Negeri Sembilan offices round out the edition.

## Key Data Points

### National Market 2025 (NAPIC)
- **Total transaction value:** MYR 241.87 billion (+4.1% YoY) — decade high
- **Total transaction volume:** 416,413 units (-1.0% YoY)
- **Central region:** 23.9% of volume but **44.3% of value** (MYR 107.09 billion) — highest value concentration
- **Southern region:** 26.1% of volume, 28.5% of value (MYR 69.05 billion)
- **Northern region:** 25.1% of volume, 15.4% of value (MYR 37.36 billion)

### State Volume & Value Changes 2025 vs 2024 (Overall)
| State | Volume % | Value % |
|---|---|---|
| Kuala Lumpur | +14.2% | +6.0% |
| Negeri Sembilan | +11.2% | +26.6% |
| Kelantan | +24.3% | +11.9% |
| Sabah | +4.1% | -1.0% |
| Kedah | +2.6% | +16.3% |
| Selangor | -4.5% | +2.1% |
| Johor | -2.3% | +4.4% |
| Penang | -3.9% | -5.0% |
| Perak | -5.2% | +3.6% |
| Pahang | -12.2% | -8.0% |

### Residential Sub-Sector 2025
- Volume: -1.5% YoY; Value: +1.3% YoY
- Residential = 61.6% of transaction volume; 44.8% of value (down from 46% in 2024)
- **New launches: 64,487 units** (down ~15% from 75,784 in 2024)
- **Sales take-up: 35.5%** (down from 37.3% in 2024)
- New launches by state: Selangor 22.3%, Johor 17.3%, Perak 11%, KL (fell to 4th)
- Johor sales take-up: 55.3% (best performer among major states)
- Primary market softening; **secondary (sub-sale) market = 84.5% of transactions** (up from 83%)
- Price segment breakdown: ≤RM300k = 52.3%; RM300k-500k = 24.9%; RM500k-1M = 16.9%; >RM1M = 5.9%
- Highest growth segment: >RM1M at +6.5%
- Property type demand: Terraced 41.3%, Vacant plots 18.1%, High-rise 14.3%, Low-cost 10.6%, Semi-D 7.3%
- **Malaysian House Price Index (MHPI): +2.6% annual growth**
  - Terraced: +3.3% | Semi-D: +2.6% | Detached: +2.4% | High-rise: +0.6%
- Unsold completed stock (overhang): Perak 12.9%, Johor 12.1%, Selangor 11.6%
- Condo/apartment = 47.1% of unsold completed stock; terraced = 30.5%

### Residential by State 2025 vs 2024
| State | Volume % | Value % |
|---|---|---|
| KL | +8.7% | +26.4% |
| Negeri Sembilan | +14.7% | +0.6% |
| Sabah | +4.6% | +4.5% |
| Kedah | +4.0% | +15.7% |
| Selangor | -5.6% | -3.7% |
| Penang | -4.9% | -7.0% |
| Perak | -7.2% | -10.9% |
| Pahang | -14.6% | -17.4% |
| Johor | 0.0% | +1.4% |

### Commercial Sub-Sector 2025
- Volume: +1.4%; Value: +1.1% (major slowdown from +13.6% / +51.6% in 2024)
- Shop transactions: 41.9% of commercial volume, 33.8% of value
- Service apartments: 32% of volume, 22% of value (concentrated in KL, Selangor, Johor)
- **Shopping complex occupancy: 78.9%** (up from 78.8%)
- **Office occupancy (national average): 71.9%**
  - KL office occupancy: **70.8%**
  - Johor office occupancy: **55.1%** (low — risk of oversupply)
  - Terengganu: 92.5% (highest)
- Most states above 80% office occupancy; KL, Selangor, Johor are the exceptions

### Industrial Sub-Sector 2025
- Volume: +1.4%; **Value: +21.3%** (strong; +16.4% in 2024)
- Selangor: 35.7% of national industrial volume (+8.76% vol, +10.98% value)
- Johor: 18.1% of national industrial volume; **value +44.1%**
- Transaction composition: Terraced warehouses 33.3%, Vacant plots 27.1%, Semi-D factories 21.8%
- Negeri Sembilan industrial value: +151.9% (low base effect)
- Industrial overhang: +2.4% volume, +12.8% value (under construction +27.1%)

### Klang Valley New Launches 2025 (HB Research)
- **66 projects** launched (vs 79 in 2024) — **-16.5% YoY**
- **27,660 units** launched (vs 45,422 in 2024) — **-39.1% YoY**
- Selangor: 71% of projects (up from 65%); KL: 29% (down from 35%)
- By units: 50% Selangor / 50% KL
- High-rises = **89% of units launched**; 54% of projects highrise vs 46% landed
- Most active months: April & August 2025 (10 launches each)
- Project types: Serviced residences/apartments (20 projects, dominant), terrace/super-link (17), condominiums (10), semi-D (10)
- Unit sizes: 601–1,500 sqft most common; 49% of projects had >2,000 sqft units
- **Price tier: 57% of projects had units >RM1M** (most common tier in 2025; was RM401k-600k in 2024)
- PSF range: RM501–750 psf most common (46% of projects); below RM500 psf (31%); RM751–1,000 psf (29%)
- Shah Alam most active submarket: 12 project launches

### KL Submarket PSF Ranges (2025 Launches)
| Area | Type | PSF Range (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| KLCC | Highrise | 1,500–3,600 |
| Bangsar | Highrise | 1,200–1,300 |
| Mont Kiara | Highrise | 800–850 |
| Old Klang Road | Highrise | 800–900 |
| Bukit Jalil | Highrise | 700–1,200 |
| Cheras (KL) | Highrise | 700–1,200 |
| Cheras (KL) | Landed | 550–650 |
| Titiwangsa | Highrise | 700–750 |
| Sentul | Highrise | 600–700 |
| Segambut | Highrise | 600–800 |
| Setapak | Highrise | 600–650 |
| Sungai Besi | Highrise | 450–550 |
| KL Sentral | Highrise | 400–450 |

### Selangor Submarket PSF Ranges (2025 Launches)
| Area | Type | PSF Range (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| Petaling Jaya | Landed | 950–1,000 |
| Petaling Jaya | Highrise | 650–800 |
| Taman Melawati | Highrise | 650–900 |
| Taman Melawati | Landed | 800–900 |
| Puchong | Highrise | 700–800 |
| Puchong | Landed | 750–850 |
| Subang Jaya | Highrise | 650–950 |
| Shah Alam | Highrise | 250–850 |
| Shah Alam | Landed | 300–800 |
| Kajang | Landed | 500–700 |
| Kajang | Highrise | 450–550 |
| Klang | Landed | 500–600 |
| Klang | Highrise | 250–600 |
| Sungai Buloh | Landed | 450–550 |
| Gombak | Landed | 450–550 |
| Cheras (Sel) | Highrise | 500–550 |
| Cheras (Sel) | Landed | 400–500 |
| Rawang | Landed | 400–550 |
| Semenyih | Landed | 400–450 |
| Sepang | Landed | 400–500 |
| Puncak Alam | Landed | 300–450 |
| Cyberjaya | Landed | 350–650 |

### Botanik Point (Ampang) — Featured Project
- Developer: Urban Hallmark Properties Sdn Bhd
- Tenure: Freehold, Strata
- Units: 46 (4 semi-D >5,500 sqft with private lifts; 42 Townvillas)
- GreenRE Platinum (Provisional) certified
- 30% of site is preserved nature reserve
- Launch: June 2026 (expected); Completion: 36 months from launch
- Connectivity: 400m to MRR2; near DUKE, AKLEH
- HB Malaysia is exclusive marketing agent
- *(PSF not disclosed in article)*

### Negeri Sembilan Residential Price Benchmarks (Historical)
- Single-storey terrace (Seremban): MYR 80k–100k in 2001 → MYR 300k–400k now
- Double-storey terrace (Seremban): MYR 140k in 2001 → MYR 600k–800k now
- Kuantan single-storey terrace (20'x80'): ~MYR 100k in 2003 → ~MYR 250k now
- Kuantan high-rise: ~+50% appreciation over 23 years (2003–2026)

## Notable Quotes

> "Malaysia's overall value of transactions surged to RM241.87 billion, marking a 4.1% increase from the previous year and regarded as a decade-high milestone." — Long Shi Chuen, Director Corporate Real Estate, HB Malaysia

> "The small 1% drop in the volume of residential transactions does not seem to be a major concern at the moment unless the decline persists and worsen in 2026." — HB Research commentary

> "The office segment has, contrary to earlier expectations, shown a high degree of resilience with all states securing higher occupancy rates of more than 80% except for Selangor, Kuala Lumpur and Johor." — HB Research

> "By and large, barring any turn for the worse in the conflict in the Middle East and the global economic situation, the Malaysian property market in 2026 is expected to continue to be generally stable, albeit on a more subdued note." — HB Research

## Updates To

- [[sources/2026-04-28-hb-advisor-2q26]] ← this page
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-28-malaysia-property-market-2025-review]]
- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[entities/areas/klcc-bukit-bintang]]
- [[entities/areas/bangsar]]
- [[entities/areas/mont-kiara]]
- [[entities/areas/sentul]]
- [[entities/areas/penang]]
- [[entities/areas/johor]]
- [[entities/areas/ampang]] ← new page

## Open Questions

- What is the asking PSF for Botanik Point Townvillas specifically? (not disclosed in this article)
- How will the Middle East conflict (Hormuz closure) affect construction material costs in Malaysia through 2026?
- What is the pipeline of new office supply in KL and JB for 2026–2028? The report warns of oversupply risk.
- Will Johor industrial transactions sustain the 44% value surge or is this a one-off from a few large deals?
- Negeri Sembilan's exceptional +26.6% value growth — what specific projects or transactions drove this?

notes.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 1 Part 1 — Development Control
notes.content:
# KLDCP 2024 Vol 1 Part 1 — Development Control

## Summary

This is the core development control document for Kuala Lumpur, gazetted May 2025 as part of the KL Local Plan (KLLP2040). It replaces the earlier KLDCP2008. It sets legally binding rules for plot ratios, building heights, setbacks, density, mixed development rules, and the Transit Planning Zone (TPZ) incentive system. It is the primary reference document for any planning application in KL. Works alongside Vol 1 Part 2 (Intensity Maps) which sets site-specific plot ratios.

## Key Data Points

### Plot Ratios by Zone (Base — site-specific from Intensity Map)

| Zone | Code | Max Plot Ratio |
|------|------|---------------|
| City Centre Commercial | CCC | 1:10 |
| Major Commercial | MC | 1:8 |
| Commercial | C | 1:8 |
| Local Commercial | LC | 1:2 |
| Mixed Development | MX | 1:8 |
| Industrial Park | IP | 1:4 |
| Mixed Industrial | MXI | 1:4 |
| Technology Park | TP | 1:3 |
| Residential 2 | R2 | 1:2.5 |
| Residential 3 | R3 | 1:4 |
| Residential 4 (Affordable) | R4 | 1:6 |

R1, EH, TV1, Open Space zones: plot ratio does NOT apply — height control used instead.

### Plot Ratios Along Key Roads (Commercial)

| Road | Plot Ratio |
|------|-----------|
| Jalan Tun Razak | 1:8 |
| Jalan Klang Lama | 1:6 |
| Jalan Ampang | 1:6 |
| Jalan Cheras | 1:5 |
| Jalan Puchong / Kepong / Ipoh / Syed Putra / Kuchai Lama / Gombak / Pahang / Genting Klang | 1:4 |

### Building Height Limits

| Zone | Height |
|------|--------|
| CCC (general) | Up to 14 storeys |
| CCC / MC (Secondary Heritage Zone, heritage shophouses) | Up to 7 storeys |
| MC (major commercial outside City Centre) | Up to 8 storeys |
| MC (Special Character Zones — Jln TAR, Masjid India, Chow Kit) | Up to 10 storeys |
| C (Commercial, outside city centre) | 2–6 storeys |
| LC (Local Commercial) | Up to 4 storeys |
| R1 / EH / TV1 (Landed residential) | Up to 3 storeys (landed strata: 4 levels) |

### TPZ/TIZ Incentive System

- **TPZ (Transit Planning Zone):** within **400m of station box**
- **TIZ (Transit Influence Zone):** within **600m of station box**
- Total stations covered: **103 stations**
- Must have direct physical connection to station + at least 1 of 9 supporting criteria

| Category | TPZ (400m) | TIZ (600m) |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Cat 1 (major interchanges: KL Sentral, TRX, Bukit Bintang MRT, Pasar Seni, Merdeka) | +20% plot ratio | +15% |
| Cat 2 (interchange stations) | +15% | +10% |
| Cat 3 (single transit stations) | +10% | +7.5% |
| Cat 4 (lower category) | +5% | +5% |

### Density / Unit Size Rules
- **Minimum unit size (free market):** 450 sq ft
- **Large unit threshold (PPA applies instead of plot ratio):** >1,600 sq ft; only at 5 locations: Mont Kiara, Jalan Robson, Jalan U-Thant, Bukit Pantai, Bukit Bangsar

### Mixed Development (MX Zone)
- Residential component is **mandatory** for large lots (>20,000 sq ft) unless commercial ≤ 40% of GFA
- Small lots (<20,000 sq ft in originally residential areas): commercial-only permissible with restricted use classes
- Typical ratio when mixed: **60% residential : 40% commercial**

### River / Lake Setbacks
- Category I & II rivers: **10m setback** (plus 7m public easement + 3m transition)
- Category III rivers: **7m setback** (plus 4m public easement + 3m transition)
- Lakes and ponds: **10m setback** minimum
- No permanent structures or vehicles in Public Easement Zone

### Redevelopment Incentive
- Brownfield / greyfield / dilapidated residential redevelopment: plot ratio uplift available
- At least **30% of entire redevelopment** must be mixed-income housing including affordable
- Affordable housing incentive: R4 base plot ratio up to 1:6.0

### Affordable Housing (RMM)
- Minimum site area: 30,000 sq ft to qualify for plot ratio incentive
- Must include: 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, kitchen, living/dining, yard, 1 parking space
- 5% of affordable units should be elderly-friendly

## Notable Quotes

> On minimum unit size: "450 square feet (for free market; applicable for DINK, YUPPIES, senior citizens)"
> On MX zones large lots: commercial activities can reach 40% of total GFA without requiring residential component, but beyond 40% commercial, residential is mandatory.

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[concepts/kl-development-control]]
- [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]]

## Open Questions

- What is the full list of Category 1 vs Category 2 vs Category 3 TPZ stations? (Cat 1 MRT stations identified; full list in KLDCP maps)
- What are specific car parking standards per use class? (referenced to separate KLCH guidelines, not in this document)
- Are there specific height controls around Istana Negara?

notes.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 1 Part 2 — Land Use Zone and Intensity Maps

frontmatter.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 1 Part 2 — Land Use Zone and Intensity Maps

notes.content:
# KLDCP 2024 Vol 1 Part 2 — Land Use Zone and Intensity Maps

## Summary

This volume contains the official zoning maps and site-specific intensity (plot ratio) maps for all land parcels in Kuala Lumpur. As a maps-heavy document (568 pages), the spatial data is the primary content. It is the reference for determining the exact zone and permitted plot ratio for any specific land parcel in KL — the numbers in Vol 1 Part 1 are the maximums; the actual approved plot ratio for a specific site is read from these maps (DCP2 — Intensity Map).

## Key Data Points

- This document contains **DCP1 — Land Use Zone Map** (zone classification per parcel) and **DCP2 — Intensity Map** (specific plot ratio per parcel)
- All land in KL is classified into one of the zones defined in Vol 1 Part 1 and Vol 1 Part 3
- Site-specific plot ratios in the DCP2 map may be **lower** than the base maximums in Vol 1 Part 1 — the DCP2 figure is the binding one
- Digital access via DBKL OSC (One Stop Centre) system or physical reference at DBKL

## Key Zones Identified Across KL

Major commercial (CCC/MC) clusters: KLCC corridor, Bukit Bintang, KL Sentral surrounds, TRX/Pudu, Bangsar South
Residential zones: Majority of KL area is R1 (landed residential), R3/R4 (high-density residential) in dense areas
Industrial: Segambut, Kepong, Cheras (light industry), Chan Sow Lin, Sungai Besi

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[concepts/kl-development-control]]

## Open Questions

- Digital access to DCP2 intensity maps? Check DBKL e-submission portal (eSS) or OSC portal for parcel-level queries
- Is there a GIS layer available publicly?

notes.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 1 Part 3 — Classes of Use of Land and Buildings (CULB2025)

frontmatter.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 1 Part 3 — Classes of Use of Land and Buildings (CULB2025)

notes.content:
# KLDCP 2024 Vol 1 Part 3 — Classes of Use (CULB2025)

## Summary

This document defines all 68 permissible classes of use for land and buildings in Kuala Lumpur, replacing earlier use class orders. It is the definitive legal reference for what activity is permitted on a given piece of land, making it critically important for investors buying commercial-titled SOHOs, serviced apartments, or mixed-use properties. It classifies use into 10 main categories (A–J), and sets out permissibility (P = permitted, PC = permitted with conditions, NP = not permitted) for each use in each zone.

## Key Data Points

### Classification of Investment-Relevant Property Types

| Property Type | Use Class | Category | Classification |
|--------------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Condominium / Apartment | B1 | Residential | NOT commercial |
| Serviced Apartment | A10 | **Commercial** | Not residential |
| SOHO (Small Office Home Office) | A11 | **Commercial** | Not residential |
| Shop lot / retail | A1 | Commercial | Commercial |
| Office | A3 | Commercial | Commercial |
| Factory / warehouse | C1–C14 | Industrial | Industrial |
| Hotel | A9 | Commercial | Commercial |

**Critical investor implication:** Serviced Apartments (A10) and SOHOs (A11) are **commercially classified** — they attract commercial utility tariffs, commercial assessment rates, and different strata rules than residential (B1) units.

### SOHO Definition (A11)

> "Office or service space that allows the provision of limited accommodation space in the individual premise. Service and business activities that do not involve frequent customer visits, fewer manpower, and the storage of goods are not allowed."

- Classified as **Commercial (A)**, not Residential (B)
- Permissible office activities: professional services (legal, accounting, town planning, online business) and support business services (spa, specialist health clinic, secretariat, computer, IT services)
- Residential occupation is incidental to office use, not the primary purpose
- **No SOVO or SOFO defined as separate classes** in CULB2025 — these are market terms; they fall under SOHO (A11) or TP zone variations

### Serviced Apartment Definition (A10)

> "Accommodation provided in commercial land use zones, usually managed by a third party, consisting of businesses or hotel facilities not available in residential apartments. It does not include hotels, lodges, worker and student dormitories."

- Classified as **Commercial (A)**
- **Not permissible within residential developments**
- In Technology Park (TP) zones: limited to **10% of total GFA**

### Permissibility Summary for Key Zones

| Zone | B1 Residential | A10 Serv.Apt | A11 SOHO | A9 Hotel |
|------|----------------|-------------|---------|---------|
| CCC (City Centre Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | PC |
| MC (Major Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | PC |
| C (Commercial) | PC | PC | PC | PC |
| MX (Mixed Development) | PC | PC | PC | PC |
| R1 (Landed Residential) | P | NP | NP | NP |
| R3 (Medium-High Residential) | P | PC | PC | NP |
| R4 (Affordable Housing) | P | PC | PC | NP |
| IP (Industrial Park) | NP | NP | NP | NP |
| MXI (Mixed Industrial) | NP | NP | NP | NP |
| TP (Technology Park) | PC (≤10% GFA) | PC (≤10% GFA) | PC (≤10% GFA) | PC |

### Shopping Mall Rule
> "Conversion of a row of commercial terrace into a shopping mall is NOT permissible" — shop lots cannot be amalgamated into mall format without proper planning

### 68 Use Classes (10 Main Categories)
- Category A (Commercial): 20 classes including A1 General Retail, A2 Business, A3 Office, A5 Food & Drink, A9 Tourist Accommodation, A10 Serviced Apartment, A11 SOHO, A14 Shopping Mall
- Category B (Residential): 6 classes including B1 Residential Unit (all types), B3 Labour Quarters, B5 Residential Care Home, B6 Home-based Business
- Category C (Industrial): 14 classes
- Categories D–J: Institution, Community Facility, Cemetery, Open Space, Infrastructure, Transport, Forest Reserve

## Notable Quotes

> On SOHO: "Office or service space that allows the provision of limited accommodation space in the individual premise."
> On Serviced Apartment: "Accommodation provided in commercial land use zones... It does not include hotels, lodges, worker and student dormitories."
> On MXI zones: "Residential characterised commercial activities especially service apartments, SOHO and hotels are not permissible."

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]]
- [[concepts/strata-title]]

## Open Questions

- What specific conditions apply for A10/A11 in CCC zones? (referenced to supplementary DBKL guidelines)
- Is there a permitted change-of-use table showing allowed conversions between classes?

notes.title: KLDCP 2024 Volume 2 — Promoting City Development
notes.content:
# KLDCP 2024 Vol 2 — Promoting City Development

## Summary

The largest of the KLDCP documents (576 pages), Vol 2 translates the KLSP2040 strategy into area-specific development frameworks. It defines a 4-tier urban growth centre hierarchy, maps 3 control zones, and provides specific development guidelines for named growth centres — from the KLCC/Bukit Bintang primary centre down to local neighbourhood centres. Critical for understanding which areas DBKL is actively promoting for development, where redevelopment pressures are targeted, and what the development intent is for specific submarkets.

## Key Data Points

### 3 Control Zones (Development Pressure)

| Zone | Description | Intensity |
|------|-------------|----------|
| Control Zone 1 | Main Centre (City Centre) | Highest; TOD-oriented |
| Control Zone 2 | Secondary Centre (along MRT3 corridor) | 26 transit stations; 73 redevelopment areas; affordable housing emphasis |
| Control Zone 3 | Support Centre (outside MRT3 network) | Lower intensity; neighbourhood focus |

### 4-Tier Growth Centre Hierarchy

**Primary (Main) Growth Centres — 3:**
1. **KLCC and Bukit Bintang** — Jalan Bukit Bintang to become pedestrian/micromobility street; Jalan Ampang as eco-district
2. **KL Sentral** — integrate with National Museum and Bukit Persekutuan
3. **Kampong Bharu** — Malay identity; integrate with MRT3, Dang Wangi LRT, Chow Kit Monorail; KLCC skyline views protected

**Secondary Growth Centres — 10+:**
Bangsar, Bangsar South (+Brickfields), Batu Kentonmen, Jalan Ampang & Datuk Keramat, KL Metropolis, Maluri, TRX and Pudu, Warisan Merdeka and Plaza Rakyat, Pusat Bandar Damansara, Sentul, Sri Hartamas/Mont Kiara

**District Growth Centres:** Bukit Jalil, Kepong, Wangsa Maju, Cheras (Taman Segar), Pavilion Bukit Jalil, Penchala/Mutiara Damansara, Taman Melati/KL East

**Local Growth Centres:** Bandar Menjalara, Bandar Sri Permaisuri, Bandar Tasik Selatan, Danau Kota, Klang Lama, Selayang, Setiawangsa, Jinjang

### 7 Primary Commercial Centres (Explicitly Mapped)
1. KLCC/Bukit Bintang
2. KL Sentral surrounds
3. Bangsar South and Media City
4. Damansara City Centre
5. KL Metropolis
6. Bandar Malaysia
7. Bukit Jalil City Centre

### Specific Area Notes

**Kampong Bharu (123.2 ha):**
- Almost 90% small lots — large-scale development very difficult without lot amalgamation
- Malay Agricultural Settlement heritage; special planning authority (Act 733)
- KLCC views and skyline to be preserved
- Height controls to respect surrounding character

**Bandar Malaysia:**
- Former RMAF Sungai Besi; massive mixed-use redevelopment
- Future KL–JB HSR terminal
- Sponge city / nature-friendly development guidelines
- Connects to Razak City via bridge; River of Life via Kwong Tong cemetery

**TRX and Pudu:**
- TRX Development Code applies (separate design control document)
- MRT2 station connection is completed; Category 1 TPZ incentive applies

**Wangsa Maju:**
- Designated **Carbon Neutral Growth Centre** (first of its kind in KL)
- Focus on commercial development supporting carbon neutrality

**Mont Kiara / Sri Hartamas:**
- Large-unit residential enclave maintained (PPA-based control applies)
- Future MRT3 stations at both Mont Kiara and Sri Hartamas

**Sentul:**
- Focus on high-value employment; MRT2 and KTM Sentul stations
- Affordable housing emphasis in redevelopment

**Bangsar South:**
- Integrate Telekom Malaysia Tower and Angkasapuri via green corridor
- Connected to Universiti and Kerinchi LRT; Taman Sri Angkasa Flats targeted for redevelopment

### TOD Framework (5 TOD Types)

| TOD Type | Character | Intensity |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| TOD 1 | City Centre CBD | High; main office, high-intensity housing, main business |
| TOD 2 | Main Growth Centre | Medium-high; business hotspot, high/medium-high housing, office |
| TOD 3 | Neighbourhood | Medium; university, limited mixed, residential |
| TOD 4 | Institution & Community | Medium; sports, cultural, civic, employment |
| TOD 5 | Suburban | Medium; vacant land, limited redevelopment, industrial |

### Financial Activity Zones (3 Designated)
1. Jalan Ampang – Jalan Tun Razak (banks, business)
2. Jalan Sultan Ismail – Jalan Raja Chulan (existing banking centre)
3. Jalan Tun Razak – TRX (new financial hub with fintech; MRT2 connected)

## Notable Quotes

> On Wangsa Maju: designated as "Carbon Neutral Growth Centre" — first explicit carbon-neutral district designation in KL
> On Bandar Malaysia: "nature-friendly city; green/low-carbon development guidelines; sponge city concept"
> On Kampong Bharu: "Almost 90% of lots are small lots" — explains why the area is still largely undeveloped despite its prime location

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[concepts/kl-development-control]]
- [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]]
- [[overview]]

## Open Questions

- Has the Bandar Malaysia developer been confirmed? What is current construction status?
- MRT3 Circle Line — what is the current funding and timeline status (as of 2026)?
- What specific redevelopment areas are targeted in Control Zone 2 (73 listed)?
- Carbon Neutral Growth Centre at Wangsa Maju — are there specific building certification requirements?

notes.title: Kuala Lumpur Structure Plan 2040 (KLSP2040)
notes.content:
# KL Structure Plan 2040 (KLSP2040)

## Summary

The KLSP2040 is Kuala Lumpur's master planning document gazetted in October 2023, replacing KLSP2020. It sets out KL's vision as a "City For All" — shifting from the KLSP2020 "World Class City" aspiration to one focused on inclusivity, equity, sustainability, and climate resilience. It is legally binding as a reference for all planning decisions in KL up to 2040 and is administered by DBKL. The plan covers population, economic, housing, transportation, and environmental targets across 6 goals, 21 strategic directions, and 73 actions.

## Key Data Points

### Population
- **Base 2020:** 1.98 million
- **Target 2040:** 2.35 million (AAGR: 1.04%)
- Fastest-growing planning areas: Bukit Jalil–Seputeh, Sentul–Menjalara, Wangsa Maju–Maluri
- Elderly (65+) to reach 17.3% by 2040
- Non-citizens: projected at 14.3% of total

### Economy
- **KL GDP 2040 target:** RM 680 billion (up from RM 216B in 2020)
- **Per capita GDP 2040:** RM 289,400 (up from RM 122,500)
- Target: 100 new MNCs by 2030
- Tourism target 2040: 35 million tourists; RM 77 billion in spend

### Housing
- **Total housing units 2040 target:** 762,500 units
- **Affordable housing target (2040):** 40% = **305,000 units**
- Existing affordable housing 2020: 40,022 units (severely undersupplied; only 8% of total)
- Existing public housing (PA + PPR) 2024: 62,136 units
- **Benchmark prices 2020:** High-rise avg RM 489,600; Terrace avg RM 867,321
- **Affordability matrix:** B40 groups: RM 350,000–650,000; M40: RM 750,000–1,200,000

### Land Scarcity
- Total undeveloped land in KL: 2,121.14 ha
- Barrier-free developable land: only **554.50 ha**
- Land required for population growth to 2040: 1,149.65 ha
- **Conclusion: KL is land-scarce; compact high-density development is the mandated response**

### Infrastructure — Rail (Planned)
- MRT2 (Putrajaya Line): 28 km / 21 stations in KL
- MRT3 (Circle Line): **42 km / 28 stations in KL** — proposed; links city boundary areas
- KL–JB HSR: starts at Bandar Malaysia; 350 km; 90 min
- Modal split target 2040: **70% public transport : 30% private vehicle**

### Key Strategic Areas
- KLCC / Bukit Bintang — primary commercial + tourism centre
- TRX — financial hub
- KL Sentral — integrated transport hub
- Kampong Bharu — 123.2 ha Special Development Area; Islamic Malay heritage
- Bandar Malaysia — former RMAF Sungai Besi; future HSR terminal; mixed development
- Bangsar South / KL Eco City / Merdeka 118 / KL Metropolis

### Climate
- GHG intensity reduction: **85% by 2040** (70% by 2030)
- Carbon neutral target: **2050**
- Tree canopy: 50% of KL by 2040
- 1 million trees to be planted

## Notable Quotes

> "Kuala Lumpur CITY FOR ALL — oriented towards the aspirations of city dwellers who wish Kuala Lumpur to be a prosperous, healthy, safe and developed city offering quality, affordable, innovative living and a sustainable, vibrant, low carbon, clean and green environment."

> On land scarcity: "There is insufficient developable land" — compact/high-density development is the mandated response to a gap of ~595 ha between available land and required land for growth.

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/klang-valley]]
- [[overview]]
- [[concepts/kl-development-control]]
- [[concepts/kl-classes-of-use]]
- [[concepts/tpz-tiz-transit-incentives]]

## Open Questions

- What is the current status of MRT3 (Circle Line) — funding approved? Timeline?
- Has Bandar Malaysia development commenced in earnest? Which developer?
- What are the updated affordable housing price caps under current government policy?
- KL–JB HSR: current status after multiple suspensions and revivals?

notes.title: Avaland unit to purchase Tmn U-Thant land for RM86mil

frontmatter.title: Avaland unit to purchase Tmn U-Thant land for RM86mil

notes.content:
## Summary

Avaland Bhd, a boutique developer listed on Bursa Malaysia, has agreed to acquire three adjoining freehold land parcels in Taman U-Thant, Kuala Lumpur for RM86.04 million (broadly in line with independent valuation of RM88 million). The site measures 7,612.63 sq m (~81,950 sq ft) and is zoned for residential use. The acquisition will be funded via internal funds and bank borrowings. Avaland intends to develop a high-rise residential project with an estimated GDV of RM700 million — implying a land cost to GDV ratio of ~12.3%, which is within market norms for prime KL freehold land. Completion of the acquisition is targeted for Q1 2027.

## Key Data Points

| Item | Detail |
|------|--------|
| Land area | 7,612.63 sq m (~81,952 sq ft) |
| Purchase price | RM86.04 million |
| Valuation | RM88 million (independent) |
| Price psf (land) | **RM1,050 psf** (RM86,040,000 ÷ 81,952 sq ft) |
| Target GDV | RM700 million |
| Land cost / GDV | ~12.3% |
| Location | Taman U-Thant, Kuala Lumpur |
| Tenure | Freehold |
| Seller | Tong Ah Company Sdn Bhd |
| Buyer subsidiary | Nexus Advertising Sdn Bhd (wholly-owned) |
| Acquisition target date | Q1 2027 |
| Funding | Internal funds + bank borrowings |

## Notable Quotes

> "The land, currently zoned for residential use, is intended for a high-rise residential development with an estimated gross development value of about RM700mil."

> "The site is located within an established embassy enclave, two kilometres from Kuala Lumpur city centre, with access to major roads including Jalan Ampang and Jalan Tun Razak."

> "The group said the acquisition supports its strategy to strengthen its presence in the luxury residential segment and is expected to contribute positively to future earnings."

## Updates To

- [[entities/areas/u-thant-embassy-row]] — Land acquisition price psf (~RM1,050 psf) updates land price benchmark for U-Thant freehold
- [[entities/developers/avaland]] — New developer page created (Avaland Bhd, listed)
- [[log]]

## Open Questions

- What is the plot ratio and GFA that Avaland is assuming for the RM700M GDV? This determines density and unit size potential.
- Has planning (development order) been obtained or is this subject to DA approval?
- Unit size and unit count targets — does RM700M GDV on 7,612 sqm imply a high-rise (500+ units) or mid-rise boutique?
- Has the Bumiputera quota been considered in the development concept?
- Who are Avaland's key bankers and what loan margin are they targeting?

notes.title: Raintree Forest Sg Long — Comparison of DCS 19B, CCE No 2 and CCE No 2A (Project 4236)

frontmatter.title: Raintree Forest Sg Long — Comparison of DCS 19B, CCE No 2 and CCE No 2A (Project 4236)
frontmatter.publisher: BRDB — Internal QS Cost Report (Author: Phon)

notes.content:
## Summary

Internal cost comparison report for the Raintree Forest Sungai Long landed housing development, comparing three cost estimate revisions: DCS 19B (base), CCE No 2, and CCE No 2A. Dated 26 November 2024. Covers all cost components — building superstructure by unit type, infrastructure, M&E, landscape, and provisions — with prelims and contingency. Grand total ranges from MYR 316M (DCS base) to MYR 383M (CCE No 2), with CCE No 2A at MYR 370M (+17% vs base). Building superstructure accounts for ~78% of the sub-total; infrastructure (within + outside site) accounts for ~13%.

## Key Data Points

**Grand Totals**

| Revision | Sub-Total (MYR) | Prelims | Contingency | Grand Total (MYR) | vs DCS Base |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DCS 19B (base) | 293,012,545 | 6.9% | 1.0% | 316,092,561 | — |
| CCE No 2 | 348,035,000 | 7.0% | 3.0% | 382,840,000 | +21.12% |
| CCE No 2A | 336,302,000 | 7.0% | 3.0% | 369,933,000 | +17.03% |

**Building Costs by Unit Type (RM/sqft NFA)**

| Unit Type | DCS NFA (sqft) | DCS RM/sqft | CCE2 NFA | CCE2 RM/sqft | CCE2A NFA | CCE2A RM/sqft |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Semi D | 759,000 | 194.00 | 896,139 | 204.93 | 829,560 | 208.72 |
| Zero Lot | 94,500 | 196.00 | 114,278 | 193.68 | 107,828 | 199.14 |
| Villa | 66,000 | 202.00 | 68,408 | 196.85 | 64,600 | 199.61 |
| RSKU (affordable) | 216,150 | 199.01 | 216,150 | 224.45 | 216,150 | 224.45 |
| Clubhouse | 10,000 | 300.00 | 38,569 | 203.48 | 38,569 | 203.48 |

- All rates on NFA (Net Floor Area) basis — for landed houses, NFA ≈ GFA (no shared floor area)
- RSKU rate jump from DCS (MYR 199) to CCE2/2A (MYR 224) = +12.8% — scope upgrade or revised spec
- Clubhouse NFA expanded from 10,000 sqft (DCS) to 38,569 sqft (CCE2/2A) — scope change, rate drop from MYR 300 to MYR 203/sqft

**Infrastructure Costs (within site boundary) — CCE2A: MYR 41,374,000**

| Item | DCS (MYR) | CCE2A (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| Site Clearance & Earthworks | 1,340,265 | 4,382,000 |
| Retaining Walls (within unit lots) | 2,474,800 | 2,270,000 |
| Road Works | 6,195,194 | 10,021,000 |
| Surface Water Drainage | 9,227,840 | 11,670,000 |
| Foul Drainage | 4,248,400 | 4,399,000 |
| Sewerage Treatment Plant | 1,450,000 | 2,180,000 |
| Water Reticulation | 4,794,700 | 3,450,000 |
| Water Reservoir | 2,036,365 | 2,070,000 |
| Fencing & Gate | 1,880,055 | 932,000 |

- DCS earthworks at only MYR 1.34M vs CCE2A MYR 4.38M (+227%) — DCS note says "soil stolen"; CCE2A adds MYR 1.74M for soil replacement/strengthening due to erosion
- DCS water reticulation higher than CCE2A (MYR 4.79M vs MYR 3.45M) — scope rationalisation

**Infrastructure Outside Site Boundary — CCE2A: MYR 4,113,000**

| Item | DCS (MYR) | CCE2A (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| Road Works | 1,076,468 | 1,967,000 |
| Surface Water Drainage | 2,736,870 | 1,058,000 |
| Water Reticulation | 660,900 | 1,088,000 |

**Other Major Items**

| Item | DCS (MYR) | CCE2A (MYR) |
|---|---|---|
| M&E Infra | 9,727,000 | 8,593,000 |
| Landscape | 11,524,000 | 7,062,000 |
| Detention Pond | 2,500,000 | 2,500,000 |
| Compact Sub-stations (6 Ph1 + 4 Ph2) | 1,000,000 | 1,000,000 |
| Electric Perimeter Fencing | 369,425 | 1,041,000 |
| CCTV (perimeter) | 831,050 | 1,041,000 |
| Structural strengthening — Club House | 1,000,000 | 1,000,000 |

## Notable Quotes

- DCS item 6a remark: "soil stolen" — earthworks cost drastically understated in DCS due to unauthorised soil removal from site; CCE adds provision for soil replacement
- Report described as "Based on CCE 2A" in the later Infra Cost comparison (Oct 2025)

## Updates To

- [[construction-costs/projects/raintree-forest-sg-long]] — added building superstructure cost section (DCS vs CCE2 vs CCE2A)
- [[construction-costs/rates/landed-house-building-cost]] — new rate page created from this source

## Open Questions

- What drove the RSKU cost increase from DCS MYR 199/sqft to CCE2/2A MYR 224/sqft (+12.8%)? Spec change or revised unit layouts?
- Was the clubhouse scope expansion (10,000 → 38,569 sqft NFA) driven by a design change or correction of DCS undercount?
- Status of the "soil stolen" issue — was it fully rectified by the time of CCE2A?
- Which phase does each unit type (Semi D, Zero Lot, Villa) correspond to in the phasing plan?

frontmatter.title: The Edge CEO Morning Brief — Issue 1144/2026

notes.content:
# The Edge CEO Morning Brief — Issue 1144/2026

*Wednesday, April 29, 2026 | theedgemalaysia.com*

---

## Summary

This issue leads with Malaysia's Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah warning of rising construction and food costs, with building materials up 12.6% on average due to higher diesel, bitumen, and logistics prices. Major property-related stories include SD Guthrie and MBI Selangor exploring a 2,500-acre Sepang mixed-use industrial park near KLIA Aeropolis; JLL Malaysia reporting data centre capacity set to more than double to 2,055MW by end-2026 (led by Johor Bahru) with industrial land at RM86 psf (+8.4% YoY in 2025); Knight Frank data showing KL luxury residential prices rose 1.1% in 2025 and Malaysia UHNWI population set to grow from 1,566 to 1,881 by 2031; and Avaland Bhd confirming the acquisition of freehold Taman U-Thant land (7,613 sqm) for RM86.04 million (~GDV RM700M). Tropicana Corp acquired 24.15 acres in Langkawi for RM195.9 million, while UEM Sunrise shares surged 12% on Johor landbank undervaluation and takeover speculation.

---

## Key Data Points

### Construction Costs
- Building material costs rose **average 12.6%** (as of April 2026 update from Economy Minister)
- Drivers: higher diesel, bitumen, logistics, and raw material input costs
- Materials make up **over 60%** of construction costs — creates significant flow-through to project budgets
- Labour market expected to face greater pressure in 2Q2026 due to delayed effects of global energy crisis
- Government committed to continue key infrastructure (roads, schools, clinics, basic infrastructure)
- Oil prices: Brent crude at **US$109.94/barrel** (week ending April 24, 2026); rose to US$112.92 on April 24, US$113.34 on April 27
- Perkeso unemployment benefit applications: 7,512 in Feb → 5,855 in March → 5,734 in early April

### SD Guthrie + MBI Selangor — Sepang Industrial Park
- **2,500 acres** mixed-use industrial development in **Sepang Estate, Selangor**
- Located adjacent to KLIA Aeropolis; within IDRISS (Integrated Development Region in South Selangor)
- Part of Sepang Infinity Corridor Hub under Sepang Local Plan 2035
- Expected to create **32,000+ job opportunities** by 2030
- Features: manufacturing, logistics, aerospace capabilities, business/training facilities
- Second collaboration between SD Guthrie and MBI Selangor (first: Carey Island)
- SDG chairman: Tan Sri Nik Norzrul Thani Nik Hassan Thani; MBI Selangor CEO: Datuk T's Saipolyazan M Yusop
- Selangor MB: Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari

### JLL Malaysia — Data Centre & Industrial
- Malaysia data centre capacity: **1,020MW current → 2,055MW by end-2026** (70% CAGR since 2020)
- **Johor Bahru**: 850MW completed, 1,800MW under construction, 2,700MW in pipeline — top 3 in Apac (alongside Tokyo and Beijing)
- JB data centre 5-year CAGR since 2020: **~5,262% growth** (cited by JLL)
- **Industrial land prices**: RM86 psf nationally in 2025, **up 8.4% YoY**
- Key industrial demand drivers: aerospace, data centre, data supply chain, cross-border logistics, life sciences, medical, oil & gas
- Grid connectivity delays: 24 months wait for some markets, up to 8 years for core markets
- Johor office spillover: occupiers exploring relocation from Singapore (rent differential); popular buildings include Mid Valley Southkey, JBCC Tower, Menara MBJB, Medini 9
- JLL notes flight-to-quality, flight-to-green trend; sustainability a key factor in asset enhancement

### KL Luxury Residential — Knight Frank Wealth Report 2026
- KL luxury residential appreciation: **+1.1% in 2025** (stable appreciation)
- Total residential transactions: 399,008 (2023) → **420,545 (2024)**, up 5.4%
- TEDUH platform (Transforming and Empowering Data Usage in Housing) supporting market sentiment
- Real Property Development Act reform proposed — supportive housing policy backdrop
- Hong Kong luxury fell 2.1% in 2025; Singapore continues to set records (>US$6,000 = RM23,782 psf)
- Malaysia DIA (direct investment abroad) rising 65% QoQ to **RM2.8 billion** in 4Q25
- UHNWI trend: ultra-mobility among super-rich reshaping buying patterns; demand for super prime rentals growing

### Malaysia UHNWI Outlook — Knight Frank
| Country | 2026 | 2031 |
|---------|------|------|
| Malaysia | 1,566 | 1,881 |
| Singapore | 7,171 | 10,495 |
| Indonesia | 3,833 | 6,966 |
| Thailand | 2,853 | 3,582 |
| Vietnam | 1,233 | 1,960 |
- Malaysia projected to add 5 more billionaires and 300+ UHNWI (>US$30M net worth) by 2031
- 89 new UHNWI added globally each day between 2021–2026
- KL positioned as safe haven amid geopolitical uncertainty

### Avaland — Taman U-Thant Land Acquisition
- **7,613 sqm (81,949 sqft) freehold land** in Taman U-Thant, Kuala Lumpur
- Acquisition price: **RM86.04 million** (RM1,050 psf of land)
- Vendor: Tong Ah Company Sdn Bhd; buyer: Nexus Advertising Sdn Bhd (wholly-owned Avaland unit)
- Currently zoned for residential use; earmarked for **high-rise residential**
- Preliminary GDV: **~RM700 million**
- Acquisition aligned with Avaland's strategy to expand in Klang Valley high-end segment

### Tropicana Corp — Langkawi Land Acquisition
- **15 parcels, 24.15 acres** in Langkawi, Kedah — total consideration **RM195.88 million**
- Transaction 1: 14 parcels in Bandar Padang Lalang, Langkawi from Maya Elemen Sdn Bhd — **RM151.1 million**; identified by LADA (Langkawi Development Authority) as key agro-tourism and commercial zone
- Transaction 2: Leasehold parcel in Padang Matsirat from Tanjung Mali Resort Development — **RM44.8 million**; near Langkawi International Airport
- Buyer entity: TSDSB (Tropicana Scenic Development Sdn Bhd, wholly-owned)
- Strengthens Tropicana's strategic presence in Langkawi; in line with expansion in high-growth areas

### UEM Sunrise — Johor Landbank Speculation
- Shares rose for **4th consecutive session** on April 28; reached intraday high of 67 sen, closed 66.5 sen
- Total 103.63M shares traded; stock up 8 sen (+12.71%) to market cap **RM3.37 billion**
- +32% rally since April 22; added >RM809 million in market value
- Malacca Securities analyst Loui Low: buying interest centred on undervalued Johor landbank; sees potential corporate takeover target
- Book value: **RM1.36 per share** (stock trading at ~49% discount to book as of close)
- UEMS holds substantial undeveloped Johor landbank

### KLCCP Stapled Group — Retail & Hotel
- Rising cost pressures flagged for retail and hotel segments amid Middle East tensions
- **Suria KLCC** annual turnover stabilised at RM2.7 billion (peak: slightly above RM3 billion)
- Tenant occupancy costs below 20% of revenue on average — manageable; supports renewals and revisions
- Single-digit rental growth; management extracting more profit despite single-digit revenue growth
- **FY2025 net profit**: RM1.28 billion (+25.95% YoY) from RM1.02 billion; revenue RM1.74 billion (up 1.69% from RM1.71bn)
- Record dividend: 47 sen per stapled security
- Hotel: Mandarin Oriental KL optimising across rooms, MICE, lifestyle; newly renovated Grand Ballroom
- Focus remaining on domestic opportunities; "no plan to go overseas yet"

### BNM SME Relief Facility
- **RM5 billion** facility for SMEs affected by global energy crisis
- Named: SME Stabilisation Relief Facility
- Maximum financing: **RM750,000 per SME**, up to 5-year tenure, max rate 3.75% p.a. (inclusive of CGC guarantee fees)
- CGC guarantees up to 80%; for SMEs without sufficient collateral
- Applications open **May 15 – Dec 31, 2026** (or until full utilisation)
- Context: meeting between PM Anwar and financial institution CEOs re: working capital support amid West Asia conflict

### World Bank Commodity Prices
- Global commodity price index climbing **~16% in 2026** — highest since 2022 (Russia-Ukraine)
- Brent crude averaging **US$86/barrel** in 2026 (baseline; "most acute" disruptions end May)
- Energy and fertiliser prices at multi-year highs since Iran war (started late February 2026)
- Fertiliser costs climbing **31% this year** — risk to farmer incomes
- World Bank chief economist: "war is hitting the global economy in cumulative waves"

---

## Notable Quotes

> "In construction, costs have risen by an average of 12.6% due to higher prices for diesel, bitumen, logistics, and other inputs. Since materials make up over 60% of construction costs, this is expected to affect ongoing infrastructure projects." — Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir

> "We are aiming to create long-term value through strategic land development and industrial clustering to support Selangor's economic transformation." — SD Guthrie chairman Tan Sri Nik Norzrul Thani

> "Comparing Malaysia to the region, we see Johor Bahru at a 5,262% growth. That is the highest in Apac. In the past five years, we have grown by about 70% CAGR since 2020." — JLL Malaysia capital markets senior director Keith Eng

> "With such growth, we see an increase in the waiting time for the connectivity of connecting to the grid, [ranging from] about 24 months for some emerging markets and up to eight years in certain core markets." — JLL's Keith Eng on data centre grid constraints

> "At the moment, there is no plan for us to go overseas yet." — KLCCP CEO Datuk Mohd Salem Kailany

---

## Updates To

- [[entities/developers/avaland]] — confirmed Taman U-Thant acquisition details (price, land area, GDV, vendor)
- [[entities/developers/uem-sunrise]] — Johor landbank speculation, +32% share rally, book value RM1.36
- [[entities/developers/sd-guthrie]] — new page created; Sepang 2,500-acre industrial park
- [[entities/developers/tropicana]] — new page created; Langkawi 24.15-acre acquisition RM195.9M
- [[entities/areas/johor]] — JLL data centre stats; industrial land RM86 psf (+8.4% YoY); office relocation spillover from Singapore
- [[entities/areas/u-thant-embassy-row]] — Avaland acquisition confirmed; RM1,050 psf land price benchmark
- [[entities/areas/klcc-bukit-bintang]] — KLCCP financials; Suria KLCC RM2.7bn turnover; record FY2025 profit
- [[entities/areas/sepang]] — new page created; IDRISS industrial park, 2,500 acres, KLIA Aeropolis
- [[entities/areas/langkawi]] — new page created; Tropicana acquisition, Padang Matsirat, LADA zone
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-construction-cost-inflation-2026]] — new analysis page
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-johor-data-centre-industrial-land-2026]] — new analysis page
- [[market-analysis/2026-04-29-kl-luxury-residential-uhnwi-2025-2026]] — new analysis page

---

## Open Questions

- When will Avaland launch the Taman U-Thant project? GDV ~RM700M suggests a significant flagship project — pricing, units, design concept not yet known
- What is the exact Sepang industrial park tenure and land cost PSF? MBI Selangor is likely the landowner; terms of the partnership (JV? Land sale? Lease?) not disclosed
- Who are the anchor tenants for the Sepang Infinity Corridor Hub? Aerospace mentioned but no names
- UEM Sunrise takeover — who are the potential acquirers? Khazanah (parent) or external party? Timeline?
- Grid delay risk for Johor data centres — up to 8-year wait in "core markets": does this cap Johor's data centre growth ceiling?
- Tropicana Langkawi — what is the intended development? Agro-tourism + commercial suggests mixed-use resort or commercial park; no GDV disclosed
- KLCCP Suria KLCC annual turnover peaked above RM3B — why has it moderated to RM2.7B? Mall reconfiguration, F&B shift, or structural change?
